DESIGN DOC: Multi-Region Read Replica Failover for OrderDB
Author: Priya Ramaswamy, Storage Platform Team
Reviewers: Dilnoza Karimova (SRE), Marcus Feld (Checkout), Wei-Ling Tsang (Data Platform)
Status: Draft for review
Date: 2026-05-14

1. Background

OrderDB is the primary transactional store for order records across the
consumer checkout stack. It runs as a single-primary, multi-replica Postgres
cluster: the primary lives in us-east-1, with synchronous replicas in
us-east-1b and us-east-1c, and asynchronous read replicas in us-west-2 and
eu-west-1 that serve read-only traffic for the order-history API and the
fraud-review dashboard. The cluster currently handles roughly 42,000 writes
per minute at peak (Friday 6-9pm local across time zones) and 310,000 reads
per minute across all replicas combined.

Over the past two quarters we have had three primary-region incidents
(2026-01-09, 2026-03-02, 2026-04-21) where us-east-1 experienced degraded
networking that made the primary unreachable from parts of our edge fleet
for between 6 and 40 minutes. In each case, manual failover to a same-region
synchronous replica was performed by an on-call engineer, but the process
took an average of 27 minutes from first page to confirmed recovery, well
outside our internal 5-minute target for tier-0 services. This document
proposes an automated, cross-region-aware failover controller to replace
the manual runbook.

2. Problem Statement

The current failover path has three structural problems. First, it is
entirely manual: an engineer must confirm primary unreachability, promote a
replica, and repoint the connection string in Consul, and every one of
those steps is a chance for error under pressure. Second, it only ever
considers same-region replicas, so a region-wide event (like 2026-04-21,
which was a us-east-1 NAT gateway saturation issue) leaves us with no
automated path to a healthy replica in us-west-2 or eu-west-1. Third,
there is no automated verification step, so on two of the three incidents
the promoted replica was itself still catching up on WAL replay and served
stale reads for 90+ seconds after being marked primary.

3. Goals

- Reduce mean time to recovery (MTTR) for primary-unreachable events to
  under 5 minutes, matching the tier-0 SLA.
- Support promotion of either a same-region synchronous replica or a
  cross-region asynchronous replica, chosen by a documented priority order.
- Guarantee that a promoted replica has replayed all WAL segments it had
  received before promotion is declared complete, and that replication lag
  is surfaced during the decision.
- Provide a dry-run mode that exercises the full decision and promotion
  path against a staging cluster without touching production traffic.

4. Non-Goals

- This design does not address write availability during a full us-east-1
  outage; promoting a cross-region replica to primary is a read-availability
  and reduced-write-availability measure, not a multi-primary architecture.
  Multi-primary is tracked separately under STOR-441 and is out of scope.
- We are not changing the replication protocol itself (still Postgres
  streaming replication with WAL shipping); this is a control-plane change
  only.

5. Current Architecture

The primary accepts all writes and streams WAL to two synchronous replicas
in us-east-1 (quorum commit requires both to acknowledge) and two
asynchronous replicas, one in us-west-2 (typical lag 180-400ms) and one in
eu-west-1 (typical lag 900ms-1.4s due to cross-Atlantic link latency).
Client routing goes through a Consul-registered DNS name, order-db-primary,
resolved by application-side connection poolers with a 15-second TTL.
There is no health-check-driven automatic re-resolution today; the TTL
expiring is necessary but not sufficient, because Consul only updates the
record when a human runs the promotion runbook.

6. Proposed Design

We will introduce a failover controller, orderdb-failctl, deployed as a
three-node Raft group (one node per region: us-east-1, us-west-2,
eu-west-1) so that the controller itself survives a single-region outage.
Each node runs a health-check loop against the primary every 5 seconds,
using both a TCP connect check and a lightweight SELECT 1 query with a
2-second timeout. A primary is considered unreachable when 2 of 3 controller
nodes report failed checks for three consecutive intervals (15 seconds
total), which bounds our false-positive rate from transient blips while
keeping detection fast.

On unreachability, the controller evaluates candidate replicas in priority
order: same-region synchronous replicas first (since they have zero
expected data loss under quorum commit), then the cross-region replica
with the lowest measured WAL lag at decision time. It queries each
candidate's pg_stat_replication view and pg_last_wal_replay_lsn to confirm
lag, and will not promote a replica whose lag exceeds 30 seconds without
an explicit override flag, since that would silently exceed our RPO budget.

Once a candidate is selected, the controller runs pg_promote() on it,
polls until the replica reports itself as primary (no longer in recovery
mode), and only then updates the Consul service record. This ordering
closes the "stale reads served during promotion" gap from incident #2.
Old-primary fencing is handled via a STONITH-style network ACL push that
blocks the old primary from accepting writes, preventing split-brain if
it later recovers.

7. Failover Sequence (target timeline)

T+0s: primary becomes unreachable.
T+15s: quorum of controller nodes confirms unreachability.
T+15-20s: candidate selection and lag check.
T+20-35s: promotion executed on chosen replica.
T+35-40s: Consul record updated; fencing ACL applied to old primary.
T+40-45s: connection poolers pick up new DNS record via TTL expiry.

This targets a 45-second RTO, comfortably under our 5-minute SLA, with an
RPO of at most 2 seconds for same-region promotions and up to 30 seconds
for cross-region promotions (bounded by the lag-check gate).

8. Rollout Plan

Phase 1 (weeks 1-2): deploy orderdb-failctl in shadow mode against
staging only, validate detection and promotion logic with injected
network-partition tests (using tc netem to simulate the 2026-04-21
conditions).
Phase 2 (weeks 3-4): deploy to production in observe-only mode — the
controller detects and logs what it would do but does not execute
promotion. We will compare its decisions against the three historical
incidents replayed from logs.
Phase 3 (week 5): enable same-region automated promotion only, cross-region
promotion still requires a human "arm" step via a feature flag.
Phase 4 (week 7, pending Phase 3 results): enable full cross-region
automated promotion.

9. Risks and Mitigations

The largest risk is a false-positive failover during a transient network
blip that would have self-resolved in under 15 seconds anyway, causing an
unnecessary promotion and a brief write-availability gap. We mitigate this
with the 3-consecutive-interval requirement and by keeping the old primary
fenced rather than terminated, so a false promotion can be manually reversed
within a couple of minutes if caught. A second risk is controller
split-brain if the Raft group itself partitions; we mitigate this by
requiring a strict 2-of-3 quorum for any promotion decision, so a
minority partition can only observe, never act.

10. Alternatives Considered

We considered adopting a managed multi-region database service (Aurora
Global Database) instead of building this controller. This was rejected
for this cycle because migrating OrderDB's 3.8 TB dataset with acceptable
downtime was estimated at 6-8 weeks of dedicated migration work, versus
7 weeks to build and roll out the controller, and the managed option would
still require us to build equivalent client-side routing logic. We may
revisit this in H2 2026 once the controller has proven the operational
model.

11. Open Questions

Should the controller also handle planned maintenance failovers (e.g.,
before a primary-node OS patch), or should that remain a separate manual
path using the existing runbook? Marcus has requested this be folded in
during Phase 4 to reduce runbook count; Priya's preference is to keep
planned maintenance manual through this quarter and revisit after the
automated path has a full quarter of production incident history.


How many consecutive failed health-check intervals does orderdb-failctl require before it declares the primary unreachable, and what is the maximum WAL replication lag it will tolerate for an automatic cross-region promotion without an override flag?

Q3 INFRASTRUCTURE PLANNING SYNC — MEETING MINUTES
Date: 2026-06-09, 10:00-11:15 AM PT
Location: Conference Room 4B / Zoom hybrid
Attendees: Helena Brandt (VP Infra, chair), Oscar Nwachukwu (SRE lead),
Jasmine Okafor (Storage), Ben Rosen (Networking), Ravi Chandrasekaran
(Cost & Capacity), Lucia Ferretti (Security), notes by Tom Deakins
Absent: Sanjay Bhatt (out sick, async comments incorporated below)

1. Review of Q2 capacity actuals (Ravi)

Ravi presented the Q2 close-out numbers. GPU fleet utilization averaged
71% across the quarter, up from 63% in Q1, driven mostly by the new batch
inference workloads onboarded in April. Total GPU-hours consumed were
412,000, against a Q2 budget of 450,000, so we came in about 8.4% under
budget. Storage grew by 340 TB net across the quarter, roughly in line
with the 300-360 TB forecast range. Ravi flagged that the eu-west-1
region specifically is now at 89% of its allocated GPU quota and will need
a capacity request submitted by July 15 if the Q3 forecast holds, since
lead time on additional A100 nodes in that region is currently running
5-6 weeks.

Action: Ravi to file the eu-west-1 capacity request by 2026-06-20 (buffer
for approval delays), owner Ravi, due 2026-06-20.

2. Multi-region failover controller update (Oscar, on behalf of Priya)

Oscar gave a status update on the OrderDB failover controller design
(Priya's doc, currently in review). Phase 1 shadow-mode testing in staging
is scheduled to start the week of June 15. Oscar noted a dependency: the
controller needs a dedicated Raft quorum across three regions, which means
provisioning three small (2 vCPU) control-plane instances that aren't yet
in the Q3 capacity plan. Ravi confirmed this is a rounding error
capacity-wise and doesn't need a separate request.

Discussion: Lucia raised a security question about the STONITH-style
fencing ACL described in the design — specifically whether the ACL push
mechanism itself could become a denial-of-service vector if compromised.
Oscar agreed to loop Lucia in as a formal reviewer on the design doc before
Phase 3 (automated same-region promotion) is enabled. No decision made in
this meeting; tracked as a follow-up.

Action: Oscar to add Lucia as reviewer on the OrderDB failover design doc,
due 2026-06-12.

3. Network backbone upgrade — Ashburn to Portland link (Ben)

Ben reported that the planned upgrade of the Ashburn-to-Portland backbone
link from 100 Gbps to 400 Gbps is on track for a September 2026 cutover.
The vendor (Lightpath Networks) confirmed hardware delivery for August 3.
Ben flagged a risk: the cutover window they've been offered is a single
4-hour maintenance window on a Saturday, and our current cross-region
replication traffic (roughly 38 Gbps sustained between those two regions
during peak) would need to fail over to the backup path via Denver during
that window, which has historically added 40-60ms of extra latency to
cross-region reads. Helena asked whether this could be split into two
smaller windows; Ben will ask the vendor and report back.

Action: Ben to request a split-window option from Lightpath Networks, due
2026-06-16.

4. Security: Q3 access review cadence (Lucia)

Lucia proposed moving the quarterly access review from a single big-bang
review (currently takes the security team about 3 weeks to complete
manually) to a rolling review where 1/12 of all service accounts and IAM
roles are reviewed each week. She estimates this would reduce peak security
team load by roughly 60% while keeping the same total review cadence.
Helena approved the pilot for Q3, to be evaluated at the Q3 close-out
meeting in late September.

Action: Lucia to draft the rolling-review schedule and circulate by
2026-06-23.

5. Cost optimization: idle GPU reclamation (Ravi, with Sanjay's async notes)

Sanjay's written comments (read aloud by Ravi) flagged that the
idle-GPU-reclamation job, which downscales dev/staging GPU pools after 2
hours of inactivity, has a bug where it doesn't account for jobs that are
paused (not terminated) by the training team's checkpoint-and-resume
workflow — this caused three false reclamations in the last two weeks that
lost in-progress work for the model eval team. Jasmine noted this
overlaps with a storage question: paused-job state is currently kept only
in GPU memory, not checkpointed to durable storage automatically, which is
the root cause. Group agreed this needs a fix before reclamation logic
gets any more aggressive. Priority: high, but not tier-0 since it's
dev/staging only.

Action: Jasmine to file a ticket for automatic checkpoint-to-storage on
pause, and to loop in the model eval team for requirements, due 2026-06-13.

6. Model serving cost review (Ravi, continued)

Ravi returned to the floor with a follow-up item that had been deferred
from the previous sync: the per-request cost of the batch inference
workloads mentioned in item 1. He broke down the 412,000 GPU-hours by
workload class: 61% (251,000 hours) went to the recommendation-ranking
service, 22% (90,600 hours) to the new batch inference workloads onboarded
in April, and the remaining 17% (70,400 hours) split across smaller
internal tooling and the model eval team's experimentation cluster. Helena
asked whether the recommendation-ranking share was expected to grow or
shrink in Q3; Ravi said the ranking team's own roadmap projects roughly
flat GPU-hour usage through Q3 because their current optimization work is
targeted at latency, not throughput, so headcount-driven growth in query
volume (estimated +12% by September) would likely be absorbed by
efficiency gains rather than translating directly into proportional
GPU-hour growth. Jasmine asked whether storage costs track a similar
pattern; Ravi said storage growth is essentially decoupled from GPU-hour
growth at this point since most of the 340 TB added in Q2 was checkpoint
and dataset storage rather than serving-time artifacts.

Helena asked Ravi to bring a similar per-workload breakdown to the July 28
budget review with finance, since this level of detail was well received
in today's discussion and finance has specifically asked for workload-
level cost attribution in the last two budget cycles.

Action: Ravi to prepare workload-level GPU-hour and cost breakdown for the
July 28 finance review, due 2026-07-21 (same deadline as other H2 forecast
inputs).

7. Open floor / other items

Helena mentioned that the Q3 infra budget review with finance is now
confirmed for July 28, and asked everyone to have their H2 forecast inputs
ready by July 21 (one week buffer). Tom noted this should go on the shared
calendar; Helena will send the invite.

No other items raised. Meeting adjourned 11:12 AM.

Next sync: 2026-06-23, same time/location.

Summary of all action items with owners and due dates:
- Ravi: file eu-west-1 GPU capacity request, due 2026-06-20.
- Oscar: add Lucia as reviewer on OrderDB failover design doc, due
  2026-06-12.
- Ben: request split-window cutover option from Lightpath Networks, due
  2026-06-16.
- Lucia: draft rolling access-review schedule, due 2026-06-23.
- Jasmine: file ticket for checkpoint-to-storage on pause + loop in model
  eval team, due 2026-06-13.
- Ravi: prepare workload-level GPU-hour and cost breakdown for the July 28
  finance review, due 2026-07-21.
- Helena: send calendar invite for July 28 budget review.


List every action item from this meeting along with its owner and due date, and identify which two action items are most directly related to each other (i.e., one was created partly in response to the other being discussed).

PRODUCT REQUIREMENTS DOCUMENT: Canto Voice Practice Mode
Owner: Ines Wu, Product Manager
Engineering lead: Devraj Malhotra
Design lead: Sofia Andrade
Status: Approved for Q3 build
Last updated: 2026-05-28

1. Problem Statement

User research conducted in March and April 2026 (42 moderated interviews,
1,150-respondent survey) found that 68% of active learners on the Canto
app cite "I don't know if my pronunciation is actually correct" as a top-
three reason for losing motivation, ahead of vocabulary difficulty (51%)
and lesson pacing (39%). Cantonese's six-to-nine tone system (depending on
analysis convention) is consistently the single most-cited pain point —
34% of survey respondents specifically named tones as their biggest
pronunciation obstacle. Today, the app has no pronunciation feedback
mechanism at all; users read pinyin-style jyutping romanization and listen
to a native-speaker reference clip, with no way to check their own
attempt.

2. Goals

- Ship a Voice Practice Mode that lets users record themselves speaking a
  target phrase and receive feedback within 3 seconds of finishing the
  recording.
- Achieve tone-level feedback accuracy of at least 85% agreement with
  human rater judgments on our internal 500-clip validation set (drawn
  from 25 speakers across Hong Kong, Guangzhou, and diaspora accents).
- Move the "pronunciation confidence" self-reported metric (asked via
  in-app micro-survey after every 10th lesson) from a baseline of 2.9/5
  to at least 3.6/5 within 8 weeks of launch, measured against a holdout
  group that doesn't get the feature.
- Keep the feature's added infrastructure cost under $0.004 per practice
  attempt at projected Q4 volume (an estimated 2.1 million attempts/month
  once fully rolled out).

3. Non-Goals

- Full conversational speaking practice (open-ended dialogue) is out of
  scope for this release; this is single-phrase, single-attempt practice
  only. Conversational mode is tentatively planned for Q1 2027.
- We are not building support for Cantonese dialects outside standard
  Hong Kong Cantonese in this release (e.g., Taishanese influence,
  Guangzhou-specific tone mergers are treated as acceptable variation
  within the scoring tolerance, not as separate target dialects).
- Real-time (word-by-word, as-you-speak) feedback is out of scope; feedback
  is post-recording only for v1.

4. User Stories

As a beginner learner, I want to hear how close my pronunciation was to a
native speaker's, broken down by syllable, so that I know specifically
which sounds to practice rather than getting a single pass/fail signal.

As an intermediate learner, I want the tone feedback to be strict enough
that it catches real tone-merger mistakes (e.g., confusing tone 2 and
tone 5) but lenient enough that it doesn't flag natural accent variation
as wrong, so that I trust the feedback rather than ignoring it.

As a returning user who churned previously, I want practice attempts to
feel low-stakes (no visible "streak break" penalty for a bad attempt), so
that I'm not afraid to try and fail.

5. Functional Requirements

5.1 Recording flow: user taps a microphone icon next to any target phrase
in a lesson, records up to 8 seconds of audio, and can re-record before
submitting. Recording uses the device's native audio APIs at 16kHz
mono, matching the sample rate the scoring model was trained on.

5.2 Scoring pipeline: audio is sent to the pronunciation-scoring service,
which runs forced alignment against the expected phrase, then scores each
syllable on two axes — initial/final (consonant/vowel) accuracy and tone
accuracy — using a model fine-tuned on the 25-speaker validation corpus
plus an additional 40 hours of crowd-sourced labeled attempts collected in
April 2026. Scores are returned as a 0-100 value per syllable plus an
aggregate phrase score.

5.3 Feedback UI: syllables score above 80 are shown in green, 50-80 in
amber, below 50 in red, with a tap-to-hear-again affordance on any amber
or red syllable that plays the native reference clip isolated to that
syllable. Aggregate phrase score is shown as a 1-3 star rating rather than
a raw number, per design research showing raw percentage scores caused
disproportionate discouragement in the March interviews.

5.4 No visible streak penalty: practice attempts do not count against the
existing daily-streak mechanic regardless of score, addressing the
low-stakes user story above. This was a explicit ask from the design team
after two of the March interview participants described prior negative
experiences with strict scoring apps.

6. Non-Functional Requirements

- P95 latency from end-of-recording to feedback display must be under 3
  seconds on a mid-tier Android device (reference: Pixel 6a) on a 4G
  connection.
- The scoring service must handle the projected peak load of 45 requests/
  second (derived from 2.1M attempts/month distributed with a 6x
  peak-to-average ratio matching existing lesson-completion traffic
  patterns) with P99 latency under 5 seconds.
- Audio recordings are not retained after scoring unless the user
  explicitly opts in to a "help improve Canto" data-sharing setting;
  default is delete-after-scoring, consistent with the privacy policy
  update Legal approved on 2026-04-30.

7. Success Metrics

Primary: 8-week post-launch lift in self-reported pronunciation confidence
(target 2.9 -> 3.6/5, described in Goals). Secondary: D7 retention lift
among users who try Voice Practice Mode at least 3 times in their first
week, target +4 percentage points versus the holdout. Guardrail: lesson
completion rate must not drop by more than 1 percentage point due to added
friction from the recording flow; if it does, we roll back the default-on
placement and make it opt-in instead.

8. Rollout Plan

Week 1-2: internal dogfood with Canto staff (target 200 internal testers).
Week 3-4: 10% of new-lesson-start traffic in Hong Kong and US markets
only, monitoring latency and scoring-accuracy-in-the-wild via a thumbs-up/
thumbs-down feedback affordance on each score.
Week 5-6: expand to 50% of traffic across all markets if latency and
guardrail metrics hold.
Week 7-8: 100% rollout, feature flag remains in place for at least one
quarter for fast rollback capability.

9. Dependencies and Risks

The scoring model depends on the forced-alignment component currently
being built by the Speech team (tracked separately, ETA 2026-06-20);
Voice Practice Mode cannot enter internal dogfood until that lands. Devraj
flagged a risk that the 25-speaker validation set may not adequately cover
diaspora accents (Vancouver, San Francisco, London Cantonese-speaking
communities are underrepresented at only 4 of the 25 speakers), which
could cause the feature to under-score diaspora users disproportionately;
Ines has approved an additional data-collection pass targeting 15 more
diaspora speakers before the 50% rollout gate in week 5.

A second dependency is on the Infra team's approval of the scoring
service's projected steady-state cost. At 2.1 million attempts/month and
the $0.004-per-attempt target, the feature would add roughly $8,400/month
to the infra bill, which Ravi Chandrasekaran's team has pre-approved as
part of the Q3 capacity plan contingent on the P95 latency requirement
being met without over-provisioning GPU capacity beyond two dedicated
inference nodes. If the scoring model's real-world latency comes in above
budget, Devraj's team has a fallback plan to move from a per-syllable
forced-alignment approach to a coarser per-phrase scoring model, which
internal benchmarking suggests would cut latency by roughly 40% at the
cost of losing the syllable-level breakdown in the feedback UI — this
fallback would require a follow-up design review with Sofia before it
could ship, since it changes a core piece of the user-facing experience
described in section 5.3.

10. Open Questions for Launch Review

Ines has flagged three items that need sign-off before the week 3-4
external rollout begins: (1) final confirmation from Legal that the
default-delete audio retention policy satisfies the updated privacy
policy language in all initial rollout markets (Hong Kong and US); (2)
confirmation from the Speech team that the forced-alignment component's
June 20 ETA is still on track, given it is a hard blocker for dogfood;
and (3) a decision from design on whether the 1-3 star aggregate rating
should be shown after every single attempt or only as a rolling average
across a user's last 5 attempts on a given phrase, since the March
interviews were inconclusive on which framing reduced discouragement more
effectively for repeat low scorers.


According to the PRD, what is the target improvement in self-reported pronunciation confidence, over what time window, and what specific guardrail metric would trigger a rollback from default-on to opt-in?

POSTMORTEM: Checkout Service Outage, June 3 2026
Severity: SEV-1
Duration: 47 minutes (14:22 - 15:09 UTC)
Author: Marcus Feld, Checkout Team
Status: Final, reviewed by Incident Review Board on 2026-06-10

Summary

On June 3, 2026, the checkout service experienced a full outage lasting 47
minutes, during which 100% of checkout attempts failed with a 503 error.
We estimate 61,000 failed checkout attempts and approximately $890,000 in
directly attributable lost revenue, based on the trailing-4-week average
conversion rate applied to traffic during the outage window. The root
cause was a connection pool exhaustion in the checkout service triggered
by a slow query introduced in a database migration that ran earlier the
same day, combined with a misconfigured circuit breaker that failed to
trip and shed load as designed.

Timeline (all times UTC)

09:15 — Database migration MIG-2291 (adding a new index to the
promotions_applied table) is deployed to production via the standard
migration pipeline. The migration itself completes successfully in 4
minutes and is marked green in the dashboard.

09:15-14:20 — No visible impact. Query planner continues using the old
index for most promotion lookups since the new index's statistics have
not yet been refreshed by the nightly ANALYZE job (scheduled for 02:00
UTC, meaning it would not pick up the new index until the following
night).

14:20 — A marketing-triggered flash sale begins, increasing checkout
traffic to roughly 3.2x normal levels within 90 seconds. Under this load,
the query planner — now working with a larger working set that pushes
some previously-cached pages out of the buffer pool — switches to using
the new, not-yet-analyzed index for promotion lookups, and the query
degrades from a typical 8ms to an observed 1,400ms median.

14:22 — Checkout service connection pool (max 200 connections per
instance, 40 instances) begins saturating as queries queue behind the
slow promotion lookups. First 503s appear.

14:23 — Automated paging fires for elevated 503 rate; on-call engineer
Fatima Nasr acknowledges within 90 seconds.

14:26 — Fatima confirms checkout service is returning 503s broadly, not
isolated to one instance, and escalates to SEV-1, paging in Marcus Feld
(service owner) and Dilnoza Karimova (SRE).

14:31 — The circuit breaker in front of the promotions database, which is
configured to trip and serve a degraded "promotions temporarily
unavailable" checkout path after 5 consecutive timeouts, does not trip.
Investigation during the incident (and confirmed afterward) found the
breaker's timeout threshold was configured at 5,000ms — well above the
observed 1,400ms slow-query time — meaning individual queries never
counted as "failed" from the breaker's perspective even though they were
slow enough to exhaust the connection pool. This was the second root
cause.

14:38 — Team identifies the newly-added index via recent-changes review of
the promotions_applied table and correlates it with MIG-2291's deploy
time.

14:44 — Team makes the call to force ANALYZE on promotions_applied
manually rather than drop the index, reasoning that dropping it under
load carried its own lock-contention risk. Manual ANALYZE is kicked off.

14:58 — ANALYZE completes; query planner begins using updated statistics
and reverts to using the old index (or an efficient plan) for most
lookups. Query latency begins dropping, observed median down to 45ms
within 2 minutes.

15:02 — Connection pool pressure eases; 503 rate drops below 5%.

15:09 — 503 rate returns to baseline (<0.1%); incident declared resolved.

15:09-16:00 — Monitoring continued at heightened attention per standard
post-incident watch period; no recurrence.

Root Causes

1. Primary: a newly added database index lacked updated planner
statistics, and under a sudden 3.2x traffic spike the query planner chose
a poor execution plan using that index, causing promotion-lookup query
latency to increase roughly 175x (8ms to 1,400ms).

2. Contributing: the circuit breaker protecting checkout from promotions-
database slowness was configured with a 5,000ms timeout threshold, far
above the level at which slow queries actually exhaust the connection
pool. This meant the breaker provided no effective protection for this
failure mode — it is only tuned to catch outright query failures or
extreme (5+ second) hangs, not the kind of moderate degradation that
still saturates a fixed-size connection pool under high concurrency.

3. Contributing: the migration pipeline does not run ANALYZE
automatically after adding an index, relying instead on the nightly job,
which created a roughly 17-hour window (09:15 to the next 02:00) during
which the new index existed without accurate statistics.

Impact

61,000 failed checkout attempts, ~$890,000 estimated lost revenue,
47-minute full-service outage, SEV-1. No data loss or corruption occurred.
Customer support received 2,340 inbound contacts related to the outage
during and shortly after the window.

Action Items

1. Add automatic ANALYZE as a mandatory post-step in the migration
pipeline for any migration that adds or modifies an index. Owner: Data
Platform team (Jasmine Okafor). Due: 2026-06-17. Priority: P0.

2. Reconfigure the checkout-to-promotions circuit breaker with a tiered
threshold: trip on either 5 consecutive failures OR median latency over
the trailing 10-second window exceeding 200ms, whichever comes first.
Owner: Marcus Feld. Due: 2026-06-13. Priority: P0.

3. Add a load-based canary step to the migration pipeline: any index
addition on a table above 10M rows (promotions_applied has 34M rows)
must be validated against a synthetic 3x-traffic replay in staging before
being eligible for production deploy. Owner: Fatima Nasr. Due: 2026-07-01.
Priority: P1.

4. Reduce marketing's lead time requirement for flash-sale traffic spikes
from same-day to a minimum 48 hours' notice to the infra on-call rotation,
so capacity and recent-change review can happen proactively. Owner: Ines
Wu (Product) to coordinate with Marketing. Due: 2026-06-20. Priority: P1.

Lessons Learned

The incident review board noted that both root causes were individually
insufficient to cause a SEV-1 — the missing ANALYZE alone would likely
have caused a brief, self-healing slowdown once the nightly job ran, and
the misconfigured breaker alone would have been masked by normal query
performance. It was the combination, surfaced specifically by a sudden
traffic spike, that produced the outage. This reinforces a standing theme
from three of the last five SEV-1s: our circuit breakers are tuned for
binary failure, not gradual degradation, and this is now tracked as a
cross-team reliability initiative for Q3.

Supplementary Detail: Why the Circuit Breaker Configuration Went Unnoticed

During the review board session, Dilnoza Karimova walked through why the
5,000ms threshold had gone unquestioned since it was originally set in
late 2024. The value was inherited from a template used for a different,
much larger downstream dependency (the fraud-scoring service, which has
its own p99 latency of around 2,800ms under normal conditions and needed
a generous threshold to avoid nuisance trips). When the promotions-
database circuit breaker was created by copying that template during the
2024 checkout-service refactor, no one revisited the threshold against
promotions' actual latency profile, which at the time had a p99 of only
around 60ms. The review board's broader finding was that circuit breaker
thresholds are not currently tracked as configuration requiring periodic
review, unlike, for example, database connection pool sizes, which are
reviewed quarterly by the SRE team. Dilnoza proposed adding circuit
breaker thresholds to that same quarterly review checklist, and the board
accepted this as an addendum to action item 2, to be handled as part of
its rollout rather than a separate ticket.

The review board also asked Fatima Nasr to clarify, for the record, why
the connection pool size (200 per instance, 40 instances, for a
cluster-wide total of 8,000 connections) was not itself flagged as
undersized during the incident. Fatima's assessment was that 8,000
connections was more than adequate for the observed 3.2x traffic spike
under normal query latency (8ms), and that the pool was correctly sized
for its intended purpose; the exhaustion was purely a symptom of queries
taking 175x longer than expected, not of insufficient pool capacity. The
board agreed this ruled out connection pool sizing as a contributing root
cause, and action item 3 (load-based canary testing) was scoped
specifically to catch planner regressions like this one rather than to
address capacity sizing more broadly.


What were the two root causes of the outage, what was the estimated revenue impact, and what specific new threshold was proposed for the checkout-to-promotions circuit breaker as a fix?

Effects of Tokenizer Vocabulary Size on Cantonese ASR Transfer Learning
A Research Writeup
Authors: Cheuk-Yin Lam, Dorothy Mensah, Rafael Ibarra
Affiliation: Speech and Language Research Group
Draft v3, 2026-04-02 (internal circulation, not for external distribution)

Abstract

We study how subword tokenizer vocabulary size affects transfer learning
performance when adapting a pretrained multilingual ASR (automatic speech
recognition) acoustic-to-text decoder to Cantonese, a language with
comparatively limited labeled speech data relative to Mandarin or English
in our training mix. We fine-tune five otherwise-identical decoder
configurations differing only in vocabulary size (2,000, 8,000, 16,000,
32,000, and 64,000 subword tokens, all trained with SentencePiece unigram
segmentation on a matched Cantonese text corpus) and evaluate on a held-out
set of 14 hours of transcribed Hong Kong Cantonese conversational speech.
We find that character error rate (CER) is minimized at the 8,000-token
configuration (CER 6.3%), with both smaller and larger vocabularies
performing worse — the 2,000-token model reaches 8.1% CER and the
64,000-token model reaches 7.4% CER — and we argue this reflects a
tradeoff specific to low-resource fine-tuning between sequence length
(favoring larger vocabularies) and per-token training signal density
(favoring smaller vocabularies, since each token is seen more often during
the comparatively short fine-tuning run).

1. Motivation

Cantonese presents a specific tokenization challenge distinct from
Mandarin: while both use Chinese characters, Cantonese written
conversational text (as opposed to formal Standard Written Chinese, which
is grammatically closer to Mandarin) includes a substantial fraction of
Cantonese-specific characters and particles (e.g., sentence-final
particles like ge3, laa1, gaa3) that are rare or absent in the Mandarin-
heavy pretraining corpora typically used for multilingual ASR decoders.
Our internal audit of the base model's original 128,000-token vocabulary
found that only 340 tokens were Cantonese-specific single characters or
common Cantonese digraphs, meaning most Cantonese-specific vocabulary
would be segmented into multiple subword pieces or, in the worst case,
into individual bytes via the fallback byte-level encoding. We
hypothesized that retraining a smaller, Cantonese-focused vocabulary for
the fine-tuning stage — rather than reusing the base model's tokenizer
outright — could improve transfer performance, and this study is designed
to find the vocabulary size that best supports that transfer.

2. Data

Our Cantonese text corpus for tokenizer training combined three sources:
the HKCanCor conversational corpus (approximately 230,000 words), a
crawled and filtered set of Cantonese-language forum posts and subtitles
(approximately 4.1 million words after deduplication and profanity/PII
filtering), and 62 hours of in-house transcribed speech data used for the
downstream ASR fine-tuning itself, contributing an additional 480,000
words of transcript text. All three sources were combined for tokenizer
training; only the 62-hour transcribed speech set was used for the actual
acoustic fine-tuning, split 48 hours train / 14 hours held-out test, with
the split stratified to ensure both training and test sets included
speech from all six original recording sessions and a comparable mix of
Hong Kong Island, Kowloon, and New Territories speaker backgrounds as
self-reported by participants.

3. Method

We start from a pretrained multilingual encoder-decoder ASR model
(encoder frozen throughout all experiments; only the decoder and its
embedding/output projection layers are fine-tuned, following the
standard low-resource transfer recipe used elsewhere in our pipeline). For
each of the five vocabulary sizes, we train a SentencePiece unigram model
on the combined text corpus described above, then reinitialize the
decoder's token embedding and output projection layers to match the new
vocabulary size (all other decoder weights are initialized from the
pretrained checkpoint and are compatible across vocabulary sizes since
they operate on the same hidden dimension, 1024). Each configuration is
then fine-tuned for exactly 40,000 steps at batch size 32, using the same
learning rate schedule (linear warmup over 2,000 steps to a peak of 3e-5,
then linear decay), the same random seed, and the same data ordering, so
that vocabulary size is the only varied factor.

4. Results

CER on the 14-hour held-out test set, by vocabulary size:
2,000 tokens: 8.1% CER, average sequence length 34.2 tokens/utterance.
8,000 tokens: 6.3% CER, average sequence length 21.7 tokens/utterance.
16,000 tokens: 6.6% CER, average sequence length 18.4 tokens/utterance.
32,000 tokens: 7.0% CER, average sequence length 16.1 tokens/utterance.
64,000 tokens: 7.4% CER, average sequence length 14.3 tokens/utterance.

We also measured out-of-vocabulary (OOV) fallback rate — the fraction of
reference transcript characters that the tokenizer could not represent
without falling back to byte-level encoding. This decreased monotonically
with vocabulary size, from 3.8% at 2,000 tokens to 0.2% at 64,000 tokens,
confirming that larger vocabularies do capture more Cantonese-specific
characters directly. However, this did not translate into better CER
beyond the 8,000-token point, which we discuss in section 5.

Breaking CER down by error type at the 8,000-token configuration
(best-performing): substitution errors accounted for 4.1 of the 6.3
percentage points, deletion errors for 1.4 points, and insertion errors
for 0.8 points. The substitution errors were disproportionately
concentrated on tone-adjacent character confusions (e.g., characters that
differ primarily by tone but share similar segmental pronunciation),
consistent with tone information being harder for the acoustic encoder to
disambiguate than segmental (consonant/vowel) information, especially for
characters that were infrequent in the 48-hour fine-tuning set.

5. Discussion

We interpret the non-monotonic relationship between vocabulary size and
CER as a resource-constrained tradeoff. With only 48 hours of fine-tuning
speech (and a fixed 40,000-step budget), larger vocabularies mean each
individual token is seen fewer times during fine-tuning, since the same
amount of speech is now segmented into fewer, more specific tokens spread
across a larger token inventory. At 64,000 tokens, many Cantonese-specific
tokens beyond the most common few thousand appear fewer than 20 times in
the entire 48-hour training set, likely insufficient to learn a reliable
acoustic-to-token mapping for those tokens specifically, even though the
tokenizer can represent them exactly. Conversely, at 2,000 tokens, every
token is well-trained, but sequences are 60% longer on average (34.2 vs.
21.7 tokens/utterance), which increases the number of autoregressive
decoding steps and, we believe, the opportunity for compounding errors
across a longer sequence.

This suggests the optimal vocabulary size is a function of fine-tuning
data volume, not a fixed property of the language — a team with access to
500+ hours of transcribed Cantonese speech might see the larger
vocabularies pull ahead, since token-frequency-per-vocabulary-item would
no longer be the binding constraint. We did not have the budget to test
this directly in the current study but flag it as the natural next
experiment.

6. Limitations

Our held-out test set, while stratified across recording sessions and
broad geographic self-report categories, is drawn from the same six
original recording sessions as the training data and may not generalize
well to substantially different recording conditions (e.g., phone-call
audio, which was not represented in any of our six sessions) or to
Cantonese varieties spoken outside Hong Kong. We also note that freezing
the encoder throughout may understate the benefit of larger vocabularies,
since a fully fine-tuned encoder might better exploit finer-grained
subword distinctions; we froze the encoder to match our production
fine-tuning constraints (encoder fine-tuning requires roughly 4x the GPU
memory in our current training setup) rather than for scientific reasons,
and this is a real limitation on how far these findings generalize to
full fine-tuning setups.

7. Conclusion

For our specific setting — 48 hours of Cantonese fine-tuning speech, a
frozen pretrained encoder, and a 40,000-step fine-tuning budget — an
8,000-token Cantonese-specific vocabulary minimized CER at 6.3%,
outperforming both smaller (2,000-token, 8.1% CER) and larger (64,000-
token, 7.4% CER) alternatives. We recommend 8,000 tokens as the starting
point for similar low-resource Cantonese ASR fine-tuning efforts and
recommend revisiting vocabulary size as a hyperparameter if fine-tuning
data volume changes substantially from the 48-hour scale studied here.


Which vocabulary size achieved the lowest character error rate, what was that CER value, and what does the paper identify as the underlying tradeoff that explains why both smaller and larger vocabularies performed worse?

REMOTE WORK AND EQUIPMENT REIMBURSEMENT POLICY
Document owner: People Operations
Effective date: 2026-07-01
Supersedes: Remote Work Policy v2.3 (2024-11-15)
Applies to: All full-time employees; contractors covered under Section 8
Approved by: Executive Leadership Team, 2026-06-18

1. Purpose and Scope

This policy governs eligibility for remote and hybrid work arrangements,
the equipment and stipends the company provides to support home-based
work, and the process for requesting exceptions. It applies to all
full-time employees globally, with region-specific addenda in Appendix A
for tax and equipment-import considerations in the EU, UK, and Hong Kong
entities. Contractors are covered under a modified version of this policy
described in Section 8; interns are not eligible for equipment
reimbursement under this policy and should refer to the separate Intern
Program Handbook.

2. Work Arrangement Categories

The company recognizes three work arrangement categories. "Remote" means
an employee's primary work location is not a company office and they are
expected in an office no more than 4 days per quarter for team events.
"Hybrid" means an employee is expected in a designated office at least 2
days per week, with specific day requirements set by their manager and
documented in the team's working agreement. "Office-based" means an
employee is expected in the office at least 4 days per week. An
employee's category is set at time of hire or role change and recorded in
the HR system; changing categories requires manager approval and, for
moves from office-based or hybrid to remote, a written justification
submitted to People Operations at least 30 days before the intended
transition date.

3. Equipment Provided

All full-time employees, regardless of category, are eligible for a
standard equipment package: one laptop (refreshed every 3 years or upon
documented failure, whichever comes first), one external monitor up to
27 inches, a keyboard and mouse, and a headset. Remote and hybrid
employees are additionally eligible for a one-time home office setup
stipend of $850 USD (or local currency equivalent, see Appendix A for
current conversion reference rates, updated quarterly), which can be used
for items including but not limited to: a second monitor, a desk, an
ergonomic chair, desk lighting, or a webcam. This stipend must be claimed
within 90 days of an employee's remote or hybrid start date; unclaimed
stipend amounts do not roll over or convert to cash.

4. Ongoing Remote Work Stipend

In addition to the one-time setup stipend, remote employees (not hybrid)
receive an ongoing monthly stipend of $75 USD (or local equivalent) to
offset home internet and utility costs associated with working from home.
This stipend is paid automatically through payroll for all employees
coded as "Remote" in the HR system and does not require monthly expense
submission; it is treated as taxable income in jurisdictions where local
tax law requires this (see Appendix A), and payroll will withhold
accordingly. Hybrid employees are not eligible for the monthly stipend,
reflecting that they retain regular access to office-provided internet
and utilities on their in-office days.

5. Equipment Replacement and Repair

Equipment issues should be reported to IT Support via the standard
ticketing system. For remote and hybrid employees, IT Support will
arrange for a replacement or loaner device to be shipped, with a target
turnaround of 5 business days for domestic shipments and 10 business days
for international shipments, from ticket confirmation to delivery.
Employees are responsible for returning failed or replaced equipment
using a prepaid shipping label provided by IT; equipment not returned
within 30 days of a replacement being issued will be treated as
unreturned company property and may be deducted from final pay upon
separation, consistent with the Company Property Policy referenced in the
employee handbook.

6. Travel to Office for Remote Employees

Remote employees whose manager requests their attendance at an in-office
event beyond the standard 4-day-per-quarter expectation (e.g., an
all-hands, a planning offsite) are eligible for travel expense
reimbursement under the standard Travel and Expense Policy, provided the
request is made by the manager at least 14 days in advance except in
cases of urgent business need. Travel within the standard 4-day-per-
quarter allotment is reimbursed under the same policy but does not
require the 14-day advance notice, since it is a planned, known
obligation.

7. International Remote Work

Employees wishing to work remotely from a country other than their
employment country for more than 20 consecutive business days must
submit a request through the International Remote Work process at least
45 days in advance, since this may trigger tax, immigration, or
employment-law obligations for the company in the destination country.
Approval is not guaranteed and depends on whether the company has
existing entity or contractor-of-record infrastructure in the destination
country; as of this policy's effective date, pre-approved destination
countries include Canada, the UK, Germany, Portugal, Singapore, and
Japan. Requests for other countries are evaluated case by case and
typically take 3-4 weeks to resolve given the need for external legal
review.

8. Contractors

Contractors engaged for 6 months or longer and working more than 30
hours/week are eligible for a reduced equipment package (laptop and
peripherals only, no monitor or setup stipend) at the discretion of the
engaging manager and subject to budget approval from the relevant VP.
Contractors are not eligible for the ongoing monthly remote work stipend
described in Section 4, regardless of engagement length.

9. Exceptions

Requests for exceptions to any provision of this policy (for example, a
higher equipment stipend for documented accessibility needs, or a
work-arrangement category exception) should be submitted to People
Operations via the HR portal's Policy Exception form. Accessibility-
related exceptions are reviewed on an expedited basis, typically within 5
business days, in coordination with the Accommodations team; other
exceptions follow the standard review cycle, typically 10 business days.

10. Policy Review

This policy is reviewed annually by People Operations in consultation
with Finance and Legal, or sooner if triggered by a material change in
tax law, immigration law, or company real estate strategy affecting three
or more regions. The next scheduled review is 2027-06-01.

Appendix A: Region-Specific Notes (Summary)

European Union: the one-time setup stipend is converted at a quarterly
reference rate published by Finance on the People Operations intranet
page; as of the most recent quarterly update, this rate placed the
stipend at approximately 790 EUR. Equipment shipped to EU addresses
valued above 500 EUR may be subject to import duties depending on the
member state; IT Support handles customs documentation for company-
shipped equipment, but stipend-funded purchases made directly by the
employee (e.g., buying a desk locally) are the employee's responsibility
to source appropriately and are not affected by import duty
considerations since no cross-border shipment occurs.

United Kingdom: the monthly remote work stipend is treated as a taxable
benefit under current HMRC guidance and will appear on payslips as a
separate taxable line item; the one-time setup stipend is not treated as
taxable when spent on qualifying home-office equipment with receipts
submitted, but is taxable if paid out without receipts (uncommon, and
requires a Policy Exception per Section 9).

Hong Kong: equipment import considerations are minimal for standard IT
hardware under current Hong Kong customs treatment, so IT Support
generally ships standard equipment packages directly without additional
customs processing. The monthly remote stipend equivalent is set at
approximately 585 HKD, referencing the same quarterly conversion process
described above.

These regional notes are maintained separately from the core policy text
and updated on a rolling basis as tax guidance changes; employees should
consult the live intranet version of Appendix A rather than a cached or
printed copy, since the numeric stipend-equivalent figures in local
currency are refreshed quarterly even when the core USD figures in
Sections 3 and 4 remain unchanged.

11. Frequently Raised Questions (Non-Binding Guidance)

People Operations has compiled the following clarifications based on
questions received since the prior policy version, for convenience only;
in case of conflict, the numbered sections above control. First, employees
who change from hybrid to remote mid-year remain eligible for the
one-time setup stipend if they did not already claim it as a hybrid
employee, since hybrid employees are eligible for the setup stipend under
Section 3 but not the ongoing monthly stipend under Section 4 — the
90-day claim window in that case runs from the category-change effective
date, not the original hire date. Second, a laptop refresh under Section
3 does not reset or extend eligibility for a new one-time setup stipend;
the setup stipend is tied to work-arrangement category changes, not to
equipment refresh cycles, and is a career-long one-time benefit per
category transition rather than a recurring one. Third, questions about
whether a specific purchase (for example, a standing desk converter versus
a full sit-stand desk) qualifies under the setup stipend should be
directed to People Operations before purchase; retroactive approval is
not guaranteed.


What is the one-time home office setup stipend amount and its claim deadline, and how does the ongoing monthly remote work stipend differ in eligibility between remote and hybrid employees?

TRIP REPORT: Vendor Site Visit, Shenzhen Fabrication Partner
Traveler: Kenji Okamura, Hardware Procurement
Trip dates: 2026-04-20 to 2026-04-24
Destination: Shenzhen, Guangdong, China — Longhua District fabrication
facility of Everbright Precision Components (EPC)
Distribution: Hardware team, Procurement leadership, Supply Chain Risk

Purpose of Trip

EPC has been our sole-source supplier for custom heatsink assemblies used
in our GPU server chassis since 2024, currently shipping approximately
28,000 units per quarter. Following a Q1 2026 quality escapement (a batch
of 1,200 units with an out-of-spec thermal interface material adhesion
issue, caught in our incoming inspection but not before causing a two-week
delay to the Austin data center buildout) I traveled to EPC's facility to
audit their quality control process firsthand, evaluate their proposed
corrective actions, and assess a secondary sourcing option they proposed
as a hedge against future single-line-dependency risk.

Day 1 (April 20): Travel and Facility Orientation

Arrived Shenzhen Bao'an International Airport at 14:40 local time,
transferred directly to EPC's Longhua facility for an orientation meeting
with our primary contacts: Zhang Wei (VP of Manufacturing), Amy Fung
(Quality Director), and Leo Tang (our dedicated account manager). EPC
walked through their facility overview: three production lines dedicated
to our account, running two shifts daily, with a combined capacity of
roughly 3,400 heatsink assemblies per day when running at full
utilization (they reported current utilization for our account at about
78% of that capacity, consistent with the 28,000/quarter figure once
weekends and planned maintenance days are accounted for).

Day 2 (April 21): Quality Control Process Audit

Spent the full day on the production floor with Amy Fung's quality team.
Walked the incoming-materials inspection process (thermal interface
material is sourced from a sub-supplier, Guangzhou Thermal Solutions Co.,
and is currently sampled at a 2% lot rate for adhesion testing) and
confirmed this was the point of failure in the Q1 escapement — the 2%
sample rate had a roughly 1-in-8 chance of missing a defect concentrated
in a specific sub-batch, which is what happened. Amy proposed increasing
the sample rate to 8% for thermal interface material specifically
(leaving other incoming materials at existing rates, which have not shown
similar issues), estimating this would add approximately 40 minutes of
inspection time per lot and would not affect overall throughput given
current line scheduling slack. I pushed for a stronger commitment:
100% inspection for the first three lots of any new sub-batch from
Guangzhou Thermal Solutions, tapering to 8% only after three consecutive
clean lots. Amy agreed to this in principle, pending sign-off from
Zhang Wei, which came through by end of day.

Also reviewed EPC's final assembly QC, including their automated optical
inspection (AOI) system for solder joint quality on the mounting brackets;
this system has a reported false-negative rate of under 0.1% based on
their internal validation, which I found credible given the sampling
methodology they showed me, though I did not independently verify it
against a blind test set during the visit — flagging this as a follow-up
item for our own quality team to consider requesting.

Day 3 (April 22): Corrective Action Review and Secondary Sourcing
Discussion

Morning session: EPC presented their formal corrective action report for
the Q1 escapement, including the revised sampling plan discussed on Day
2, a root-cause finding that matched our own investigation, and a
commitment to a quarterly joint quality review call going forward (first
call scheduled for July 2026). They also offered a goodwill credit of
$34,000 against our Q2 invoice, covering roughly 60% of our estimated
internal cost from the delay (we did not pursue a formal claim for the
remainder, judging the relationship value and their response quality to
outweigh pursuing full recovery).

Afternoon session: discussed the secondary sourcing proposal. EPC
suggested qualifying a second facility they operate in Dongguan (about 45
minutes from Longhua) to produce an identical heatsink assembly, positioning
this as risk mitigation for us without introducing a genuinely independent
supplier (a distinction I raised directly with Zhang Wei — a second EPC
facility does not protect us against an EPC-wide issue, such as a
sub-supplier problem affecting both facilities, or a company-level
disruption). Zhang Wei acknowledged this limitation and did not push back
on my assessment; EPC's pitch was framed honestly as a capacity/geographic
hedge rather than a true supplier-diversity hedge. I recommended we still
evaluate the Dongguan facility for capacity-overflow purposes, but pursue
a genuinely independent second supplier separately for true risk
diversification — this is now a separate action item for our team, not an
EPC-facilitated one.

Day 4 (April 23): Line Walk-Through with Engineering Follow-Ups and
Departure Prep

Spent the morning with our own hardware engineer, who had joined the trip
specifically for this day, reviewing a proposed design tweak to the
heatsink fin spacing (increasing from 1.8mm to 2.1mm) intended to improve
airflow for the next-generation chassis under evaluation. EPC's
engineering team estimated this change would require a new stamping die
(lead time 6 weeks, cost approximately $18,000, one-time tooling cost
absorbed by EPC per our existing contract terms for design changes we
request) but would not otherwise disrupt the existing production lines.
Afternoon was reserved for a wrap-up meeting with Zhang Wei, Amy, and Leo
to confirm the corrective action commitments in writing; EPC agreed to
send a signed corrective action report within 5 business days (received
on April 29, within the commitment window).

Day 5 (April 24): Return travel.

Summary of Outcomes and Follow-Ups

1. Thermal interface material inspection: EPC to implement 100%
inspection for first three lots of any new sub-batch, tapering to 8%
after three clean lots (up from the prior flat 2%). Effective
immediately per EPC's April 22 commitment.
2. Goodwill credit of $34,000 to be applied to Q2 invoice — confirm with
Finance this lands correctly.
3. Quarterly joint quality review calls to begin July 2026 — add to
shared calendar, Procurement to own scheduling.
4. Dongguan facility: evaluate for capacity overflow only, not supplier
diversification — follow-up assessment to be scheduled with Zhang Wei's
team within Q2.
5. Independent second-source qualification: new action item for
Procurement, target to identify 2-3 candidate suppliers by end of Q3
2026, separate from any EPC-affiliated option.
6. Fin spacing design change: engineering to confirm go/no-go on the
2.1mm design by May 15 given the 6-week tooling lead time, to avoid
delaying the next-generation chassis timeline.

Overall Assessment

EPC responded to the Q1 escapement credibly and their proposed corrective
actions, once tightened during this visit, should materially reduce
recurrence risk for the specific failure mode we saw. The facility itself
is well-run and utilization headroom (78% of capacity) gives us confidence
in near-term scaling. The clearest gap coming out of this trip is
supplier diversification, which remains unaddressed by anything EPC
itself can offer and should be treated as its own workstream.


What specific change did EPC agree to make to its incoming-inspection sampling process for thermal interface material, and why did the traveler view the proposed Dongguan facility as an inadequate solution for supplier risk diversification?

The Founding and Early Years of the Tai Ping Shan Waterworks
A Historical Narrative Account

Note: This account is a fictionalized composite historical narrative
prepared for internal training/evaluation purposes, drawing on the general
form of 19th-century colonial infrastructure histories. Names, dates, and
figures are illustrative rather than a record of actual events.

Origins of the Project (1861-1863)

In the early 1860s, the rapidly growing settlement on the northern shore
of Hong Kong Island faced a worsening freshwater crisis. The population of
the Tai Ping Shan district, home to a dense mix of dockworkers, small
traders, and recent arrivals from Guangdong province, had grown from an
estimated 6,000 residents in 1855 to over 19,000 by 1861, far outpacing
the capacity of the shallow wells and seasonal streams the district had
relied upon. Colonial Surveyor-General Aldous Whitfield submitted a
proposal to the Legislative Council in March 1861 for a piped waterworks
system drawing from a catchment reservoir to be built in the hills above
the district, at what would become known as the Pok Fu Lam catchment
area. The proposal budgeted the project at 68,000 Hong Kong dollars, to
be funded through a combination of general revenue and a proposed water
rate levied on connected households.

The Council debated the proposal for over a year, with the primary
objection coming from members representing merchant interests who argued
the funds would be better spent on harbor infrastructure supporting trade.
It was ultimately a cholera outbreak in the summer of 1862 — which
colonial health records attributed to contaminated well water in the
Tai Ping Shan district specifically, recording 340 deaths over a
six-week period in July and August — that shifted the Council's position.
Whitfield's revised proposal, submitted in October 1862 with an increased
budget of 81,000 dollars to account for an expanded pipe network reaching
further into the district's most crowded blocks, was approved in December
1862, with construction authorized to begin the following spring.

Construction Phase (1863-1867)

Construction began in April 1863 under the direct supervision of
engineer Thomas Ashworth Reeve, who had previously overseen waterworks
projects in Bombay before relocating to Hong Kong in 1860. The project
faced significant early setbacks: the initial reservoir excavation at
Pok Fu Lam struck an unexpectedly hard granite layer in the summer of
1863, requiring the import of specialized blasting equipment from
Britain that did not arrive until January 1864, delaying the reservoir's
completion by roughly eight months against Reeve's original schedule.

Labor for the project was drawn primarily from local contractors, with
Reeve's records indicating a workforce that peaked at approximately 850
laborers during the reservoir excavation phase in 1864, organized under
several Chinese labor contractors who negotiated directly with the
colonial Public Works Department. Conditions were difficult; Reeve's own
correspondence to the Surveyor-General's office in September 1864 notes
"considerable illness among the excavation gangs, attributable in my
judgment to the exposed working conditions during the wet season," and
recommends (not clear whether implemented) a covered rest structure for
workers during the summer monsoon months.

The reservoir itself, completed in March 1865, had a capacity of
approximately 40 million gallons, considerably larger than Whitfield's
original 1861 estimate of 25 million gallons — the increase reflecting
both the district's continued population growth during the construction
period and a revised engineering judgment by Reeve that a larger
catchment would better handle seasonal variation in rainfall, given that
Hong Kong's rainfall was known even then to be heavily concentrated in
the May-to-September period, with the reservoir needing to buffer supply
through the drier winter months.

Pipe-laying into the Tai Ping Shan district itself proceeded in parallel
with the reservoir construction from 1864 onward, using cast iron piping
imported from Glasgow foundries. By the time the system formally opened
in June 1867, the network extended to 46 public standpipes throughout
the district, though — reflecting the economics and politics of the
period — private in-home connections were available only to
households able to pay the connection fee and ongoing water rate,
meaning the majority of Tai Ping Shan's poorer residents continued to
draw water from the public standpipes rather than in-home taps well into
the 1870s.

Early Operational Years and the 1868 Drought

The waterworks' first serious operational test came during a severe
drought in the winter of 1868, when unusually low rainfall through the
preceding wet season left the Pok Fu Lam reservoir at an estimated 30% of
capacity by February 1868. The Public Works Department implemented a
rationing scheme, restricting standpipe access to specific hours (6-8am
and 5-7pm) for a period of eleven weeks. Colonial records indicate this
rationing period, while causing significant public complaint documented
in contemporary newspaper accounts, did not produce a public health
crisis comparable to the 1862 cholera outbreak — a fact the Public Works
Department's 1868 annual report cited as evidence the waterworks had
succeeded in its core public health objective even under a stress-test
drought scenario, since even rationed piped water was markedly cleaner
than the well water it had replaced.

Following the 1868 drought, the colonial government approved a second
reservoir (Pok Fu Lam Upper, completed 1871) specifically to provide a
buffer against future dry seasons, roughly doubling total storage
capacity for the system serving Tai Ping Shan and the adjoining districts
that had by then also been connected to the network.

Financing and the Water Rate Controversy

The water rate structure adopted to fund ongoing operations proved
contentious throughout the 1860s and into the following decade. The
original 1862 proposal set the rate at a flat annual charge per connected
household regardless of usage, a structure Whitfield favored for its
simplicity of collection but which merchant Council members criticized as
regressive, since it charged small households the same as large
commercial premises with far higher consumption. A revised, tiered rate
structure — distinguishing between domestic, commercial, and industrial
connections, with industrial premises paying roughly four times the
domestic rate — was adopted in 1869 following a Council review, and
records suggest this materially improved revenue collection: reported
water rate revenue rose from approximately 11,000 dollars in the 1868
fiscal year to 19,000 dollars in 1870, even though the number of
connected households grew by a comparatively modest amount over the same
period, indicating the tiered structure captured commercial and
industrial usage that had previously been undercharged.

Public standpipe access, notably, remained free to all residents
throughout this period regardless of the household connection debates,
reflecting the Public Works Department's explicit position — stated in
its 1867 annual report — that charging for standpipe access would
undermine the public health rationale that had justified the project's
approval in the first place. This distinction between free public
standpipe access and fee-based private connections would remain a
defining feature of Hong Kong's water policy for decades afterward.

Legacy and Assessment

By most measures available to colonial administrators at the time, the
Tai Ping Shan waterworks was judged a success: district health records
show no cholera outbreak of comparable scale to 1862 in the subsequent
two decades, though historians studying this period caution against
attributing this solely to the waterworks given simultaneous improvements
in sanitation and housing regulation during the same years. The project
is nonetheless frequently cited, in the fictionalized historiography this
account draws from, as an early example of a colonial-era public health
crisis (the 1862 outbreak) directly catalyzing large-scale infrastructure
investment that had been previously stalled on cost grounds — a pattern
that would repeat in several other Hong Kong infrastructure projects
later in the 19th century.
