Canto Inference API v2 — Reference Documentation
Base URL: https://api.canto-inference.dev/v2
Last updated: 2026-05-20
API version: 2.3.0

Overview

The Canto Inference API provides programmatic access to the Canto
language model family for text generation, embeddings, and Cantonese-
specific text normalization tasks. This document covers authentication,
core endpoints, rate limits, error handling, and versioning policy. For
SDK-specific documentation (Python, Node.js, Go), see the separate SDK
reference pages linked from the developer portal.

Authentication

All requests must include an API key via the Authorization header using
the Bearer scheme: Authorization: Bearer sk-canto-xxxxxxxx. API keys are
generated from the developer console and are scoped to a single project.
Keys do not expire automatically but can be rotated or revoked at any
time from the console; revoked keys return a 401 Unauthorized response
within approximately 30 seconds of revocation (propagation delay across
our edge authentication cache). Requests without a valid Authorization
header return 401 with error code auth_missing; requests with a malformed
or revoked key return 401 with error code auth_invalid.

Core Endpoint: POST /v2/generate

Generates text completions given a prompt or chat-formatted message list.

Request body fields:
- model (string, required): model identifier, e.g. "canto-7b-chat" or
  "canto-32b-instruct".
- messages (array, required): list of {role, content} objects, where role
  is one of "system", "user", "assistant".
- max_tokens (integer, optional, default 512): maximum tokens to
  generate. Hard ceiling is 8,192 for canto-7b-chat and 4,096 for
  canto-32b-instruct due to current serving memory constraints on the
  larger model.
- temperature (float, optional, default 0.7): sampling temperature,
  valid range 0.0-2.0.
- top_p (float, optional, default 0.95): nucleus sampling threshold.
- stream (boolean, optional, default false): if true, response is
  returned as a server-sent events stream of incremental token chunks
  rather than a single JSON payload.
- stop (array of strings, optional): up to 4 stop sequences.

Response fields (non-streaming): id, model, created (Unix timestamp),
choices (array containing message and finish_reason, where finish_reason
is one of "stop", "length", or "content_filter"), and usage (object with
prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, total_tokens).

Example: a request to canto-7b-chat with a 340-token prompt and
max_tokens of 200 that completes naturally (hits a stop condition before
the token ceiling) would return finish_reason "stop" and a usage object
showing prompt_tokens 340, completion_tokens somewhere under 200,
total_tokens as their sum.

Core Endpoint: POST /v2/embeddings

Generates dense vector embeddings for input text, primarily used for
semantic search and retrieval-augmented generation pipelines.

Request body fields:
- model (string, required): currently only "canto-embed-v2" is
  supported; the older "canto-embed-v1" was deprecated on 2026-03-01 and
  will be fully removed from the API on 2026-09-01, per the deprecation
  policy described below.
- input (string or array of strings, required): text to embed. Batches
  of up to 96 strings are accepted per request.
- dimensions (integer, optional, default 1536): output embedding
  dimensionality. canto-embed-v2 supports truncation to 256, 512, 768, or
  1536 dimensions via Matryoshka representation learning; requesting a
  non-supported intermediate value returns a 400 error with code
  invalid_dimensions.

Response fields: data (array of {embedding, index} objects, one per input
string, preserving input order), model, usage (object with
prompt_tokens only, since embeddings have no completion tokens).

Rate Limits

Rate limits are enforced per API key and vary by pricing tier:

Free tier: 20 requests/minute, 40,000 tokens/minute (input + output
combined), max 3 concurrent requests.
Pro tier: 300 requests/minute, 1,000,000 tokens/minute, max 20 concurrent
requests.
Enterprise tier: custom limits negotiated per contract; default
enterprise baseline (before any custom negotiation) is 1,500
requests/minute, 5,000,000 tokens/minute, max 100 concurrent requests.

When a rate limit is exceeded, the API returns 429 Too Many Requests with
a Retry-After header indicating the number of seconds to wait before
retrying. Rate limit status for the current key can be checked without
consuming quota via GET /v2/rate_limit_status, which returns current
usage against each of the three limit dimensions (requests, tokens,
concurrency) for the trailing 60-second window.

Error Handling

Errors follow a consistent JSON structure: {"error": {"code": string,
"message": string, "type": string}}. Common error types are
invalid_request_error (4xx, client-side issue), rate_limit_error (429),
authentication_error (401), and api_error (5xx, server-side issue,
should generally be retried with exponential backoff). We recommend a
retry strategy of up to 3 attempts with backoff starting at 1 second and
doubling, for any 429 or 5xx response; 4xx errors other than 429 should
not be retried without modifying the request, since they indicate a
malformed or invalid request that will fail identically on retry.

Content Filtering

Both /v2/generate and streaming responses may return a finish_reason of
"content_filter" if the model's output triggers the default safety
filter before reaching a natural stopping point. The filter operates on
generated output only (not on prompts, which are not blocked but may
still be logged per the data usage terms in the developer portal).
Enterprise tier customers can request adjusted filter sensitivity via
their account manager; this is not self-service configurable via the API
as of v2.3.0.

Versioning and Deprecation Policy

The API follows a minimum 6-month deprecation window for any breaking
change: deprecated endpoints or fields are announced via the developer
portal changelog and via email to project owners, remain functional for
at least 6 months post-announcement, and return a Deprecation header in
responses during that window. The v1 API (a separate base path,
api.canto-inference.dev/v1) is deprecated as of 2026-01-15 and will be
fully shut down on 2026-07-15; we strongly recommend migrating any
remaining v1 integrations before that date, since v1 traffic after
shutdown will receive 410 Gone responses with no fallback.

Webhooks (Enterprise tier only)

Enterprise customers can configure webhook endpoints to receive
asynchronous notifications for long-running batch generation jobs
submitted via POST /v2/batch. Webhook payloads are signed using
HMAC-SHA256 with a per-project secret available in the developer console;
we recommend verifying the X-Canto-Signature header against the raw
request body before processing any webhook payload, to guard against
spoofed requests. Webhook delivery is attempted up to 5 times with
exponential backoff over a 24-hour window if the receiving endpoint does
not return a 2xx response; after 5 failed attempts, the notification is
dropped and the batch job's status must be polled manually via GET
/v2/batch/{batch_id}.


Batch Endpoint: POST /v2/batch (Enterprise tier only)

For high-volume, latency-insensitive workloads, the batch endpoint accepts
up to 50,000 requests in a single submission (as a JSONL file reference
uploaded beforehand via POST /v2/files) and processes them asynchronously,
typically completing within 24 hours and billed at a 50% discount versus
the equivalent synchronous /v2/generate calls. Batch jobs return a
batch_id immediately on submission with status "queued"; status
transitions through "in_progress" to either "completed" or
"failed_partial" (if some but not all requests in the batch succeeded — a
per-request error file is made available for download in this case) or
"failed" (if the entire batch could not be processed, e.g., due to a
malformed input file). Batch jobs can be cancelled via POST
/v2/batch/{batch_id}/cancel while in "queued" or "in_progress" status;
requests already completed at time of cancellation are still billed and
their outputs remain available for download, but queued/in-flight
requests are not billed.

Idempotency

POST requests to /v2/generate and /v2/embeddings accept an optional
Idempotency-Key header. If a request with a given key is retried within
24 hours (for example, after a client-side timeout where the original
response was never received), the API returns the cached original
response rather than performing generation again, preventing duplicate
billing for what is effectively the same logical request. Idempotency
keys are scoped per API key; the same key value used by two different
projects would not collide. Note that streaming requests (stream: true)
do not support idempotency keys, since a partially-consumed stream cannot
be meaningfully replayed from cache.

SDKs and Client Libraries

Official SDKs are maintained for Python (canto-inference-python, current
version 2.3.1), Node.js (canto-inference-node, current version 2.2.0),
and Go (canto-inference-go, current version 2.0.4, released 2026-04-10 and
still marked beta). All three SDKs handle retry-with-backoff for 429 and
5xx responses automatically by default (configurable), and support both
streaming and non-streaming generation. Community-maintained SDKs exist
for Ruby and Rust but are not officially supported; issues filed against
them should go to their respective community repositories, not the
official developer support channel.


According to the API documentation, what is the maximum max_tokens value for canto-32b-instruct, what happens after 5 failed webhook delivery attempts, and by what date will the v1 API return 410 Gone responses?

Q2 2026 BUSINESS REVIEW — Cantoport Logistics
Prepared for: Board of Directors
Prepared by: Finance & Strategy Office
Distribution: Board, Executive Leadership Team
Date: 2026-07-08

Executive Summary

Cantoport Logistics closed Q2 2026 with revenue of $184.2 million, up
14.1% year-over-year and up 6.3% quarter-over-quarter, exceeding the
board-approved Q2 target of $178 million by 3.5%. Adjusted EBITDA margin
came in at 18.6%, up from 16.9% in Q1, driven primarily by the
cross-border consolidation efficiency gains from the Shenzhen Bay
transshipment hub that came fully online in March. Net new enterprise
customer additions were 47 against a target of 40, though average
contract value for new enterprise customers ($412,000 annualized) came in
below the $460,000 target, which the Sales section below discusses in
more detail. Free cash flow was $21.3 million, ahead of the $17 million
forecast, primarily due to favorable working capital timing that Finance
does not expect to fully repeat in Q3.

Revenue by Segment

Freight Forwarding: $98.7 million (53.6% of total revenue), up 9.2% YoY.
This remains our largest and most mature segment; growth is now primarily
volume-driven rather than pricing-driven, with average revenue per
shipment essentially flat YoY at $1,340.

Warehousing & Fulfillment: $52.1 million (28.3% of total revenue), up
22.8% YoY, the fastest-growing segment. This growth was driven by the
addition of two new fulfillment centers (Dongguan, opened February 2026,
and a expanded facility in Tuen Mun, capacity roughly doubled in April
2026) plus strong same-facility volume growth of 11% at existing
locations.

Customs Brokerage & Compliance: $33.4 million (18.1% of total revenue),
up 6.7% YoY, roughly in line with underlying trade volume growth in our
served corridors.

Regional Performance

Greater Bay Area (Hong Kong, Shenzhen, Guangzhou): $121.6 million
revenue, 66% of total, up 15.8% YoY — this remains our core market and
the primary beneficiary of the Shenzhen Bay hub coming online.

Southeast Asia (Singapore, Vietnam, Thailand hub operations): $41.2
million, 22.4% of total, up 12.1% YoY, roughly matching plan.

North America (freight forwarding origination only, no owned warehousing
yet): $21.4 million, 11.6% of total, up 8.9% YoY, slightly below the
10.5% growth target Finance had modeled for this segment, attributed in
the Sales commentary below to a slower-than-expected ramp on two large
accounts signed in Q1.

Key Operational Metrics

On-time delivery rate: 94.7%, up from 93.1% in Q1, driven by the Shenzhen
Bay hub reducing average cross-border transshipment time from 2.8 days to
1.9 days for the roughly 60% of Greater Bay Area freight volume now
routed through it.

Warehouse capacity utilization: 81.3% blended across all facilities
(76% at legacy facilities, 88% at the two new/expanded facilities,
reflecting strong initial demand outpacing our own conservative ramp
assumptions for the new capacity).

Customer churn (enterprise accounts, annualized): 7.2%, down from 8.9% in
Q1 and below our internal target ceiling of 9%. Finance attributes part
of this improvement to the on-time delivery gains above, though the
correlation has not been formally tested and Sales has flagged this as a
hypothesis rather than a confirmed causal driver.

Sales and Go-to-Market Commentary

New enterprise customer additions (47, against a target of 40) reflect
strong top-of-funnel performance, particularly in the Warehousing &
Fulfillment segment where the new Dongguan facility became a concrete
selling point in 26 of the 47 new deals. However, average annualized
contract value for these new customers ($412,000) fell short of the
$460,000 target; the Sales leadership's assessment is that the sales team,
incentivized partly on logo count this quarter as part of a deliberate
push to build density around the new Greater Bay Area capacity,
prioritized deal volume over deal size, and several of the 47 new
accounts are smaller pilot engagements expected to expand over the
following 2-3 quarters rather than land-and-expand-in-quarter deals. Sales
leadership has requested that Q3 incentive structure shift back toward a
blended logo-count-and-ACV metric to rebalance this.

Cost and Margin Discussion

Adjusted EBITDA margin improvement (16.9% to 18.6% quarter-over-quarter)
was driven primarily by the Shenzhen Bay hub, which Finance estimates
contributed approximately 1.3 percentage points of the 1.7-point margin
improvement through reduced transshipment costs and reduced third-party
customs clearance fees now handled in-house at the hub. The remaining 0.4
points came from general operating leverage as revenue grew faster than
fixed overhead. Fuel and transport costs, which had been a headwind in
2025, were roughly flat quarter-over-quarter as a percentage of freight
forwarding revenue (unchanged at approximately 31%), suggesting the prior
year's cost pressure from fuel price volatility has stabilized, though
Finance is not forecasting further improvement here without a structural
change (e.g., fleet electrification, currently under separate evaluation
by Operations and not expected to materially affect costs before 2028).

Risks and Watch Items for Q3

1. North America ramp: the two large Q1 accounts behind the segment's
growth shortfall are expected to reach full contracted volume by
September; if this slips further, North America growth could miss its
full-year target.
2. Warehouse utilization at new facilities (88%) is approaching a level
where Operations has flagged potential service-quality risk if it
continues climbing without additional capacity planning; a capacity
expansion review is scheduled for August.
3. Average new-enterprise contract value: tracked above as a Sales watch
item; Q3 incentive rebalancing is the proposed mitigation.
4. Currency: approximately 34% of revenue is denominated in currencies
other than USD (primarily HKD and CNY); Finance's Q2 results benefited
from modest favorable currency movement (~0.6 points of the 14.1% YoY
revenue growth) that is not assumed to repeat in Q3 guidance.

Q3 2026 Guidance

Revenue: $192-198 million (implies 4.2-7.5% QoQ growth).
Adjusted EBITDA margin: 18-19%, roughly flat to slightly up versus Q2.
Free cash flow: $14-17 million, reflecting the expectation that Q2's
favorable working capital timing normalizes in Q3.

Headcount and Organizational Update

Total headcount at quarter end was 2,340, up from 2,190 at the end of Q1,
with the majority of net additions (approximately 110 of the 150 net new
hires) in Warehousing & Fulfillment operations roles supporting the two
new facilities. Corporate/G&A headcount grew by only 12 net, reflecting
continued discipline on overhead growth relative to revenue growth — G&A
as a percentage of revenue improved to 8.1% from 8.6% in Q1. The Board
previously approved a Q2 hiring plan ceiling of 165 net additions; actual
net additions of 150 came in under that ceiling, which Finance attributes
to slightly slower-than-planned ramp at the Dongguan facility specifically
(hiring there is tied to a phased ramp schedule rather than a single
day-one headcount target).

Capital Expenditure

Q2 capex totaled $18.6 million, of which $11.2 million related to the
Tuen Mun facility expansion (now complete) and $4.8 million to continued
build-out at the Shenzhen Bay hub's second phase, expected to add
automated sorting capacity in Q4 2026. The remaining $2.6 million was
routine fleet and IT infrastructure spend. Full-year 2026 capex guidance
remains unchanged at $70-78 million, with Q2's spend tracking toward the
lower half of that range given the Tuen Mun project completing slightly
under its original $12.5 million budget.

Customer Concentration

Our top 10 customers by revenue represented 31% of total Q2 revenue,
essentially unchanged from 32% in Q1, and no single customer exceeded 6%
of total revenue. The Board has previously flagged customer concentration
as a standing risk item to monitor; management's assessment is that the
current level remains within an acceptable range and is not raising it as
an active concern this quarter, though it will continue to be tracked
each quarter as a standard watch item alongside the four items listed
above under Risks and Watch Items.


What drove the improvement in adjusted EBITDA margin from Q1 to Q2, and why did average annualized contract value for new enterprise customers fall short of target despite exceeding the new-customer-count target?

DESIGN DOC: Speculative Decoding Rollout for the Batched Serving Path
Author: Wei-Ling Tsang, Model Serving Team
Reviewers: Devraj Malhotra, Priya Ramaswamy, Cheuk-Yin Lam
Status: Approved, implementation in progress
Date: 2026-05-30

1. Background

Our production serving path for the canto-32b-instruct model currently
uses standard autoregressive decoding within a continuous-batching engine,
achieving a median inter-token latency of 38ms and a P99 of 71ms under
normal load (measured on our A100 fleet, 8-way tensor parallel). Token
generation throughput per GPU is the primary cost driver for the serving
fleet, currently running at approximately 2,300 GPUs across three regions
to meet demand. Speculative decoding — using a smaller draft model to
propose multiple tokens per step, verified in parallel by the full model —
is a well-established technique for improving decode throughput without
changing output distribution (when implemented correctly with rejection
sampling), and this document proposes a rollout plan for adopting it on
the batched serving path.

2. Goals

- Reduce median inter-token latency by at least 30% for canto-32b-instruct
  under representative production traffic, without any change to output
  quality or the model's sampling distribution.
- Reduce per-token serving cost (GPU-seconds per generated token) by at
  least 20%, freeing capacity for projected H2 traffic growth without a
  proportional GPU fleet expansion.
- Preserve exact-match output distribution parity with standard decoding
  under greedy and temperature-sampled generation, verified via the
  rejection-sampling correctness proof and empirical distribution testing
  described in section 6.

3. Draft Model Selection

We evaluated three candidate draft models: canto-1b-chat (a distilled
version of our chat model family), a from-scratch 350M-parameter draft
model trained specifically for speculative decoding on canto-32b's output
distribution, and an n-gram-based draft model (no neural component,
using retrieval from a static corpus). Acceptance rate — the fraction of
draft-proposed tokens accepted by the verifier — was measured on a
10,000-prompt sample drawn from production traffic logs (with PII
scrubbed per standard data handling policy):

canto-1b-chat: 71% average acceptance rate, draft model itself adds
roughly 1.8ms/token generation overhead.
350M from-scratch draft: 68% average acceptance rate, 1.1ms/token
overhead (smaller model, faster per-step).
n-gram retrieval: 34% average acceptance rate, negligible per-token
overhead but far lower acceptance limits overall speedup.

We selected the 350M from-scratch draft model as the best tradeoff:
despite a slightly lower acceptance rate than canto-1b-chat, its lower
per-step overhead produced a better net speedup in our simulation (see
section 5), and its smaller memory footprint (700MB in fp16 versus
canto-1b-chat's 2.2GB) leaves more headroom for KV cache within the same
GPU memory budget, which matters directly for our continuous-batching
engine's achievable batch size.

4. Verification and Rejection Sampling

For each batch step, the draft model proposes K tokens (K=4, chosen based
on the throughput-vs-verification-cost sweep in section 5) autoregressively,
then the full canto-32b-instruct model verifies all K proposed tokens in a
single forward pass (parallelizable since verification, unlike drafting,
does not require sequential dependency at generation time — the full
model scores all K positions simultaneously against the actual model
distribution). Rejection sampling is applied per the standard algorithm:
for each position, we accept the draft token with probability
min(1, p_target(token)/p_draft(token)), and on the first rejection at
position i, we resample from the residual distribution
max(0, p_target - p_draft) renormalized, discard the remaining proposed
tokens after position i, and continue drafting from that resampled token.
This guarantees the output distribution exactly matches standard
autoregressive sampling from canto-32b-instruct, which is the correctness
property required by Goal 3.

5. Performance Simulation and K Selection

We swept K (draft tokens proposed per step) from 2 to 8 in an offline
simulation using the measured 68% acceptance rate and profiled per-step
timings for both draft and verify passes. Expected speedup peaked at K=4
(estimated 1.6x throughput improvement, translating to the >30% latency
reduction goal) and began declining above K=6, since higher K increases
the chance of an early rejection wasting the remaining drafted tokens
without proportionally increasing accepted-tokens-per-step given the 68%
per-token acceptance rate (0.68^6 is only about 10%, meaning at K=6 there
is only roughly a 10% chance all six proposed tokens are accepted in a
single step, versus 0.68^4 at about 21% for K=4). K=4 was selected as the
production default, with the value exposed as a per-deployment tunable in
case future draft model improvements shift the optimal point.

6. Distribution Parity Testing

Beyond the theoretical rejection-sampling proof, we ran an empirical
validation: generating 50,000 completions from an identical prompt set
under both standard decoding and speculative decoding (temperature 0.8),
then comparing the resulting token distributions using a chi-squared
goodness-of-fit test at the aggregate vocabulary level. Results showed no
statistically significant distributional difference (p=0.34, well above
our pre-registered significance threshold of 0.01 for flagging a
concern), which is consistent with the theoretical guarantee and gives us
empirical confidence the implementation is correct, not just the
algorithm on paper.

7. Integration with Continuous Batching

The trickiest engineering challenge is integrating variable-length
accepted-token counts (anywhere from 0 to K tokens accepted per step,
per-sequence, within a single batch) into the continuous-batching engine's
existing fixed-shape batch step assumption. We extend the engine's step
function to track a per-sequence "tokens accepted this step" count and
adjust each sequence's position in the KV cache accordingly before the
next batch step is assembled; sequences that had fewer tokens accepted
free up their now-unneeded draft-token KV cache slots immediately, which
required a moderate refactor of the KV cache allocator's slot-recycling
logic (tracked as a separate implementation ticket, SERV-892, completed
2026-05-22 ahead of this design doc's approval).

8. Rollout Plan

Phase 1: shadow deployment on 5% of canto-32b-instruct traffic in
us-east-1 only, comparing latency and correctness metrics against the
non-speculative path serving the remaining 95%, for two weeks.
Phase 2: expand to 50% of us-east-1 traffic for one week if Phase 1 shows
no correctness regressions and at least a 25% latency improvement.
Phase 3: full rollout across all regions, with the non-speculative path
kept available behind a feature flag for at least one quarter as a
fallback.

9. Risks

The primary risk is draft-model staleness: if canto-32b-instruct is
updated (fine-tuned or otherwise changed) without a corresponding draft
model update, acceptance rate could degrade silently, reducing speedup
without breaking correctness (rejection sampling still guarantees correct
output distribution even against a "wrong" draft model — it just becomes
less efficient). We are adding a dashboard alert if observed acceptance
rate drops more than 10 percentage points below the 68% baseline over any
rolling 1-hour window, which would indicate the draft model needs
retraining against the current target model.


10. Cost Model and Expected Savings

Combining the K=4 acceptance-rate-derived throughput improvement with
measured GPU-second costs, Wei-Ling's team modeled expected fleet savings
at steady state. At the current fleet size of roughly 2,300 GPUs serving
canto-32b-instruct, a sustained 1.6x throughput improvement would allow
the same traffic to be served by approximately 1,440 GPUs, a reduction of
roughly 860 GPUs, or about $2.1 million/month in infrastructure cost at
current per-GPU-hour rates, before accounting for the added cost of
running the 350M draft model itself (estimated at under 3% of the total
GPU-hour budget given its small size relative to the 32B target model).
Realistically, the team does not expect to reduce fleet size by the full
860 GPUs immediately; the more likely near-term outcome is that the freed
capacity absorbs H2 traffic growth (independently forecast by the
capacity planning team at roughly 35% growth through year end) without a
proportional fleet expansion, meaning the savings will show up primarily
as avoided future spend rather than an immediate fleet reduction.

11. Monitoring and Rollback Criteria

Beyond the acceptance-rate alert described in section 9, the rollout will
be monitored against three rollback triggers during Phases 1 and 2: (a)
any statistically significant divergence in output distribution detected
by a continuously-running chi-squared test against a sampled traffic
subset, thresholded at the same p<0.01 level used in section 6; (b) P99
latency regression versus the non-speculative path (which would indicate
a bug in the batching integration rather than the expected improvement);
and (c) GPU memory pressure alerts from the KV cache allocator, given the
allocator refactor in SERV-892 introduced new slot-recycling logic that,
while tested, has not yet run against full production traffic patterns.
Any of the three triggers during Phase 1 or Phase 2 will pause the
rollout and revert affected traffic to the non-speculative path
automatically via the existing feature-flag infrastructure, with a
required incident write-up before the rollout can resume.


What draft model was selected for speculative decoding, what was its measured acceptance rate, and what value of K (draft tokens proposed per step) was chosen as the production default and why?

MODEL EVALUATION WORKING GROUP — MINUTES
Date: 2026-06-25, 3:00-4:00 PM PT
Location: Zoom (recording available internally, link in calendar invite)
Attendees: Cheuk-Yin Lam (chair), Dorothy Mensah, Rafael Ibarra,
Ines Wu (Product, guest), Sofia Andrade (Design, guest), notes by
Rafael Ibarra
Regrets: Devraj Malhotra (conflicting deploy window)

1. Review of May evaluation suite results

Cheuk-Yin presented results from the May run of the full evaluation
suite against canto-32b-instruct's latest checkpoint (trained through
2026-05-18). Aggregate score on the internal Cantonese comprehension
benchmark (420 held-out items spanning reading comprehension, tone-aware
transcription correction, and colloquial-to-formal register conversion)
was 78.4%, up from 74.1% on the prior checkpoint from March. The largest
single-category gain was in register conversion (61% to 71%), which
Cheuk-Yin attributed to the additional 40 hours of crowd-sourced register-
paired data added to the May training run. Tone-aware transcription
correction improved only marginally (82% to 83.5%), which the group
discussed at length below.

2. Discussion: plateau in tone-correction scores

Dorothy raised concern that tone-aware transcription correction has now
shown less than 2 points of improvement across the last three checkpoint
evaluations (79%, 82%, 83.5%), despite continued increases in overall
training data volume, suggesting this specific capability may be hitting
a data-composition ceiling rather than a data-volume ceiling. She
referenced the tokenizer vocabulary research (Cheuk-Yin's own prior
writeup) as a possible contributing factor, since tone-adjacent character
confusions were flagged there as the dominant error type even in a
dedicated ASR fine-tuning setting, and speculated the same underlying
tone-representation weakness could be limiting gains here too. Rafael
suggested a targeted experiment: constructing a training data slice
specifically balanced across tone-confusion-prone character pairs
(identified from the error analysis in the March eval run) rather than
relying on organic data collection to surface enough examples of each
confusion pair. Cheuk-Yin agreed this was worth trying and volunteered to
scope the data requirements.

Action: Cheuk-Yin to scope a tone-confusion-balanced training slice and
estimate data collection effort, report back by 2026-07-09 (next working
group meeting).

3. Product perspective on evaluation priorities (Ines, guest)

Ines was invited to share Product's view on which evaluation categories
matter most for the upcoming Voice Practice Mode launch (see the PRD,
target internal dogfood pending Speech team's forced-alignment component).
She noted that tone-aware transcription correction is the single most
directly relevant capability to that launch, since the scoring pipeline
depends on accurately identifying where a user's tone diverged from the
target. Given this, she asked whether the working group could prioritize
the tone-confusion data slice work discussed in item 2 ahead of other
planned evaluation-suite expansions (specifically, a planned expansion
into multi-turn dialogue coherence scoring, previously scheduled to start
in July). Cheuk-Yin was hesitant to fully deprioritize the dialogue
coherence work, noting it has its own stakeholder (the conversational
mode effort tentatively planned for Q1 2027) but agreed the tone-confusion
work could be pulled forward to run in parallel rather than sequentially,
given it's a smaller-scoped effort. Group consensus: proceed with both in
parallel, revisit prioritization if either turns out to need more
resourcing than currently estimated.

4. Design's input on error presentation (Sofia, guest)

Sofia raised a question tangential to the evaluation methodology itself:
whether the working group's error taxonomy (currently: substitution,
deletion, insertion, plus the tone-vs-segmental sub-classification used
in the ASR research) maps cleanly onto the kind of feedback Voice
Practice Mode needs to show users (syllable-level initial/final accuracy
plus tone accuracy, per the PRD). Cheuk-Yin confirmed the existing
taxonomy's tone-vs-segmental split does map reasonably well onto that
distinction, though the working group's taxonomy operates on written text
correction rather than live speech scoring, so some translation work
between the two systems will be needed by Devraj's team regardless.
No action item; noted as a dependency to flag in Devraj's implementation
work.

5. Evaluation suite infrastructure: flakiness in the register-conversion
category

Dorothy reported that the register-conversion evaluation category has
shown run-to-run score variance of up to 3 points on an unchanged model
checkpoint (re-running the same 420-item suite against the same
checkpoint twice in the same week produced 71% and 68%), which is large
enough to be mistaken for a real regression if not accounted for.
Investigation traced this to nondeterminism in the sampling-based scoring
step for this category specifically (unlike the other categories, which
use exact-match or rule-based scoring, register conversion uses an
LLM-as-judge approach with temperature 0.3 sampling for the judge model
itself). Group agreed to fix the judge model's sampling seed for future
runs to eliminate this specific source of variance, while noting this
only fixes run-to-run reproducibility, not judge accuracy itself, which
remains a separate open question.

Action: Dorothy to fix the judge-model seed in the eval harness config
and re-run a reproducibility check (same checkpoint, 3 runs, confirm
score variance drops near zero), due 2026-07-02.

6. Benchmark contamination check (Rafael)

Rafael presented findings from a contamination audit he ran independently
over the past two weeks, checking whether any of the 420 held-out
benchmark items had leaked into training data given the increasing use of
crowd-sourced and crawled text sources mentioned in item 1. Using n-gram
overlap detection (13-gram exact match threshold, consistent with the

pipeline), he found 6 of the 420 items had a near-exact match (11+ of 13

credited with the register-conversion score jump in item 1. This raised

register-conversion improvement was contamination rather than genuine
capability gain.

Cheuk-Yin re-ran the register-conversion category excluding the 6
flagged items and found the score dropped from 71% to 69.6%, a smaller
effect than the group had initially worried about, suggesting
contamination explains only a modest fraction of the observed gain
rather than most of it. Group agreed this was still worth fixing
properly: the 6 overlapping items will be removed from the crowd-sourced
training batch's future reuse, and a contamination check will be added as
a standard pre-training-run step going forward rather than an ad hoc
audit. Dorothy noted this should also apply retroactively to the tone-
confusion-balanced slice being scoped in item 2, so that work doesn't
introduce the same problem from the outset.

Action: Rafael to add automated 13-gram contamination checking as a
required step in the training data pipeline before any future training
run, coordinating with the Data Platform team on implementation, due
2026-07-16. Cheuk-Yin to apply the same check retroactively to the
tone-confusion slice scoping work from item 2.

7. Next steps

be looped back in once the tone-confusion work has concrete results
