POSTMORTEM: GPU Fleet Thermal Throttling Incident
Severity: SEV-2
Duration: 3 hours 12 minutes (degraded performance, no full outage)
Date of incident: 2026-06-28
Author: Oscar Nwachukwu, SRE
Status: Final, reviewed 2026-07-05

Summary

On June 28, 2026, between 13:05 and 16:17 UTC, approximately 340 GPUs
(14.8% of the us-west-2 serving fleet) entered sustained thermal
throttling, reducing their effective compute throughput by an average of
38%. This degraded canto-32b-instruct P99 latency in us-west-2 from a
normal baseline of 71ms to a peak observed value of 189ms during the
incident window. No requests were dropped or errored; the continuous-
batching engine's existing backpressure mechanism correctly shed excess
load onto other regions, meaning the customer-visible impact was elevated
latency for us-west-2-routed traffic rather than failures, and overall
global capacity absorbed the shortfall without breaching any SLA. This is
classified SEV-2 (degraded service, no outage) rather than SEV-1.

Background

The us-west-2 data center's affected row (Row 14, racks 14-22) had its
cooling infrastructure serviced two days prior, on June 26, as part of a
previously scheduled quarterly maintenance window covering coolant loop
inspection and filter replacement across four rows. This maintenance is
performed by the data center's facilities contractor (Meridian Facilities
Group) under a standing service agreement, with our own infrastructure
team present to validate post-maintenance readings before the row is
brought back to full load.

Timeline (all times UTC)

June 26, 09:00-14:30 — Scheduled coolant loop maintenance performed on
Row 14 by Meridian Facilities Group. Post-maintenance temperature and
flow-rate readings taken by Meridian's technician were logged as within
normal range, and our on-site infrastructure liaison (Dan Ostrowski)
signed off on returning the row to production load based on those
readings.

June 26, 14:30 - June 28, 13:00 — Row 14 operating normally under
production load, no anomalies observed in standard monitoring (rack-level
inlet temperature sensors, sampled every 5 minutes).

June 28, 13:05 — GPU-level thermal sensors (sampled every 30 seconds, a
finer granularity than the rack-level inlet sensors) begin showing
elevated readings on racks 14-17 specifically, not the full Row 14. First
individual GPUs cross the 83°C throttling threshold and begin reducing
clock speed automatically per the standard hardware protection firmware
behavior.

June 28, 13:11 — Automated alert fires for elevated GPU temperature
readings exceeding threshold on more than 20 GPUs simultaneously; on-call
Oscar Nwachukwu acknowledges within 4 minutes.

June 28, 13:19 — Oscar confirms the pattern is isolated to racks 14-17
within Row 14 (not the full row, and not other rows), and rules out a
software-driven cause (e.g., a workload change) by confirming traffic
patterns and batch sizes were within normal variance for the time of day.

June 28, 13:32 — Oscar escalates to Dan Ostrowski (infrastructure
liaison) given the temporal correlation with the June 26 maintenance,
and requests Meridian re-inspect racks 14-17 specifically.

June 28, 14:05 — Meridian technician arrives on-site (54-minute response
time, within their contracted 60-minute SLA for this facility) and
identifies that a coolant loop valve serving racks 14-17 specifically had
been only partially reopened following the June 26 maintenance — flow
rate to those four racks was measured at approximately 60% of normal,
sufficient to avoid triggering the rack-level inlet sensors (which
measure air temperature, not coolant flow directly, and are positioned
such that airflow mixing from adjacent, unaffected racks partially masked
the degradation) but insufficient to fully dissipate heat under sustained
production load over the intervening 46 hours.

June 28, 14:22 — Valve fully reopened by Meridian technician; coolant
flow rate confirmed back to 100% of normal within 8 minutes via direct
flow-rate measurement.

June 28, 14:30-16:17 — GPU temperatures gradually normalize as thermal
mass in the affected racks cools down; throttling clears GPU-by-GPU as
each crosses back below the 83°C threshold with sufficient margin for the
firmware to restore full clock speed. Full clearance across all 340
affected GPUs confirmed at 16:17.

Root Cause

A coolant loop valve serving racks 14-17 was not fully reopened following
the scheduled June 26 maintenance. The partial closure (approximately 40%
restriction) was not caught by Meridian's post-maintenance readings,
which — per their own after-action report shared with us — were taken at
a measurement point upstream of the specific valve in question and
therefore did not reflect the actual flow rate delivered to those four
racks. Our own rack-level inlet temperature sensors, while they did
eventually show slightly elevated readings in retrospective analysis
(approximately 2-3°C above the racks' typical baseline during the 46-hour
window before throttling began), did not cross any alerting threshold
during that window, since the elevation was within normal day-to-day
variance until cumulative thermal buildup under sustained load pushed
individual GPU junction temperatures over the hardware's own 83°C
throttling threshold.

Contributing Factors

1. Meridian's post-maintenance verification measured flow rate at a point
that did not isolate the four affected racks individually, creating a
blind spot for exactly this failure mode (a valve serving a subset of a
row being incompletely reopened while the row-level aggregate reading
looks acceptable).
2. Our own monitoring relies on rack-level inlet air temperature as the
leading indicator, with GPU-level junction temperature (the more direct
and sensitive signal) used only for the hardware's own automatic
throttling response, not as a proactive alerting input at the
infrastructure-monitoring layer. Had GPU-level temperature trends been
fed into a proactive alert (e.g., "gradual upward trend over 24+ hours"
rather than only a hard threshold crossing), this likely would have been
caught well before throttling began.

Impact

340 GPUs (14.8% of us-west-2 fleet) throttled for up to 3 hours 12
minutes; us-west-2 P99 latency peaked at 189ms (versus 71ms baseline); no
dropped or errored requests; no SLA breach, since the backpressure
mechanism successfully redistributed load to other regions. Estimated
additional cross-region data transfer cost from the load redistribution:
approximately $1,200, immaterial.

Action Items

1. Request Meridian Facilities Group revise their post-maintenance
verification procedure to measure flow rate at each individually-valved
rack group, not only at the row level. Owner: Dan Ostrowski. Due:
2026-07-15. Priority: P1.
2. Add GPU-level junction temperature trend monitoring (24-hour rolling
window, alert on sustained upward drift exceeding 5°C even without
crossing the hard throttling threshold) as a new proactive alert. Owner:
Oscar Nwachukwu. Due: 2026-07-20. Priority: P0, since this is the more
generally applicable fix and would catch a broader class of gradual
cooling degradation, not just this specific valve failure mode.
3. Audit all data centers under Meridian's service agreement for similar
valve-topology blind spots in their standard post-maintenance
verification procedure. Owner: Dan Ostrowski, in coordination with
Meridian account management. Due: 2026-08-01. Priority: P1.

Lessons Learned

This incident is a reminder that the continuous-batching engine's
backpressure and cross-region load-shedding worked exactly as intended,
converting what could have been a regional latency SLA breach into a
fully absorbed, customer-invisible event — this is called out explicitly
as a positive finding, not just a gap analysis. The gap that does need
closing is proactive, trend-based hardware health monitoring rather than
threshold-only alerting, which is a pattern the review board noted may
apply to other hardware health signals beyond GPU temperature (e.g.,
power delivery stability) and recommended as a broader review topic for
Q3, tracked separately from this incident's specific action items.


What was the root cause of the thermal throttling, why didn't the rack-level inlet temperature sensors catch it earlier, and what two action items address the monitoring gap versus the facilities-process gap respectively?

PRODUCT REQUIREMENTS DOCUMENT: Multi-Tenant Rate Limiting for the
Inference Gateway
Owner: Devraj Malhotra, Engineering
Product sponsor: Ines Wu
Status: Approved for Q3 build
Last updated: 2026-06-02

1. Problem Statement

The inference gateway currently enforces rate limits using a single
global token bucket per API key, configured at account creation and
rarely revisited. This has caused two recurring problems documented in
support tickets over the past quarter: first, enterprise customers running
multiple distinct internal applications against a single API key (common
because provisioning separate keys per internal app requires a manual
request to our support team, which many customers don't bother with) have
no way to prevent one noisy internal application from exhausting the
shared rate limit and starving their other applications — this was cited
directly in 14 of the 61 enterprise support tickets tagged "rate limit"
in Q2. Second, our current limits are static per pricing tier (see the
API documentation's Rate Limits section) with no ability for an account
to temporarily burst above their tier limit for a legitimate, predictable
event (e.g., a customer's own product launch), forcing them either to
upgrade tier prematurely or to contact support for a manual, engineer-
applied temporary override, which happened 23 times in Q2 and averaged
6.4 hours turnaround per request.

2. Goals

- Allow enterprise-tier accounts to create sub-keys (scoped API keys tied
  to a parent account) with independently configurable rate limits that
  sum to no more than the parent account's total limit, addressing the
  noisy-neighbor problem.
- Provide a self-service burst mechanism allowing any account to request
  a temporary rate limit increase (up to 3x their tier baseline, for up
  to 48 hours) without engineer involvement, reducing the 6.4-hour
  average manual turnaround to near-zero for requests within these
  bounds.
- Maintain accurate, real-time rate limit enforcement across our globally
  distributed gateway fleet (12 edge regions) with no more than a 2%
  margin of error versus the configured limit, to avoid either
  under-enforcing (revenue/cost risk) or over-enforcing (customer-facing
  false 429s).

3. Non-Goals

- This project does not change the underlying tier-based default limits
  themselves (Free/Pro/Enterprise baselines documented in the API
  reference); it adds configurability on top of those defaults.
- Automatic, ML-driven dynamic rate limiting (e.g., automatically
  detecting and allowing "good" traffic spikes without any customer
  action) is out of scope for this release; the self-service burst
  mechanism requires an explicit customer action to request the burst.
- Sub-key support is Enterprise-tier only for this release; Pro-tier
  sub-keys are a candidate for a future release if enterprise adoption
  validates demand, but are explicitly not being built now.

4. Functional Requirements

4.1 Sub-key creation: Enterprise account admins can create up to 20
sub-keys per parent account via the developer console, each with its own
requests/minute, tokens/minute, and max-concurrency limits. The console
enforces that the sum of all sub-key limits (plus any traffic on the
parent key itself, which remains usable directly) cannot exceed the
parent account's total contracted limit; attempting to exceed this shows
a validation error before the sub-key can be saved.

4.2 Sub-key usage attribution: usage dashboards (both the customer-facing
console and our internal billing pipeline) must break down consumption
per sub-key as well as in aggregate, so customers can see which internal
application is consuming what share of their total quota.

4.3 Self-service burst requests: any account (any tier) can request a
temporary burst via a new POST /v2/account/burst_request endpoint or the
console UI, specifying a desired multiplier (up to 3x baseline) and
duration (up to 48 hours). Requests are auto-approved if the requested
multiplier is under 2x; requests between 2x and 3x require a lightweight
automated risk check (comparing requested burst capacity against the
account's trailing 30-day peak usage and payment standing) and are
auto-approved if that check passes, or routed to a support queue with a
target 2-hour turnaround if it does not.

4.4 Burst request rate limiting (meta): to prevent abuse of the burst
mechanism itself, accounts are limited to 4 burst requests per rolling
30-day period; a 5th request in that window is auto-rejected with
guidance to contact support for a manual review, which also serves as a
signal for the account team to have a proactive conversation about
whether the account should upgrade tier.

5. Non-Functional Requirements

- Rate limit state must be enforced consistently across all 12 edge
  regions; a sub-key's limit is a single global quota, not a per-region
  quota (customers should not be able to effectively multiply their
  quota by distributing requests across regions). This requires a
  low-latency, globally-consistent counter, which the team plans to
  implement via a sharded, regionally-replicated counting service with
  bounded staleness (target: no shard's view of global usage is more
  than 250ms stale under normal network conditions).
- The 2% margin-of-error goal in section 2 is measured against this
  bounded-staleness design: because usage is only eventually consistent
  across regions within the 250ms window, a burst of requests arriving
  simultaneously across multiple regions could transiently exceed the
  configured limit before the counters converge. Engineering's analysis
  estimates this transient overage is bounded by the request rate
  achievable within one 250ms window, which for the largest anticipated
  enterprise limits (1,500 req/min baseline, up to 4,500 req/min at 3x
  burst) works out to a worst-case overage of roughly 19 requests, well
  under the 2% margin when measured against a full minute's quota.

6. Success Metrics

Primary: reduction in "rate limit" tagged enterprise support tickets,
target -50% quarter-over-quarter following full rollout. Secondary:
reduction in manual engineer-applied override turnaround time, target
average under 30 minutes for cases that do reach the support queue (down
from 6.4 hours), since even queued cases should be faster given the
automated risk-check pre-screening. Guardrail: no increase in successful
abuse of rate limits (measured via the existing abuse-detection pipeline,
which flags accounts with usage patterns inconsistent with their stated
use case) attributable to the new self-service burst mechanism.

7. Rollout Plan

Phase 1 (weeks 1-3): sub-key creation and attribution dashboards, limited
beta with 8 existing enterprise customers who specifically requested this
capability in support tickets.
Phase 2 (weeks 4-5): self-service burst requests, same beta group.
Phase 3 (weeks 6-8): general availability for all enterprise accounts;
Pro/Free tier burst requests (which don't need sub-key support, only the
burst mechanism) also go GA in this phase since they don't depend on the
sub-key work.

8. Dependencies and Risks

The globally-consistent counting service (section 5) is a new piece of
shared infrastructure with its own project, owned by the Gateway
Infrastructure team; this PRD's Phase 1 timeline assumes that service is
available in staging by the start of week 1, which as of this writing
(2026-06-02) is tracking on schedule per that team's own status updates
but represents the single largest schedule risk to this project, since
sub-key attribution depends directly on it. Devraj has a fallback design
using the existing per-region counting mechanism (looser global
consistency, larger worst-case overage) that could ship Phase 1 without
the new counting service if it slips, at the cost of a wider error margin
than the 2% goal; this fallback would need explicit sign-off from Ines
given it misses a stated goal, and is not the default plan.


What is the maximum burst multiplier and duration a customer can self-service request, what distinguishes an auto-approved burst request from one routed to the support queue, and what is the target reduction in rate-limit-tagged support tickets?

A Comparative Study of Continuous Batching Schedulers Under Bursty Load
A Research Writeup
Authors: Rafael Ibarra, Wei-Ling Tsang
Draft v2, 2026-06-14 (internal circulation)

Abstract

Continuous batching (also called dynamic batching with iteration-level
scheduling) is now the standard technique for serving autoregressive
language models efficiently, allowing new requests to join a batch
mid-generation rather than waiting for the entire batch to complete. This
study compares three scheduling policies for admitting new requests into
an in-flight batch — first-come-first-served (FCFS), shortest-remaining-
tokens-first (SRTF, using a predicted output length), and a hybrid
priority scheme that weights both wait time and predicted length — under
synthetic bursty traffic patterns designed to resemble our own production
traffic's diurnal and event-driven spikes. We find the hybrid scheme
reduces P99 time-to-first-token (TTFT) by 41% versus FCFS under our
burstiest tested traffic pattern, at a cost of only a 6% increase in
median TTFT, and recommend it as a replacement for our current FCFS
scheduler pending the production validation described in section 7.

1. Motivation

Our production serving fleet uses FCFS admission into the continuous
batch: new requests join the next available batch slot in strict arrival
order. This is simple and fair in a strict sense, but production traffic
logs show it produces poor tail latency specifically during burst events
(e.g., a marketing-triggered flash sale, or — as in the June 3 checkout
outage postmortem — any event driving a sudden multi-x traffic spike),
because a long request that happens to arrive just before a burst
occupies a batch slot for its full duration, delaying the first token of
every request that arrives during the burst regardless of how short those
later requests are. This motivates exploring scheduling policies that can
use predicted output length as a signal, at the cost of added scheduling
complexity and a dependency on length prediction accuracy.

2. Scheduling Policies Studied

FCFS (baseline): requests are admitted to available batch slots strictly
in arrival order, with no consideration of predicted length. This matches
our current production scheduler.

SRTF: requests are admitted in order of predicted remaining output length
(shortest first), using a length predictor (a lightweight classifier
trained on prompt features, achieving a mean absolute error of 34 tokens
against actual output length on our validation set, described further in
section 4). This is analogous to shortest-job-first scheduling in
classical OS scheduling theory, adapted to the continuous-batching
setting where "job length" must be predicted rather than known.

Hybrid priority: requests are assigned a priority score combining
normalized wait time and normalized predicted length,
priority = alpha * (wait_time / max_wait_time) + (1 - alpha) *
(1 - predicted_length / max_predicted_length), with alpha = 0.4 chosen
via the sweep described in section 5. This prevents the starvation risk
inherent to pure SRTF, where a continuous stream of short requests could
in principle delay a long request indefinitely, by ensuring wait time
alone eventually dominates the priority score for any sufficiently
long-waiting request.

3. Experimental Setup

We replayed three synthetic traffic patterns against a simulated
continuous-batching engine (calibrated against our production engine's
measured per-token compute time and batch-size-dependent throughput
curves, validated to within 4% of real engine measurements on a held-out
calibration trace): (a) steady-state, Poisson-arrival traffic at our
typical average rate, used as a control; (b) diurnal burst, modeling our
typical evening peak (2.5x average rate sustained for 3 hours); and (c)
flash-event burst, modeling a marketing-triggered spike (6x average rate
for 15 minutes, then rapid decay back to baseline over 10 minutes),
calibrated against actual traffic logs from the April 20 flash sale event
referenced in the June 3 postmortem. Request length distribution
(prompt and expected output tokens) was drawn from a resampled
distribution matching 30 days of production traffic logs, with PII
scrubbed per standard data handling policy.

4. Length Prediction

The length predictor used for SRTF and hybrid scheduling is a small
gradient-boosted tree model trained on prompt-level features (prompt
token count, presence of specific system-prompt templates known to
correlate with response length, and a small set of lexical features)
against actual output length as the training target, using 6 months of
production traffic as training data and a held-out final month for
validation. It achieves a mean absolute error of 34 tokens (against a
mean actual output length of 187 tokens in the validation set, so
roughly 18% relative error on average) and, critically for SRTF's
ordering behavior, a rank correlation (Spearman's rho) of 0.71 against
true output length — imperfect, but sufficient for the ordering-based
scheduling decisions this study relies on, since SRTF and the hybrid
scheme only need relative ordering to be mostly correct, not precise
length values.

5. Alpha Sweep for the Hybrid Scheme

We swept alpha (the wait-time weighting in the hybrid priority formula)
from 0.1 to 0.9 in increments of 0.1 against the flash-event burst
pattern, measuring both P99 TTFT and maximum observed wait time for any
single request (a proxy for worst-case starvation risk). P99 TTFT was
minimized around alpha=0.3-0.4, while maximum wait time increased sharply
for alpha below 0.3 (indicating starvation risk becomes material below
that point) and P99 TTFT began degrading back toward FCFS-like behavior
above alpha=0.5 (since higher alpha increasingly ignores the length
signal that gives SRTF-like policies their advantage). We selected
alpha=0.4 as the production candidate, favoring a small safety margin
above the raw P99-minimizing point (alpha=0.3) given the starvation-risk
tradeoff.

6. Results

Under steady-state traffic (control), all three policies performed
similarly: median TTFT within 3% of each other across policies, and P99
TTFT within 8%, confirming the scheduling policy mostly doesn't matter
absent bursts, as expected.

Under diurnal burst traffic: FCFS P99 TTFT was 1,240ms; SRTF reduced this
to 810ms (-35%); hybrid reduced this to 790ms (-36%), essentially matching
SRTF's benefit while avoiding its starvation risk (max wait time under
hybrid was 2.1 seconds versus SRTF's 6.8 seconds for the single
worst-case long request in this trace).

Under flash-event burst traffic (the burstier, more extreme pattern):
FCFS P99 TTFT was 3,180ms; SRTF reduced this to 1,650ms (-48%) but with a
max wait time of 14.2 seconds for the longest-waiting request, which we
consider unacceptable against our internal 10-second hard timeout
(requests would be dropped/errored, not merely delayed, at that point);
hybrid reduced P99 TTFT to 1,880ms (-41%) with a max wait time of 4.6
seconds, comfortably under the timeout. Median TTFT under hybrid
increased by 6% versus FCFS in this scenario (median short requests wait
slightly longer on average since some batch capacity is reserved for
longer-waiting requests via the wait-time term), which we judge an
acceptable tradeoff given the P99 and starvation-risk improvements.

7. Recommendation and Next Steps

We recommend replacing the production FCFS scheduler with the hybrid
priority scheme (alpha=0.4) based on these results, prioritizing it over
pure SRTF specifically because of the starvation risk SRTF showed under
the flash-event pattern, which is the traffic pattern most likely to
actually occur in production given precedent (the April 20 event and the
broader pattern of marketing-triggered spikes discussed elsewhere).
Before production rollout, we recommend a shadow-mode validation period
(scoring the hybrid scheduler's decisions against live production traffic
without actually changing admission order) to confirm the length
predictor's accuracy holds up on genuinely live traffic rather than only
the historical validation set, since production traffic composition may
have shifted since the 6-month training window. This is tracked as a
follow-up implementation project, not yet scheped as of this writeup.

8. Limitations

Our simulated engine, while calibrated to within 4% of production
measurements on the calibration trace, has not been validated against
truly out-of-distribution traffic patterns beyond the three studied here;
a genuinely novel burst shape (for example, a sustained multi-day spike
rather than the short bursts studied) could behave differently than our
simulation predicts. We also note the length predictor's 0.71 rank
correlation, while adequate for our purposes here, leaves room for
improvement, and a better predictor would likely improve SRTF and hybrid
results further without changing the qualitative conclusion that hybrid
outperforms both alternatives under bursty conditions.


Under the flash-event burst traffic pattern, why did the researchers reject SRTF despite its larger P99 TTFT improvement, and what alpha value did they select for the hybrid scheme and why did they not choose the value that minimized P99 TTFT most?

DATA RETENTION AND MODEL TRAINING DATA HANDLING POLICY
Document owner: Legal & Privacy, in partnership with Data Platform
Effective date: 2026-05-01
Supersedes: Data Handling Policy v1.4 (2025-02-01)
Approved by: Chief Privacy Officer, General Counsel, VP Engineering

1. Purpose

This policy governs how the company collects, retains, uses, and
deletes data used in training, fine-tuning, and evaluating our language
models, including API traffic data, crowd-sourced labeled data, and
internally generated synthetic data. It is designed to satisfy our
obligations under applicable privacy law (including GDPR for EU users and
the Hong Kong Personal Data (Privacy) Ordinance for users in Hong Kong)
and our public-facing data usage commitments described in the developer
portal terms of service.

2. Data Categories

This policy distinguishes four categories of data relevant to model
training: (a) API traffic data — prompts and completions passing through
the production inference API; (b) crowd-sourced labeled data — data
collected through paid annotation vendors or in-app feedback mechanisms
specifically for training or evaluation purposes; (c) internally
generated synthetic data — data generated by our own models for training
or evaluation of other models; and (d) third-party licensed data —
data licensed from external providers under specific contractual terms
that may impose additional restrictions beyond this policy.

3. API Traffic Data

3.1 Default handling: API traffic data is not used for model training or
fine-tuning by default. Customers on Free and Pro tiers may opt in to
data sharing (described as "help improve Canto" in the consumer-facing
product and as a console toggle for API customers) to allow their traffic
to be used for training; Enterprise tier customers' traffic is never used
for training regardless of any toggle state, per standing contractual
commitments made to enterprise customers as a baseline term.

3.2 Retention for opted-in traffic: traffic data from accounts that have
opted in is retained for up to 12 months from collection for potential
use in training runs, after which it is automatically deleted from the
training data lake (a separate, shorter retention period applies to
operational logs used for abuse detection and debugging, described in
Section 6, which is distinct from the training-data-lake retention
governed here).

3.3 Retention for non-opted-in traffic: traffic data from accounts that
have not opted in, or have explicitly opted out, is not copied into the
training data lake at all; it exists only in the operational logging
systems described in Section 6, subject to that shorter retention period
and never used for training.

3.4 Opt-out and deletion requests: any account can change their opt-in
status at any time via the console; changing from opted-in to opted-out
does not retroactively delete data already in the training data lake
from the period the account was opted in unless the account additionally
submits a data deletion request per Section 7, since opt-in status
governs future collection, not retroactive deletion. This distinction is
called out explicitly in the console UI when a customer changes their
opt-in toggle, to avoid the common misunderstanding that toggling off
opt-in status alone deletes prior data.

4. Crowd-Sourced Labeled Data

4.1 Vendor requirements: annotation vendors must be under a signed data
processing agreement (DPA) that flows down the relevant terms of this
policy, including deletion timelines and prohibition on the vendor's own
reuse of the data for purposes beyond the contracted annotation task.

4.2 PII handling: crowd-sourced data collection tasks must not solicit
personally identifiable information as part of the task design; where PII
appears incidentally in submitted data (for example, if a crowd worker
includes a real name in an example sentence), the standard PII-scrubbing
pipeline (automated named-entity detection plus a sampled manual review,
target 95% recall against a held-out PII-injected test set, reviewed
quarterly by the Privacy team) is applied before the data enters the
training data lake.

4.3 Retention: crowd-sourced labeled data is retained for up to 36 months
from collection, longer than API traffic data's 12-month default, given
its higher collection cost and the explicit consent obtained at
collection time (annotation vendor contracts specify the data usage
purpose and retention period directly to workers as part of the task
consent flow, unlike API traffic data's opt-in toggle model).

5. Internally Generated Synthetic Data

Synthetic data generated by our own models for training other models is
retained indefinitely by default, since it does not carry the same
per-individual privacy obligations as data derived from real user
interactions — however, any synthetic data generation process that uses
real user data as a seed or few-shot example (for example, generating
synthetic variations of a real, opted-in user prompt) inherits the
retention and deletion obligations of the underlying seed data, and must
be tagged accordingly in the training data lake's metadata so that a
deletion request against the seed data can propagate to derived synthetic
examples. This tagging requirement, added in this policy version (it was
not present in v1.4), was driven by a Q1 2026 internal audit that found
12% of a sampled synthetic dataset could be traced back to specific
opted-in user prompts without corresponding deletion-propagation
metadata, creating a compliance gap the audit flagged as needing
remediation before the next major synthetic data generation run.

6. Operational Logs (Distinct from Training Data Lake)

Operational logs — request/response pairs retained for abuse detection,
debugging, and billing accuracy, independent of any training use — are
retained for 90 days by default across all tiers and opt-in statuses,
then automatically deleted, except where retention is extended for an
active abuse investigation or legal hold, in which case retention
continues only for the specific records under investigation, not the
full 90-day cohort. This 90-day period is unrelated to and shorter than
the training-data-lake retention periods described above; a customer who
has opted out of training data use still has their traffic retained in
operational logs for 90 days for these operational purposes, which is
disclosed in the terms of service as a standard operational necessity
distinct from training data usage.

7. Data Deletion Requests

Any customer (or, where applicable under GDPR/PDPO, any individual whose
data may be present, such as an end user of a customer's product built on
our API) may submit a data deletion request via the privacy portal.
Requests are processed within 30 days per our standard commitment,
propagating deletion across the training data lake, any tagged derived
synthetic data (per Section 5's tagging requirement), and operational
logs if still within their retention window. Deletion requests do not
retroactively affect model weights already trained on the data prior to
the request — this policy governs data retention, not model unlearning,
which is tracked as a separate, harder problem outside this policy's
scope; the terms of service disclose this distinction to customers
explicitly.

8. Third-Party Licensed Data

Data licensed from external providers is governed primarily by the
specific licensing agreement for that dataset, which may impose
retention, deletion, or usage-scope terms stricter or different from this
policy's defaults. Data Platform maintains a registry of all licensed
datasets with their specific terms, reviewed by Legal at acquisition
time and re-reviewed annually or upon contract renewal, whichever comes
first. Where a licensing agreement's terms are silent on a specific point
this policy addresses (e.g., synthetic-data-tagging propagation), this
policy's defaults apply as a fallback.

9. Policy Review and Audit

This policy is subject to an internal compliance audit at least annually,
with the next scheduled audit for Q1 2027 to specifically re-check the
synthetic-data-tagging remediation described in Section 5 for
completeness. Material changes in applicable privacy law trigger an
off-cycle review; Legal is responsible for monitoring relevant regulatory
developments and initiating an off-cycle review when warranted.


How does the retention period for crowd-sourced labeled data compare to API traffic data and operational logs, and what compliance gap in the Q1 2026 audit led to the new synthetic-data-tagging requirement in this policy version?

APERTURE: DISTRIBUTED RATE LIMITER SERVICE
Design Document

Document ID: ENG-DD-2291
Status: Approved for Implementation
Authors: Priya Raman (Platform Infra), Tobias Klein (Edge Systems)
Reviewers: Marcus Oduya (SRE Lead), Sana Farouk (Payments Platform), Dev Chatterjee (Security)
Current Version: v1.3
Last Updated: 2026-05-29

1. SUMMARY

This document proposes Aperture, a distributed rate limiting service intended to
replace the per-process in-memory limiters currently embedded in each of our edge
proxies. The current approach cannot enforce a global limit across a fleet of
proxies, which means a client can exceed its contracted quota simply by spreading
requests across enough edge nodes. Aperture centralizes limit state in a small
cluster of coordinator nodes backed by a sharded Redis counter store, exposes a
gRPC decision API that edge proxies call on the hot path, and is designed to fail
open under coordinator unavailability so that a limiter outage never becomes a
full outage of the services it protects. This document covers the problem, the
requirements we gathered from the Payments, Search, and Notifications teams, the
proposed architecture, the consistency and failure-handling model, capacity
planning, and the rollout plan.

2. BACKGROUND AND PROBLEM STATEMENT

Every edge proxy in the fleet currently runs a local token-bucket limiter backed
by an in-process counter. This works acceptably for coarse per-IP throttling but
breaks down for any limit that is supposed to apply per API key, per tenant, or
per downstream partner, because those clients are not pinned to a single proxy.
During the March 2026 partner integration with Halcyon Logistics, we observed
that a single partner key configured for 500 requests per second was regularly
bursting to over 3,000 requests per second in aggregate, because the partner's
traffic was load-balanced across 14 proxies and each proxy's local bucket only
saw roughly 500/14 of the traffic and never tripped. This directly contributed to
a latency degradation in the Search service on 2026-03-11, tracked separately as
INC-4390.

We also heard from the Payments Platform team that they need a stricter
"fail-closed" mode for a small number of extremely sensitive endpoints (card
authorization retries) where it is better to reject a request than to risk an
uncontrolled retry storm hitting a downstream card network. That requirement
conflicts with the general fail-open design goal below and is called out
explicitly in Section 6 and Section 9.

A secondary motivation, raised independently by the Notifications team during
early requirements gathering in February 2026, is cost control rather than
correctness: their push-notification fan-out job can, under a bug, attempt to
resend the same notification to a device token thousands of times in a tight
loop, and each attempt costs real money against a third-party push gateway
contract billed per call. A per-key limit on that fan-out job's calls to the
push gateway, enforced centrally rather than per-worker-process, would have
capped the financial exposure of the January 2026 incident where a retry loop
in that job sent approximately 40,000 duplicate notifications for a single
campaign before an on-call engineer noticed and killed the job manually. This
is one of the three initial adopter use cases used to validate the
requirements in Section 5, alongside Search's partner-key enforcement and
Payments' card-auth-retry protection.

3. GOALS

- Provide a single source of truth for rate-limit counters that is consistent
  across the full edge proxy fleet, with a target staleness bound of under one
  second under normal operation.
- Support per-key, per-tenant, and per-route limit dimensions, composable in a
  single decision call.
- Default to fail-open behavior when the coordinator cluster is unreachable or
  overloaded, since an outage of the limiter must not become an outage of the
  protected service.
- Allow a small, explicitly configured allowlist of routes to instead fail
  closed, for cases like card authorization retries where an uncontrolled
  request storm is worse than a false rejection.
- Keep p99 decision latency under 10ms as measured from the edge proxy, since
  Aperture sits on the hot path of every proxied request.
- Support dynamic limit configuration changes without a proxy restart.

4. NON-GOALS

- Aperture is not a general-purpose distributed counter service; it is scoped to
  rate limiting decisions only, and other teams should not build unrelated
  counting features on top of it.
- We are not attempting exact global consistency (i.e., a hard guarantee that
  the aggregate limit is never exceeded by even one request). The sliding
  window algorithm in Section 8 gives a bounded overshoot, not a zero overshoot.
- We are not replacing the existing DDoS-mitigation layer at the CDN edge, which
  operates on a completely different (and much coarser) time scale.

5. REQUIREMENTS

R1. The system must support at least 50,000 distinct rate-limit keys active
    concurrently across the fleet.
R2. The system must support configuring a limit as a (count, window) pair, e.g.
    500 requests per 1 second, or 200,000 requests per 1 hour.
R3. Decision latency at p50 must be under 3ms and at p99 under 10ms, measured
    from the calling proxy.
R4. The system must survive the loss of any single coordinator node without a
    visible increase in decision latency beyond the p99 target.
R5. Configuration changes to limits must propagate to all proxies within 30
    seconds.
R6. The system must expose per-key current usage via an introspection API for
    debugging and for partner-facing usage dashboards.
R7. The system must support the fail-closed override list described in Goal 4,
    configurable per route.
R8. All decision calls must be logged with enough detail to reconstruct why a
    request was allowed or denied, for at least 7 days.
R9. The system must not become a single point of failure for the proxies that
    call it, consistent with the fail-open default.
R10. Coordinator nodes must be independently deployable from the Redis counter
     shards, so either layer can be scaled without touching the other.

6. ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW

Aperture has four components. The Edge Proxy Client is a small library linked
into each proxy that turns an incoming request into one or more rate-limit
checks and calls the Limiter Core over gRPC. The Limiter Core is a stateless
decision service, deployed as a fleet of at least 6 nodes across 3 availability
zones, that receives a check request, consults the Coordinator for the current
limit configuration (cached locally with a 30-second TTL, satisfying R5), reads
and increments the appropriate counter in the Redis Counter Store, and returns
an allow/deny decision. The Coordinator is a small Raft-backed cluster of 3
nodes that owns limit configuration (the mapping from key pattern to (count,
window) and fail-open/fail-closed mode) and pushes configuration updates to
Limiter Core nodes via a streaming gRPC subscription. The Redis Counter Store is
a set of 8 shards, each a Redis 7.2 instance with a hot standby, holding the
actual sliding-window counters keyed by rate-limit key.

The Edge Proxy Client is designed to fail open by default: if the Limiter Core
does not respond within a configurable client-side timeout, the client allows
the request and logs a "limiter-unavailable" event rather than blocking. The
default value of this client-side timeout, as originally specified in v1.0 of
this document, is 150ms, matched to the proxy's overall per-request budget so
that a limiter timeout does not push the whole request past its own SLA.

7. COMPONENT DEEP DIVE: LIMITER CORE ALGORITHM

The Limiter Core implements a sliding-window counter algorithm rather than a
strict token bucket, because a token bucket requires a single owner for bucket
state and does not shard cleanly across the 8 Redis shards. The sliding window
algorithm divides each configured window into 10 sub-buckets; a check
increments the current sub-bucket and sums the counts of all sub-buckets whose
timestamp falls within the trailing window, weighting the oldest partially
expired sub-bucket by the fraction of it still inside the window. This gives an
approximation of a true sliding window with bounded error, at a cost of 10x the
Redis operations of a fixed-window counter, which our capacity plan in Section
10 accounts for.

Each Limiter Core node holds a local, short-lived cache of the Coordinator's
current limit configuration, refreshed via the streaming subscription described
above and falling back to a 30-second poll if the stream is interrupted. This
cache is what lets a single Limiter Core node make correct decisions without a
Coordinator round trip on every request, which is essential for meeting the R3
latency target.

8. COMPONENT DEEP DIVE: COORDINATOR

The Coordinator cluster runs a Raft consensus protocol across 3 nodes to agree
on the current limit configuration. A configuration change (adding a new
rate-limit key pattern, changing a count/window pair, or moving a route between
fail-open and fail-closed mode) is proposed by an operator via the Aperture CLI,
committed through Raft, and then fanned out to all Limiter Core nodes over the
streaming subscription mentioned above. The Coordinator does not sit on the
per-request hot path; it is only consulted when a Limiter Core node's local
cache is cold or stale, which happens on startup or after a subscription
interruption longer than 30 seconds.

Leader election within the Coordinator cluster uses the standard Raft
leader-lease mechanism with a 5-second election timeout. Loss of the Coordinator
leader does not affect in-flight decision traffic at all, since Limiter Core
nodes continue to serve decisions from their local cache; it only delays the
propagation of new configuration changes until a new leader is elected, which
in testing takes under 2 seconds.

9. DATA MODEL

Each rate-limit key is a string composed of a key pattern id and a set of
dimension values, for example "partner-qps:halcyon-logistics" or
"card-auth-retry:tenant-4471". The Redis representation for a given key is a
hash containing one field per sub-bucket, named by sub-bucket index 0-9, holding
an integer count, plus a metadata field recording the window start timestamp.
Keys are sharded across the 8 Redis shards by a consistent hash of the key
string, and each shard's keys carry a TTL equal to twice the configured window,
so that idle keys are automatically reclaimed without a separate cleanup job.

10. API SPECIFICATION

The primary decision RPC is Check(CheckRequest) returns (CheckResponse).
CheckRequest carries a repeated field of RateLimitCheck messages, each with a
key, a key_pattern_id, and an optional cost (defaulting to 1, but settable
higher for expensive operations that should count as more than one unit against
the limit). CheckResponse carries a repeated field of RateLimitResult, each with
an allowed boolean, a current_count, a limit, a reset_after_ms, and a decision
source enum (COORDINATOR_FRESH, CACHE, or FAIL_OPEN) that lets the calling proxy
and our logging pipeline distinguish a real decision from a fail-open default.
A secondary introspection RPC, GetUsage(GetUsageRequest) returns (GetUsageResponse),
lets internal tools and the partner usage dashboard query current counts for a
key without incrementing them.

11. CONSISTENCY MODEL AND FAILURE MODES

Aperture provides eventually-consistent, best-effort global limits, not exact
ones. Because each Limiter Core node increments counters directly in the shared
Redis shards, concurrent increments from different Limiter Core nodes are
linearized by Redis itself for a single key, so there is no double-counting
within a shard. The approximation error comes entirely from the sliding-window
sub-bucket algorithm described in Section 7, which in the worst case allows a
burst of up to 10% over the configured limit right at a sub-bucket boundary.
This was deemed acceptable by the Payments team for all routes except the
card-auth-retry family, which is why that family is configured fail-closed and
additionally uses a tighter, single-sub-bucket window (see Appendix A).

Failure handling follows two tracks depending on the route's configured mode.
For fail-open routes (the default), if the Edge Proxy Client's call to Limiter
Core does not complete within the client-side timeout, currently defaulted to
150ms, the client allows the request. If a Limiter Core node itself cannot
reach its Redis shard (shard down, network partition), it also fails open for
that specific key, logs a "shard-unavailable" event, and continues serving
other keys normally. For fail-closed routes, the Edge Proxy Client instead
denies the request if the Limiter Core call fails or times out, and Limiter
Core denies the request if it cannot reach the relevant Redis shard. Because
fail-closed is only used for a small allowlist of extremely sensitive routes,
this asymmetric handling was accepted by all reviewers.

12. CAPACITY PLANNING

We sized the system against a target of 400,000 decision RPCs per second at
peak, based on current edge proxy traffic of roughly 350,000 requests per
second and an estimate of 1.15 rate-limit checks per request (some requests
check both a per-key and a per-tenant limit). At 10 Redis operations per check
(the sliding window sub-bucket fan-out from Section 7), that is 4.6 million
Redis operations per second across the 8 shards, or roughly 575,000 operations
per second per shard, which is comfortably within the ~1M ops/sec ceiling we
measured for a single Redis 7.2 instance on our standard r6g.2xlarge node class
in the September 2025 Redis capacity study. Limiter Core is sized at 6 nodes
across 3 zones, each handling roughly 67,000 decision RPCs per second at peak,
which our load testing (Appendix B) showed keeps p99 latency at 8ms, within the
10ms budget from R3, assuming the 150ms client timeout is not being hit
routinely; a higher rate of timeouts would itself be a signal to add capacity.

13. ROLLOUT PLAN

Phase 0 (2026-04-01 to 2026-04-14): Internal dogfood. Aperture deployed
alongside existing per-process limiters in shadow mode, decisions logged but not
enforced, across the internal tooling proxies only. During this phase we
compared Aperture's shadow decisions against the legacy limiter's real
decisions for every request and logged any divergence. Divergence rate started
at 6.2% on day one, mostly because the legacy limiter's per-process buckets
were, as expected, systematically more permissive for hot partner keys spread
across many proxies. By the end of Phase 0, after tuning the sub-bucket count
and fixing an off-by-one in the trailing-window boundary calculation, the
divergence rate that could not be explained by the known per-process
undercounting problem was down to 0.03%, which the team judged acceptable to
proceed to Phase 1.

Phase 1 (2026-04-15 to 2026-04-28): 5% canary. Aperture enforces decisions for
5% of edge proxy traffic, selected by a consistent hash of client IP, with
automatic rollback if the fail-open rate exceeds 1% or p99 decision latency
exceeds 10ms for more than 5 consecutive minutes. This phase is where the
latency spikes referenced in the v1.2 revision entry below were first
observed: on 2026-05-06 and again on 2026-05-09, p99 client-side timeouts
spiked to 0.4% of canary traffic during the daily 14:00 UTC traffic peak,
correlated with a Redis shard undergoing a routine failover to its hot
standby. The automatic rollback guardrail did not trip because the absolute
p99 decision latency stayed under 10ms; it was only the client-side timeout
rate, a separate signal, that showed the problem. This is what prompted the
v1.2 timeout change described in Section 14.

Phase 2 (2026-04-29 to 2026-05-12): 50% rollout, same guardrails as Phase 1,
plus a manual go/no-go review with the Payments and Search teams before
proceeding. The go/no-go review on 2026-05-12 was attended by Marcus Oduya,
Sana Farouk, and Dev Chatterjee, and approved proceeding to Phase 3 conditional
on the v1.2 timeout change (already deployed by that date) staying in effect
for at least one additional week of observation, which it did.

Phase 3 (2026-05-13 to 2026-05-27): 100% rollout, with the legacy per-process
limiters left in place but set to a 10x looser threshold as a backstop for two
additional weeks before final removal. Full removal of the legacy limiter code
path is tracked as a separate follow-up ticket, ENG-3355, targeted for
2026-06-10, and is intentionally out of scope for this document.

14. MONITORING AND ALERTING

Aperture exports per-Limiter-Core-node metrics to the standard metrics
pipeline: decision_latency_ms (histogram, tagged by decision source),
fail_open_rate (counter), fail_closed_deny_rate (counter), redis_shard_error_rate
(counter, tagged by shard id), and coordinator_subscription_staleness_seconds
(gauge). Five alerts are defined against these metrics. ApertureFailOpenRateHigh
fires when fail_open_rate exceeds 1% of decisions over a 5-minute window,
mirroring the Phase 1/2 rollback guardrail so the same condition that would
have blocked a canary also pages once at 100% rollout. ApertureP99LatencyHigh
fires when the p99 of decision_latency_ms exceeds 10ms for 5 consecutive
minutes. ApertureRedisShardDown fires when a single shard's error rate exceeds
50% for 1 minute, which is treated as page-worthy because a full shard outage
takes roughly 1/8 of all keys down to fail-open behavior at once.
ApertureCoordinatorStale fires when subscription staleness exceeds 60 seconds
for any Limiter Core node, indicating a subscription problem worth
investigating even though it does not yet violate R5's 30-second propagation
budget. ApertureConfigPropagationSlow fires when the same staleness metric
exceeds 30 seconds fleet-wide for more than 2 minutes, which is the direct R5
SLO alert. A Grafana dashboard, "Aperture Overview," plots all five signals
plus a per-partner top-20 usage table refreshed every 30 seconds, and is the
first thing the on-call SRE is expected to open when any of these alerts page.

15. TESTING STRATEGY

Unit tests cover the sliding-window sub-bucket math in isolation, including
boundary conditions at exact sub-bucket edges and the partial-weight
calculation for a sub-bucket that straddles the window boundary. Integration
tests run a real 3-node Coordinator and a real 2-node Limiter Core against a
Redis test container, and exercise configuration propagation, leader failover,
and Redis shard failover end to end. Chaos testing, run weekly against the
staging environment, includes five scenarios: killing a random Coordinator
node, killing a random Limiter Core node, partitioning a Redis shard from all
Limiter Core nodes for 60 seconds, injecting 500ms of added latency on the
Coordinator-to-Limiter-Core subscription stream, and injecting a burst of
malformed CheckRequest messages to verify the API layer degrades gracefully
rather than crashing a Limiter Core process. All five chaos scenarios passed
their defined success criteria (no client-visible latency increase beyond the
R3 budget, no crash, correct fail-open or fail-closed behavior per route) as
of the 2026-05-19 staging run, which was the last chaos run before the Phase 3
go-live decision.

The Redis shard partition scenario deserves more detail because it is the
closest analogue to the real Phase 1 latency spike described in Section 13.
In staging, partitioning one of the 8 shards for 60 seconds causes every key
hashed to that shard, roughly 12.5% of all active keys, to fall back to
fail-open behavior for the duration of the partition, logging a
shard-unavailable event per affected check as described in Section 11.
Client-visible p99 latency during the partition rose to 6.8ms in the
2026-05-19 run, still within budget, because the fail-open path returns
immediately on a Redis error rather than waiting for a timeout; the Phase 1
production incident was different in character because it was a slow
failover rather than a hard partition, meaning requests waited out most of
the client-side timeout before falling back, which is exactly the failure
mode the v1.2 timeout increase was meant to give more headroom against.

The malformed-request scenario replays a corpus of 40 hand-crafted invalid
CheckRequest messages, including missing required fields, negative cost
values, and cost values above max_cost_per_check, against a staging Limiter
Core node at low background load. Every malformed message was correctly
rejected at the API validation layer with a structured error rather than
causing a panic or an unhandled exception, and background decision latency
for concurrent, well-formed requests was unaffected.

16. SECURITY CONSIDERATIONS

The gRPC channel between Edge Proxy Client and Limiter Core, and between
Limiter Core and the Coordinator, is mutually authenticated via the standard
internal service mesh mTLS, consistent with every other internal RPC path; no
new authentication mechanism was introduced for Aperture. The Aperture CLI
used to propose configuration changes (Section 8) requires the
platform-infra-admin role, and every configuration change is written to the
audit log with the proposing operator's identity, timestamp, and a diff of the
old and new configuration, satisfying the general audit requirement from the
Security team's internal services checklist. Dev Chatterjee's review flagged
one residual risk: because Limiter Core nodes cache configuration locally
(Section 7), a compromised or buggy Limiter Core node could theoretically
continue enforcing a revoked or since-tightened limit for up to the 30-second
config_cache_ttl_seconds window after a legitimate operator change. This was
accepted as a low-severity risk given the short window and the fact that
Aperture is not itself an authorization system, only a rate limiter.

17. COST ANALYSIS

The 8-shard Redis layer, sized per Section 12, runs on r6g.2xlarge instances
with a hot standby per shard, for 16 instances total, at an estimated monthly
cost of approximately $9,800 at current reserved-instance pricing. The 6-node
Limiter Core fleet runs on c6g.xlarge instances at approximately $2,400 per
month. The 3-node Coordinator cluster, being low-throughput, runs on smaller
c6g.large instances at approximately $340 per month. Total infrastructure cost
is therefore approximately $12,540 per month, which the team compared against
the estimated cost of the March 2026 Halcyon Logistics incident (INC-4390),
where the Search latency degradation was estimated internally at several times
this monthly figure in a single incident when accounting for on-call time and
partner trust impact; this comparison was used to justify the project during
initial scoping and is not repeated in later sections.

18. REVISION HISTORY

v1.0 (2026-03-20, Priya Raman): Initial draft. Fail-open client timeout set to
150ms across all routes; no fail-closed mode existed yet.

v1.1 (2026-04-22, Tobias Klein): Added the fail-closed override mode and the
card-auth-retry allowlist requirement (R7) after a request from the Payments
Platform team during Phase 0 dogfood review.

v1.2 (2026-05-14, Priya Raman): Following latency spikes observed during the
Phase 1 canary, where p99 client-side timeouts were being hit at a rate of
0.4%, well above the noise floor, the default fail-open client timeout was
raised from 150ms to 300ms for all fail-open routes to give the Limiter Core
fleet more headroom under transient load. The Capacity Planning section
(Section 12) and Appendix A were updated to reflect the new default; the
Architecture Overview (Section 6) and Consistency Model (Section 11) sections
still describe the original 150ms figure from v1.0, which was not caught in
review and remains an open documentation inconsistency between sections as of
this version.

v1.3 (2026-05-29, Tobias Klein): Clarified the rollout plan guardrail
thresholds in Section 13 after the Phase 1 go/no-go review; no functional
changes.

19. ALTERNATIVES CONSIDERED

Alternative A: Sticky routing. Route each rate-limit key's traffic to a single
"owning" proxy via consistent hashing at the load balancer layer, so the
existing in-process limiter would be globally correct without a new service.
Rejected because it would require load balancer changes across three different
cloud providers' load balancer products, and because a single owning proxy per
hot key becomes a scaling bottleneck for our highest-volume partner keys. A
secondary concern, raised by Marcus Oduya during the design review, was that
sticky routing would make it much harder to reason about capacity during a
zone failure, since evacuating a zone would suddenly concentrate a hot key's
entire traffic onto whichever proxies remained, with no rebalancing
mechanism short of a full re-hash.

Alternative B: Client-side approximate counting with periodic reconciliation.
Each proxy keeps a local counter and periodically reconciles with peers via
gossip, tolerating a larger overshoot window. Rejected because the Payments
team's card-auth-retry requirement needs a tighter bound than gossip
reconciliation, on the order of seconds, could realistically provide. This
alternative was otherwise attractive: it would have removed the Coordinator
and Redis layers entirely and eliminated Aperture's hot-path network hop, at
the cost of a much looser consistency bound. The team's assessment was that
this tradeoff would have been acceptable for Search and Notifications traffic
but not for Payments, and building two separate rate-limiting systems for the
two use cases was judged worse than building one system that is slightly more
conservative for the majority of traffic.

Alternative C: Use an existing open-source rate limiting proxy (Envoy's
built-in global rate limit service) instead of building Aperture in-house.
Evaluated and prototyped in February 2026; rejected primarily because the
existing Envoy ratelimit service does not support the cost-weighted checks
needed for expensive operations (Section 10), and integrating the fail-closed
allowlist behavior would have required a nontrivial fork. The prototype,
built by Tobias Klein over roughly a week, also showed p99 latency of 14ms
under a synthetic 50,000 RPS load, already over the R3 budget before reaching
our target scale, which the team attributed to Envoy ratelimit's use of a
single global counter per key rather than the sharded sub-bucket approach
described in Section 7.

20. APPENDIX A: CONFIG REFERENCE

fail_open_client_timeout_ms: default 300 (raised from 150 in v1.2); applies to
all fail-open routes; override per route supported.

fail_closed_client_timeout_ms: default 80; applies to routes on the
fail-closed allowlist; deliberately tighter than the fail-open default because
a fail-closed route should reject quickly rather than hold a caller open.

subbucket_count: default 10; number of sub-buckets per sliding window.

config_cache_ttl_seconds: default 30; matches requirement R5.

coordinator_election_timeout_ms: default 5000.

redis_key_ttl_multiplier: default 2; TTL is this multiplier times the
configured window.

max_cost_per_check: default 1000; guards against a misconfigured caller passing
an unreasonably large cost value.

21. APPENDIX B: BENCHMARK RESULTS

Load test 1 (2026-04-10, synthetic uniform key distribution, 100,000 distinct
keys, 200,000 RPS): p50 2.1ms, p90 4.8ms, p99 7.3ms, p99.9 14.1ms. Run against
a pre-production 4-shard Redis layer and 4-node Limiter Core fleet, before the
capacity plan in Section 12 was finalized; included here for historical
comparison against Load test 3.

Load test 2 (2026-04-24, Phase 1 canary shadow replay, real traffic shape,
peak 21,000 RPS on canary slice): p50 2.4ms, p90 5.6ms, p99 8.9ms, p99.9 19.7ms.
This test used replayed production traffic captured during the 2026-04-22
peak window rather than synthetic uniform load, which is why the tail latency
is higher than Load test 1 despite lower absolute RPS: real key distributions
are heavily skewed toward a small number of hot partner keys, and hot keys
serialize more contention at the Redis layer.

Load test 3 (2026-05-20, Phase 3 pre-rollout, full 400,000 RPS synthetic load
against the production-sized 8-shard Redis layer and 6-node Limiter Core
fleet): p50 2.6ms, p90 6.1ms, p99 9.4ms, p99.9 22.3ms. This is the load test
that gated the Phase 3 go-live decision; the p99 of 9.4ms was judged
acceptably within the 10ms R3 budget but with less margin than the team would
like, and is called out as a follow-up in Appendix C below.

22. APPENDIX C: OPEN QUESTIONS

- Should the fail-closed allowlist be extended beyond card-auth-retry to cover
  the new fraud-scoring endpoint the Risk team is launching in Q3? Tracked as a
  follow-up, not blocking this rollout.
- Do we need a per-partner override of the sub-bucket count for partners with
  very bursty, spiky traffic shapes, rather than a global default of 10?
- The 150ms vs 300ms documentation inconsistency noted in the v1.2 revision
  entry above should be resolved in the next revision by updating Sections 6
  and 11 to match Appendix A.
- Load test 3's p99 of 9.4ms leaves only 0.6ms of margin against the R3
  budget of 10ms; should Limiter Core be sized to 8 nodes instead of 6 ahead
  of the next traffic growth review, currently scheduled for 2026-Q3?
- Should the Appendix A default of fail_closed_client_timeout_ms (80ms) be
  revisited now that the fail-open default has moved to 300ms, so that the
  two defaults do not drift further apart than originally intended?

23. APPENDIX D: FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS

Q: Why does Aperture use Redis rather than a purpose-built counter store?
A: Redis was already an approved, well-operated piece of infrastructure with
existing on-call runbooks, and the sliding-window sub-bucket algorithm maps
cleanly onto Redis hash operations with atomic HINCRBY. Building a bespoke
counter store was considered out of scope for the problem at hand.

Q: What happens to in-flight decisions during a Coordinator leader election?
A: Nothing changes for decision traffic, because Limiter Core nodes serve
decisions from their local configuration cache regardless of Coordinator
leadership state, as described in Section 8. Only new configuration changes
are delayed until a leader is re-elected.

Q: Can a team self-serve a new rate-limit key pattern without going through
Platform Infra?
A: Not at launch. All configuration changes go through the Aperture CLI,
which currently requires the platform-infra-admin role described in Section
16. Self-service is tracked as a possible Phase 4 follow-up but is explicitly
out of scope for this document.

Q: Why 8 Redis shards specifically, rather than 4 or 16?
A: 8 was chosen as the smallest shard count that keeps per-shard operations
under the ~1M ops/sec ceiling from the September 2025 Redis capacity study
with headroom for the traffic growth projected through the next two
quarters, while not fragmenting keys so finely that a single shard failure
becomes a rounding error not worth alerting on; see Section 12 and Section 14.

24. APPENDIX E: GLOSSARY

Decision RPC: The Check() call made by the Edge Proxy Client to Limiter Core
for a given request; may bundle multiple RateLimitCheck entries.

Fail-open: The default failure mode where an unreachable or slow limiter
results in the request being allowed rather than denied.

Fail-closed: The alternative failure mode, used only for the allowlisted
routes in Section 11, where an unreachable or slow limiter results in the
request being denied.

Key pattern: A named template (for example "partner-qps") that determines how
a concrete rate-limit key is constructed from request attributes.

Sub-bucket: One of the fixed-size time slices that a configured window is
divided into for the sliding-window approximation described in Section 7.

Staleness: The age of the configuration a Limiter Core node is currently
using, relative to the latest committed configuration in the Coordinator;
tracked by the coordinator_subscription_staleness_seconds metric in Section
14.

25. APPENDIX F: GROWTH PROJECTION

The capacity plan in Section 12 is sized against current traffic of roughly
350,000 requests per second. The Platform Infra team's Q3 2026 growth
forecast, based on the Search team's planned expansion into two additional
regions and the Payments team's planned onboarding of four new large
merchants, projects edge proxy traffic reaching approximately 480,000
requests per second by the end of Q3 2026 and approximately 620,000 requests
per second by the end of Q4 2026. At the same 1.15 checks-per-request and
10-Redis-operations-per-check ratios used in Section 12, the Q4 2026 figure
implies roughly 7.1 million Redis operations per second fleet-wide, or
890,000 operations per second per shard at the current 8-shard layout,
already above the ~1M ops/sec single-instance ceiling once normal headroom is
subtracted. The team's current plan, not yet committed as a formal decision,
is to add a ninth and tenth Redis shard ahead of the Q4 2026 traffic growth
rather than waiting for the existing 8-shard layout to show sustained
saturation, and to revisit the Limiter Core node count in the same review
given the reduced latency margin already observed in Load test 3 (Appendix
B).
