INCIDENT REPORT: INC-4471
Payment Authorization Outage

Severity: SEV1
Date: 2026-06-18
Detection Time: 14:07 UTC
Full Recovery Time: 15:39 UTC
Formal Close Time: 16:09 UTC
Duration (detection to recovery): 92 minutes
Services Affected: paysvc (Payment Authorization Service), checkout-api (indirectly)
Incident Commander: Rina Chow
Primary Responders: Elena Vasquez (on-call, Payments Platform), Marcus Bello
  (secondary on-call, Payments Platform), Devon Okafor (Payments Platform,
  paged in during escalation)
Status: Resolved, corrective actions tracked below

1. SUMMARY

At 14:07 UTC on 2026-06-18, the paysvc-error-rate-high alert fired, indicating
elevated authorization failures in the Payment Authorization Service (paysvc).
The root cause was a connection pool exhaustion bug introduced by paysvc
release v2.44.0, deployed at 14:02 UTC, which caused paysvc's connections to
the fraud-scoring sidecar to leak under load and eventually saturate the pool,
causing authorization requests to queue and time out. Two mitigation attempts
prior to the eventual fix did not resolve the issue: a rolling restart of a
subset of paysvc pods, and a failover of the fraud-scoring service to its
backup cluster. The incident was ultimately resolved by rolling paysvc back to
the previous release, v2.43.2, though this rollback was itself complicated by
a deploy-tool bug that silently left one of three regional clusters
(us-west-2) on the bad release even after the rollback was reported complete,
which extended the outage by approximately 22 minutes beyond what a clean
rollback would have taken. Full recovery was confirmed at 15:39 UTC after the
us-west-2 cluster was manually rolled back.

2. IMPACT

During the 92-minute window between detection and full recovery, paysvc's
authorization error rate peaked at 41% (compared to a baseline of under 0.3%).
Checkout-api, which depends on paysvc synchronously for card authorization,
surfaced these failures to end users as "payment could not be processed"
errors. Based on the checkout-api request logs for the affected window, an
estimated 118,000 checkout attempts encountered an authorization failure that
would not have occurred absent this incident, of which an estimated 74,000
represent attempts where the customer did not subsequently retry
successfully within the same session. Finance's preliminary estimate, shared
in the incident channel at 16:02 UTC by Priya Anand from Finance Ops, put the
directly attributable revenue impact at approximately $410,000, with a wider
range of $340,000 to $520,000 depending on assumptions about how many
customers retried later outside the session window. Customer support received
approximately 2,300 tickets referencing checkout failures during and shortly
after the incident window.

2B. IMPACT METHODOLOGY

The 118,000 figure in Section 2 was derived by comparing checkout-api's
authorization failure count during the 14:07-15:39 window against the
trailing 7-day median failure count for the same time-of-day window, with the
difference attributed to the incident. The 74,000 "did not subsequently
retry successfully" figure was derived by joining failed authorization
attempts against the same session's later checkout attempts within a 20
minute window; a customer who retried and succeeded within that window is
excluded from the 74,000 even though their initial attempt still counts
toward the 118,000. Finance's revenue estimate in Section 2 multiplies the
74,000 figure by the trailing 30-day average order value for the affected
merchant mix, which is why Finance flagged their own figure as preliminary
pending a manual review of a sample of the affected merchants, since average
order value varies significantly by merchant category and the affected
traffic was not necessarily representative of the overall merchant mix.

2A. REGIONAL BREAKDOWN

Impact was not evenly distributed across the three regional clusters, which
matters for interpreting the timeline in Section 4. us-east-1 and eu-west-1
both recovered promptly once the 14:49 rollback was initiated, since both
correctly rolled back to v2.43.2 at 14:52. us-west-2, which serves
approximately 38% of total paysvc traffic, remained on the broken v2.44.0
release until 15:14, and therefore accounted for a disproportionate share of
the total impact figures in Section 2: of the estimated 118,000 affected
checkout attempts, approximately 61,000 (52%) occurred in the us-west-2
region alone, despite that region carrying well under half of total traffic,
because us-west-2 was the only region still failing between 14:52 and 15:14
while the other two regions had already recovered.

3. DETECTION

The incident was detected automatically. The paysvc-error-rate-high alert,
configured to fire when the rolling 5-minute authorization error rate exceeds
5%, tripped at 14:07 UTC when the error rate crossed 6.1%, and paged the
primary on-call for Payments Platform, Elena Vasquez, via PagerDuty. This was
4 minutes prior to any customer-reported ticket reaching support, and 5
minutes prior to the first automated anomaly signal from the checkout-api
dashboard, so the paysvc alert was the earliest available signal and worked as
designed.

4. TIMELINE

All times UTC on 2026-06-18 unless otherwise noted.

14:02 - paysvc v2.44.0 deployed to all three regional clusters (us-east-1,
us-west-2, eu-west-1) via the standard rolling deploy pipeline. Deploy tool
reports success for all three clusters. This release included a change to
the connection pooling configuration for the fraud-scoring sidecar client,
intended to reduce idle connection overhead.

14:07 - paysvc-error-rate-high alert fires at 6.1% rolling error rate. Pages
Elena Vasquez (primary on-call, Payments Platform).

14:09 - Elena acknowledges the page.

14:11 - Elena begins investigating, posts in #paysvc-oncall: "Looking at
elevated 5xx on paysvc, error rate climbing, currently 9%."

14:14 - Error rate reaches 22%. Elena posts: "This is not settling, opening an
incident."

14:15 - Elena declares SEV2 and opens incident channel #inc-4471.

14:17 - Elena's initial working theory, posted in-channel: "Possible memory
leak from today's deploy, pool metrics look off, checking dashboards."

14:19 - Elena pages secondary on-call Marcus Bello for a second set of hands,
and requests an Incident Commander per the on-call runbook. Rina Chow is
assigned IC by the paging system.

14:21 - Rina joins #inc-4471, confirms IC role, asks for current error rate
and customer impact assessment.

14:23 - Marcus joins, reports paysvc connection pool utilization dashboard
shows the fraud-scoring sidecar client pool pinned at 100% capacity across
all three clusters, consistent with Elena's leak theory.

14:24 - Attempt 1: Marcus initiates a rolling restart of one third of paysvc
pods in us-east-1, reasoning that a pool leak should clear on process
restart, as a fast partial mitigation while investigation continues.

14:27 - Restarted pods come back healthy. Error rate dips from 24% to 19%
over the next two minutes, consistent with the restarted third of the fleet
temporarily having healthy connection pools.

14:30 - Error rate climbs back to 27% as the restarted pods' pools begin
saturating again under continued load. Marcus posts: "Restart bought us
about 3 minutes, pools are filling right back up, this isn't a slow leak,
something about this deploy is actively breaking pool release."

14:31 - Given sustained customer impact and no resolution, Rina upgrades the
incident to SEV1 and pages the VP of Payments Engineering (notification
only, no action expected) and pages additional Payments Platform engineers.
Devon Okafor, who was not on-call but has deep familiarity with the
fraud-scoring sidecar integration, joins in response to the broadcast page.

14:33 - Error rate 34%.

14:36 - Attempt 2: Devon's working theory is that the fraud-scoring sidecar
itself, rather than paysvc's client code, is slow to release connections
server-side, possibly due to a fraud-scoring-side issue unrelated to the
paysvc deploy. Devon initiates a failover of the fraud-scoring service to
its backup cluster (fraud-scoring-backup), which is kept warm for exactly
this kind of failover.

14:39 - Failover to fraud-scoring-backup completes. Error rate: 37%, no
improvement.

14:41 - Devon posts: "Backup fraud cluster is live and pool exhaustion is
continuing at the same rate, so this isn't a fraud-scoring-side problem,
it's specific to paysvc's client behavior. Reverting my failover isn't
urgent, backup cluster is healthy, leaving it as-is for now to reduce moving
parts."

14:44 - Elena, cross-referencing the deploy log with the alert start time,
posts: "v2.44.0 went out at 14:02, alert fired at 14:07, that's a tight
enough gap that I think we should stop chasing the sidecar and just roll
paysvc back to v2.43.2."

14:46 - Rina, as IC, agrees and directs Marcus to initiate the rollback of
paysvc to v2.43.2 across all three clusters.

14:47 - Error rate 39%.

14:49 - Attempt 3: Marcus initiates the rollback via the standard deploy
tool, targeting all three regional clusters (us-east-1, us-west-2,
eu-west-1).

14:52 - Deploy tool reports rollback complete for all three clusters. Marcus
posts in #inc-4471: "Rollback finished 14:52, all three clusters back on
v2.43.2, should start seeing recovery any minute."

14:55 - Error rate still 40%, no sign of recovery.

14:58 - Marcus posts: "Confused, rollback finished 14:52, why still
failing," and begins re-checking the deploy tool output.

15:01 - Rina asks whether the rollback might not actually be complete
everywhere despite the tool's success report, and asks Devon to check
per-cluster version directly rather than trusting the deploy tool summary.

15:03 - Devon checks the canary/version dashboard directly, cluster by
cluster, and finds us-east-1 and eu-west-1 correctly running v2.43.2, but
us-west-2 still running v2.44.0. Devon posts: "Found it. us-west-2 never
actually rolled back, it's still on v2.44.0. Deploy tool reported success but
the region was marked draining at the time from an unrelated autoscaling
event, and the tool silently skips draining regions instead of failing
loudly. This is the same bug tracked in TOOLING-891 from March, we clearly
didn't fully fix it."

15:05 - Rina directs Devon to manually force the rollback on us-west-2,
bypassing the standard tool's drain check.

15:07 - Devon begins the manual rollback of us-west-2 to v2.43.2.

15:14 - Manual rollback of us-west-2 completes. Error rate begins dropping
immediately: 40% down to 18% within two minutes.

15:19 - Error rate 6%.

15:24 - Error rate 1.2%.

15:31 - Error rate back to baseline, 0.2%.

15:39 - Rina declares full recovery and starts a 30-minute bake period with
elevated monitoring before formally closing the incident, per the SEV1
runbook.

16:02 - Priya Anand (Finance Ops) posts the preliminary revenue impact
estimate referenced in Section 2.

16:09 - Rina formally closes #inc-4471 after the bake period shows no
regression.

4A. PRE-INCIDENT CONTEXT

The v2.44.0 release had gone through the standard paysvc review and staging
process. It was authored by Devon Okafor over the preceding week, reviewed by
Elena Vasquez on 2026-06-16, and passed the standard staging soak test, which
runs synthetic traffic against a staging paysvc cluster for 4 hours prior to
any production deploy. The staging soak test did not surface the connection
pool issue because, as described in Section 6, the staging fraud-scoring
sidecar's synthetic traffic generator does not produce backpressure/rate-limit
responses at realistic rates; staging traffic volume is deliberately kept
under the threshold where the sidecar would ever return a non-2xx response,
so the leak path in the new pooling code was never exercised before
production. The deploy itself was scheduled for the low-traffic pre-lunch
window in US time zones but slipped to 14:02 UTC (10:02 ET) due to an
unrelated CI queue backlog that morning, landing closer to the lunch-hour
peak than originally intended; this timing is believed to have made the pool
exhaustion happen faster than it might have at the originally scheduled time,
though the bug itself would have surfaced regardless once traffic increased.

5. ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS

The proximate cause was a bug in paysvc v2.44.0's updated connection pooling
configuration for the fraud-scoring sidecar client. The change, intended to
reduce idle connection overhead, altered the pool's connection-return logic
so that connections were only returned to the pool on a successful response;
on any non-2xx response from the fraud-scoring sidecar (including the sidecar's
normal rate-limit backpressure responses under load, which are an expected and
previously harmless occurrence), the connection was leaked rather than
returned. Under the moderately elevated load present at 14:02 UTC, a normal
weekday lunch-hour peak, the rate of fraud-scoring backpressure responses was
high enough that the pool exhausted within roughly five minutes of the
deploy, which lines up with the 14:07 alert.

The incident took materially longer to resolve than the underlying bug alone
would explain, because of a second, independent issue: the deploy tool's
handling of regions marked "draining" for unrelated reasons (in this case, a
scheduled autoscaling event in us-west-2 that happened to be in progress at
14:49) causes it to silently skip those regions during a rollback rather than
failing the rollback or clearly reporting a partial result. This meant the
team had high confidence, from 14:52 to 15:03, that the fix had already been
applied and had simply not yet taken effect, rather than correctly
understanding that the fix had not been applied to a third of the fleet at
all. This is judged to have added approximately 11 minutes directly (from
14:52 to 15:03, before the team started re-verifying) plus additional time
spent second-guessing the rollback rather than immediately investigating a
partial rollback as the first hypothesis.

5A. TECHNICAL DETAIL: THE POOLING CHANGE

The relevant change in v2.44.0 replaced a try/finally-style connection
release with a release call placed only in the success branch of the response
handler. Prior to v2.44.0, the client released its fraud-scoring connection
back to the pool unconditionally after every request, regardless of the
response status, which is safe but was believed to be marginally slower
because it involved an extra pool bookkeeping call on every single request
rather than being skipped for the (rare, in the original author's mental
model) error path. The v2.44.0 author's intent was to skip that bookkeeping
call specifically for connections that would be discarded anyway on a hard
failure such as a connection reset; the bug is that the sidecar's ordinary
rate-limit backpressure response (HTTP 429, a routine and expected response
under load, not a hard failure) was classified into the same "discard, do not
release" branch as a true hard failure, even though a 429 response arrives on
a perfectly healthy connection that should simply be returned to the pool
for reuse. Under the moderate load at 14:02 UTC, the fraud-scoring sidecar
was issuing 429 responses at a rate of roughly 3-4% of requests, which,
compounded continuously, was enough to exhaust a 200-connection pool within
about five minutes, matching the gap between the 14:02 deploy and the 14:07
alert.

6. CONTRIBUTING FACTORS

- The connection pool change in v2.44.0 was not covered by the paysvc load
  test suite, which does not currently simulate fraud-scoring backpressure
  responses at realistic rates; it only tests the pure-success path.
- The deploy tool's silent-skip-on-drain behavior (TOOLING-891) was
  previously identified in a March 2026 incident review as a bug to fix, but
  the fix that shipped only addressed the case where a region is draining at
  the start of a deploy, not the case where a region enters draining state
  mid-rollout, which is what happened here.
- The two initial mitigation attempts (pod restart, fraud-scoring failover)
  were reasonable given the information available at the time, but both
  consumed time (roughly 20 minutes combined) before the team converged on
  the deploy-correlation signal that pointed at a rollback. Earlier
  correlation of the deploy timestamp against the alert timestamp might have
  shortened this.
- There is no automated alert that compares a service's currently-deployed
  version across regions and flags a mismatch; this gap directly delayed
  detection of the partial rollback.

7. WHAT WENT WELL

- Detection was fast and automatic; the alert fired within 5 minutes of the
  bad deploy, well ahead of any customer-facing signal.
- Escalation from SEV2 to SEV1 happened promptly once sustained impact was
  clear, and the broader page correctly pulled in Devon Okafor, whose
  fraud-scoring expertise was valuable even though his specific hypothesis
  did not turn out to be the root cause.
- Once Devon checked per-cluster versions directly rather than trusting the
  deploy tool's summary, the actual problem (partial rollback) was found and
  fixed within about 11 minutes.

8. WHAT WENT POORLY

- The team trusted the deploy tool's "rollback complete" status for
  approximately 11 minutes without independently verifying per-cluster
  versions, despite TOOLING-891 being a known, previously-filed issue with
  exactly this class of failure.
- Two sequential mitigation attempts before the rollback (pod restart,
  fraud-scoring failover) were tried without a clear, shared hypothesis
  ranking; in hindsight, correlating the deploy timestamp against the alert
  timestamp, which was available from the very start of the incident, should
  have been one of the first things checked rather than the fourth.
- The fraud-scoring failover performed by Devon at 14:36 was left in place
  for the rest of the incident and, per the corrective actions below, was
  not reverted until the following day, which is outside intended runbook
  practice of reverting non-load-bearing mitigations once they are ruled
  out.

8A. SIMILAR PAST INCIDENTS

This is the second paysvc incident in 2026 involving the connection pool to
the fraud-scoring sidecar. INC-4102, on 2026-02-11, involved a similar pool
exhaustion but was caused by the fraud-scoring service itself becoming slow
to respond (a genuine sidecar-side degradation, unlike this incident), and
was resolved by scaling up the fraud-scoring cluster rather than a paysvc
rollback. Devon Okafor's initial hypothesis at 14:36 in this incident,
described in Section 4, was influenced by that earlier incident's pattern,
which is a reasonable prior but did not turn out to apply here; the two
incidents are noted together in the corrective actions below because CA-7's
audit is intended to also check whether INC-4102's fix and this incident's
fix could interact.

This is also the third incident in the past twelve months, after TOOLING-891's
original March 2026 review and a separate June 2025 incident (INC-3778, not
detailed here), where the deploy tool's handling of regional cluster state
during a rollout has directly extended an incident's duration. Rina Chow's
incident review notes flag this pattern as warranting a broader review of the
deploy tool's reliability guarantees beyond the specific fix in CA-3.

9. CORRECTIVE ACTIONS

CA-1: Fix the paysvc connection pool client to return connections on any
completed response, not only on 2xx responses. Owner: Devon Okafor. Due:
2026-06-25. Status: In progress. Devon's fix reclassifies HTTP 429 and other
soft-backpressure responses into the "release" branch, reserving the
"discard, do not release" path strictly for cases where the underlying
transport connection itself is no longer usable, such as a reset or a
timeout with no response at all. A draft PR was opened 2026-06-20 and is
pending review from Elena Vasquez.

CA-2: Add load test coverage for fraud-scoring sidecar backpressure
responses to the paysvc load test suite. Owner: Elena Vasquez. Due:
2026-07-09. Status: Not started. The plan is to extend the existing staging
soak test's synthetic traffic generator so that it can be configured to
produce a target rate of 429 responses from the fraud-scoring sidecar mock,
calibrated against the roughly 3-4% rate observed in production during this
incident, so that future pooling changes are exercised against a realistic
backpressure rate rather than the current all-success synthetic load.

CA-3: Fix the deploy tool so that a region entering "draining" state mid-
rollout causes the rollout to fail loudly for that region rather than
silently skipping it, closing the gap left by the original TOOLING-891 fix.
Owner: Marcus Bello. Due: 2026-06-30. Status: In progress. Marcus notes that
the original TOOLING-891 fix, which shipped in April 2026, only added a
pre-flight check for regions already draining before a rollout starts; it
never handled the case of a region transitioning into draining state after
the rollout for that region has already been kicked off but before it
completes, which is the specific sequence that occurred here.

across all regional clusters and fires if they diverge for more than 5
minutes outside of an active, intentional rollout window. Owner: Rina Chow.

measure independent of CA-3, so that even if a future deploy tool bug of a
different shape produces a similar partial rollout, on-call engineers get a
direct signal rather than needing to manually check per-cluster versions as
Devon did at 15:03.

against alert timestamp" and "verify per-cluster deployed version directly,
not via deploy tool summary" as early, high-priority checks. Owner: Rina

primary fraud-scoring cluster now that it is confirmed unrelated to the root

cluster health checks passed.

library for the same non-2xx connection leak pattern introduced in v2.44.0.
Owner: Elena Vasquez. Due: 2026-07-02. Status: Not started. At least two
other services, notifications-relay and risk-scoring-proxy, are known to
depend on the same shared client library and are the first two targets for
this audit; both are believed lower-risk because neither has deployed a
version bump to the pooling-related code since it was introduced, but
neither has been formally confirmed clean as of this report.

9A. LESSONS FOR THE RUNBOOK

Beyond the specific corrective actions in Section 9, the incident review
identified a broader process lesson: the team's mitigation attempts were
each individually reasonable but were not explicitly tracked as a ranked
hypothesis list with expected signals to confirm or rule each one out. Rina
Chow's post-incident notes recommend that future SEV1s maintain a visible,
timestamped hypothesis log in the incident channel, separate from the general
discussion, so that an attempt like the 14:36 fraud-scoring failover is
explicitly marked as "ruled out, no effect" as soon as it is judged
inconclusive, rather than left ambiguous while the channel discussion moves
on to the next idea. This is expected to be folded into the SEV1 runbook
update tracked as CA-5, though CA-5 as currently scoped and already merged
only covers the two specific checks called out in Section 8, and the
broader hypothesis-log practice remains a separate, not-yet-scheduled
follow-up.
