POSTMORTEM: NIGHTLY ANALYTICS PIPELINE DATA LOSS
Incident Date: 2026-03-08
Postmortem Author: Yara Solberg (Data Platform)
Reviewers: Malik Ferreira (Data Platform Lead), Connie Huang (Analytics
Engineering), Owen Baptiste (Data Platform)
Status: Final

1. SUMMARY

On the night of 2026-03-08, the nightly analytics pipeline that populates the
company's primary reporting warehouse silently dropped approximately 6.2% of
the day's event records for the orders_events and inventory_events source
tables, affecting the March 8 partition of twelve downstream tables and, by
extension, every dashboard and scheduled report built on those tables. The
corruption was not detected by any automated check at the time it occurred;
it was discovered the following morning, 2026-03-09, at 08:14 local time when
an analyst on the Merchandising team, Deepa Krishnan, noticed that a daily
revenue-by-category report looked implausibly low compared to the trailing
7-day average and escalated to the Data Platform on-call. The root cause was
a retried upstream ingestion job (orders-events-ingest) that, on its second
attempt, wrote a partial batch to a staging table without first clearing the
partial output of its first, failed attempt, and a downstream deduplication
step that was supposed to catch exactly this case had a boundary condition
bug that caused it to silently drop the affected rows instead of correctly
deduplicating them. Full data recovery was completed by 2026-03-10 at 19:40,
approximately 35 hours after the corruption was introduced and about 35
hours after the corrupted partitions had already been served to end users of
the affected dashboards and reports.

1A. WHY THIS POSTMORTEM IS BLAMELESS

Consistent with the Data Platform team's standing postmortem policy, this
document is written to identify systemic and process gaps rather than to
assign fault to any individual, including the author of the January 2026
refactor referenced in Section 6, who is no longer with the company for
reasons unrelated to this incident. The two bugs described in this
postmortem were each, individually, reasonable code to have written and
reasonably difficult to have caught in ordinary review; the incident exists
because of their specific, previously-untested interaction, not because
either engineer involved was careless.

2. IMPACT

Twelve downstream tables in the reporting warehouse were affected for the
2026-03-08 partition: revenue_by_category_daily, orders_summary_daily,
inventory_snapshot_daily, and nine others derived from the same two source
tables. At least 34 scheduled reports and 11 executive dashboards consumed
one or more of these tables before the corruption was caught. The
Merchandising, Finance, and Executive teams all received at least one report
or dashboard view reflecting the corrupted data during the affected window,
2026-03-09 05:00 (when the first scheduled reports ran against the corrupted
partition) through 2026-03-09 09:20 (when the affected tables were pulled
from user-facing consumption, described in Section 4). No data was
permanently lost; the source events remained available in the upstream
event log (see Section 5) and were fully recovered, but every report and
dashboard view generated during the affected window reflected undercounted
figures, and at least three of those views, including a Finance weekly
revenue summary sent to executive leadership on the morning of 2026-03-09,
were consumed and acted on before the correction, requiring a follow-up
correction email from Finance.

3. TIMELINE

All times Pacific, 2026-03-08 unless otherwise noted.

22:41 - The scheduled orders-events-ingest job begins its nightly run,
reading from the upstream orders event log and writing to a staging table,
orders_events_staging, ahead of the downstream deduplication and load steps.

23:02 - orders-events-ingest fails partway through with a transient
connection error to the upstream event log's read replica, having written
approximately 41% of the night's orders events to orders_events_staging
before failing. The job's failure is logged but the job's automatic retry
policy, configured to retry up to 2 times, means this is not treated as an
alerting event; a retry is expected and considered normal operation.

23:04 - The orchestrator (an internal scheduling system, not otherwise
detailed in this report) automatically retries orders-events-ingest.

23:04 - The retried job attempt begins. Per its design, the job is intended
to first truncate any partial output from a prior failed attempt in
orders_events_staging before writing new output, specifically to handle
exactly the failure mode from 23:02. This truncate step is implemented as a
DELETE statement scoped by a batch_id column that should uniquely identify
each run's rows.

23:04 - The truncate step's DELETE statement uses the wrong batch_id: due to
a bug introduced in a January 2026 refactor of the ingestion job (see
Section 6), the retried attempt generates a new batch_id for itself rather
than reusing or correctly targeting the failed attempt's batch_id, so the
DELETE statement, scoped to the new batch_id, deletes zero rows rather than
the intended 41% partial output from the 23:02 failure.

23:04-23:52 - The retried job attempt proceeds to write its own full output
to orders_events_staging without having cleared the prior partial output,
resulting in orders_events_staging containing both the partial output from
the failed 23:02 attempt (41% of the night's events, all correctly-formed
individual rows) and the complete output from the successful 23:04 attempt
(100% of the night's events), for a table that at this point contains
approximately 141% of the intended row count, with the overlapping 41%
present as exact duplicate rows differing only in their batch_id column.

23:53 - The downstream deduplication step, dedupe-orders-events, begins.
This step is designed to detect and collapse duplicate rows that can arise
from exactly this kind of retry scenario, keying on a composite of
(order_id, event_type, event_timestamp) rather than batch_id, specifically
because batch_id was known to be an unreliable dedup key even before this
incident.

23:53-00:10 (2026-03-09) - dedupe-orders-events runs and encounters the bug
described in Section 6: its deduplication logic correctly identifies
duplicate (order_id, event_type, event_timestamp) tuples, but a boundary
condition in how it selects which of the duplicate rows to keep causes it,
for rows where the duplicate pair spans the specific batch boundary created
by this incident's retry pattern, to drop both copies of the row rather than
keeping one, for a subset of the duplicated rows. This affected 6.2% of the
total night's orders_events volume (measured as: 6.2% of all rows present in
the source event log for that night are absent from the deduplicated output,
though the underlying cause is specific to rows near the batch boundary, not
a uniform random 6.2% sample).

00:10-00:35 - The downstream load step completes, loading the (silently
under-counted) deduplicated output into the reporting warehouse's
orders_events table for the 2026-03-08 partition. No error or warning is
raised at any point in this sequence; from the orchestrator's perspective,
every step in the pipeline completed successfully.

00:35-04:50 - A parallel, structurally similar issue occurs in the
inventory-events-ingest pipeline for unrelated reasons (a separate, lower-
volume upstream retry, also hitting the same dedupe-orders-events boundary
bug, which is shared code also used for inventory events under the name
dedupe-inventory-events). This affects a smaller fraction of inventory
events, contributing to the corruption of the inventory_snapshot_daily table
along with the eleven other affected downstream tables listed in Section 2.

05:00-08:00 (2026-03-09) - Scheduled downstream transformation jobs run
against the corrupted 2026-03-08 partitions of orders_events and
inventory_events, populating all twelve affected downstream tables listed in
Section 2 with undercounted figures. No anomaly detection fires, because the
existing pipeline monitoring checks row counts against a fixed absolute
floor threshold rather than a relative comparison against recent history,
and the corrupted row counts, while approximately 6% low, remained above
that floor threshold.

08:14 - Deepa Krishnan (Merchandising) notices the implausibly low
revenue-by-category figures in her daily report and escalates to the Data
Platform on-call, Owen Baptiste.

08:22 - Owen acknowledges and begins investigating, initially suspecting an
upstream source system issue rather than the pipeline itself, since no
pipeline alert had fired.

08:41 - Owen rules out an upstream source system issue after confirming the
raw event log (upstream of orders-events-ingest) contains the expected full
volume of events for the night, narrowing the investigation to the pipeline
itself.

09:03 - Owen identifies the row-count discrepancy between the raw event log
and the loaded orders_events table for the 2026-03-08 partition, confirming
data loss within the pipeline, and escalates to Malik Ferreira (Data
Platform Lead) and declares an incident.

09:20 - Malik directs that the twelve affected downstream tables be pulled
from user-facing consumption (dashboards and scheduled reports disabled or
marked with a data-quality warning banner) while the root cause is
investigated, to prevent further consumption of corrupted data. This marks
the end of the user-facing impact window described in Section 2.

09:20-11:45 - Investigation continues. Owen and Malik trace the discrepancy
back through the pipeline's steps in reverse: load step, then
dedupe-orders-events, then orders_events_staging.

11:45 - Owen discovers the duplicate rows with mismatched batch_id values in
orders_events_staging, which is the first concrete evidence pointing at the
23:04 retry's failure to truncate the prior partial attempt's output,
described in Section 3's 23:04 entries above.

13:10 - Yara Solberg, joining the investigation after Owen's finding, traces
the dedupe-orders-events boundary bug (Section 6) by reproducing it against
a copy of the corrupted orders_events_staging data in a staging environment,
confirming this, not the duplicate rows themselves, as the mechanism by
which rows were actually lost rather than merely duplicated.

14:30 - Root cause confirmed and documented in the incident channel: the
January 2026 batch_id refactor bug (truncate failure) combined with the
pre-existing dedupe boundary bug together caused the data loss; either bug
alone would not have caused data loss (the truncate bug alone would have
caused duplicates, which dedupe should have correctly collapsed; the dedupe
bug alone would never have been triggered without the specific duplicate
pattern the truncate bug created).

15:00-19:40 (2026-03-10, spanning into the next day) - Recovery: Yara
develops and tests a corrected re-run of the affected pipeline steps for the
2026-03-08 partition, re-processing from the raw upstream event log (not
from the corrupted orders_events_staging table) with the dedupe bug
patched. This is tested against a staging environment first, validated
against the known-good raw row counts, and then run against production,
completing at 2026-03-10 19:40 and restoring all twelve affected downstream
tables to correct values for the 2026-03-08 partition.

3A. WHO KNEW WHAT, WHEN: STAKEHOLDER COMMUNICATION

Separately from the technical timeline in Section 3, this section tracks
when affected stakeholder teams were notified, since the gap between data
corruption and stakeholder notification is itself a factor discussed in
Section 8. Deepa Krishnan's Merchandising team was the first to notice
anything wrong, at 08:14, and functioned as the de facto initial reporter
rather than being notified by Data Platform. Finance was not proactively
notified of a potential issue until 09:35, fifteen minutes after the
incident was formally declared at 09:20, by which point Finance's weekly
revenue summary (referenced in Section 2) had already been sent to
executive leadership at approximately 07:00 that morning, before Data
Platform had any awareness of the problem. Executive leadership itself was
notified directly by Malik Ferreira at 10:15, once root cause investigation
was far enough along to describe the issue with some confidence rather than
only "we are investigating a possible data quality issue." Finance's
follow-up correction email to executive leadership, noted in Section 2, was
sent at 2026-03-10 20:15, shortly after recovery validation completed
(Appendix B), with corrected figures.

3B. RELATED PAST INCIDENTS

This is not the first data-quality incident traced to the orders-events
pipeline. INCIDENT-DP-118 (2025-09-22) involved a different bug in the same
pipeline causing duplicate (not missing) rows in a downstream table, caught
within two hours by a downstream consumer's own row-count sanity check
rather than by Data Platform's own monitoring, which is part of the
motivation cited by Owen Baptiste for CA-5's reconciliation check. Unlike
this incident, INCIDENT-DP-118 did not involve the truncate-before-retry
logic or the dedupe boundary bug described in Section 6, and its corrective
actions (adding a downstream row-count check to the specific table
involved) did not generalize to catch this incident, which is part of why
Owen and Malik pushed for CA-4's broader relative-threshold approach rather
than another table-specific fix.

4. FIVE WHYS ANALYSIS

Why did downstream reports show undercounted data? Because the
orders_events and inventory_events tables for the 2026-03-08 partition were
missing 6.2% and a smaller unspecified percentage of rows respectively.

Why were rows missing from those tables? Because the deduplication step,
dedupe-orders-events (and its inventory-events counterpart), dropped both
copies of certain duplicate rows instead of correctly keeping one, due to a
boundary condition bug.

Why did the deduplication step encounter duplicate rows it wasn't designed
to handle correctly? Because the retried orders-events-ingest job failed to
truncate the prior failed attempt's partial output before writing its own
output, due to a batch_id bug introduced in a January 2026 refactor.

Why wasn't this caught before reaching production data? Because the
January 2026 refactor's test suite did not include a test case for the
retry-after-partial-failure scenario specifically, and the dedupe boundary
bug had never been triggered in the roughly 14 months dedupe-orders-events
had been in production prior to this incident, since the specific duplicate
pattern this incident created had never previously occurred.

Why wasn't the corruption caught by monitoring before it reached end users?
Because the pipeline's row-count anomaly detection used a fixed absolute
floor rather than a relative comparison against recent history, and 6.2%
undercounting did not cross that floor.

5. CONTRIBUTING FACTORS

- The January 2026 refactor of orders-events-ingest (described in Section 6)
  was reviewed and merged without a test case covering the retry-after-
  partial-failure scenario, despite that scenario being the entire reason
  the truncate-before-retry logic exists in the first place.
- dedupe-orders-events's boundary condition bug had been latent in
  production for approximately 14 months without being triggered, because
  the specific duplicate pattern required to trigger it (rows spanning a
  batch_id boundary in the specific way this incident created) had never
  previously occurred; this is why root cause required combining two
  independently unremarkable bugs.
- Row-count anomaly detection (Section 3, 05:00-08:00 entry) uses an
  absolute floor rather than a relative, trend-aware check, meaning any
  gradual or moderate-percentage data loss that stays above the floor will
  not be automatically detected regardless of cause.
- The raw upstream event log (source of truth) was never itself corrupted or
  at risk, but no automated process compares downstream row counts against
  it on an ongoing basis; the comparison Owen performed manually at 08:41
  was a manual, ad hoc investigative step, not a standing check.

6. TECHNICAL DETAIL: THE TWO BUGS

The January 2026 refactor of orders-events-ingest was undertaken to make the
job's batch_id generation more robust against clock skew across worker
nodes, replacing a timestamp-based batch_id with a UUID generated fresh at
the start of each job attempt. This inadvertently broke the truncate-before-
retry logic, which had previously (correctly) relied on retried attempts
sharing a deterministic, timestamp-derived batch_id with their failed
predecessor, allowing the truncate step to find and delete the prior
attempt's rows. After the refactor, each attempt, including retries,
generates a fresh UUID, so the truncate step's DELETE, scoped to the new
attempt's own (different) batch_id, never matches any existing rows. The
refactor's author, who no longer works at the company as of February 2026,
is not available to provide additional context on why this interaction was
missed; Malik Ferreira's assessment, based on the PR history, is that the
refactor's stated goal (clock-skew robustness) and its actual effect on
retry semantics were simply not connected during review, since the retry
path is only exercised in the relatively rare case of a transient upstream
failure and was not covered by the refactor's own test additions.

The dedupe-orders-events boundary bug is independent and unrelated in
origin: its deduplication logic, when it finds multiple rows sharing an
(order_id, event_type, event_timestamp) key, is intended to keep exactly one
row per key, arbitrarily choosing among duplicates using a row-ranking
window function. The boundary bug is in the ranking function's tie-breaking
logic: when two duplicate rows have identical values in every ranking column
used for tie-breaking (which is common for true exact duplicates, as opposed
to near-duplicates with minor differences), the ranking function's fallback
comparison, intended as a final deterministic tie-breaker, instead evaluates
to a comparison that excludes both rows from the "keep" set under a specific
combination of NULL-handling in one of the fallback columns, rather than
including exactly one. This NULL-handling edge case had simply never been
exercised in 14 months of production traffic before this incident's specific
duplicate pattern triggered it.

7. WHAT WENT WELL

- Once escalated, root cause investigation was thorough and reasonably fast,
  moving from initial detection (08:14) to confirmed root cause (14:30) in
  about six hours despite requiring two independent, previously-unrelated
  bugs to be understood together.
- The decision to pull affected tables from user-facing consumption at 09:20
  limited further spread of corrupted data to end users once the issue was
  confirmed, even though it came after the primary damage (executive reports
  already sent) had occurred.
- Recovery was performed by re-processing from the raw upstream event log
  rather than attempting to patch the corrupted intermediate tables, which
  the team considers the safer approach and validated successfully against
  known-good row counts before promoting to production. This approach was
  possible only because the raw upstream event log retains 90 days of
  history and was never itself at risk of corruption or deletion, a
  property the team explicitly credits as making this incident recoverable
  in a way that would not have been possible if the raw event log had a
  shorter retention window or had been affected by the same bugs.

8. WHAT WENT POORLY

- Detection depended entirely on a Merchandising analyst noticing implausible
  numbers, rather than any automated system; six-plus hours elapsed between
  the corrupted data being loaded (00:35) and detection (08:14), during
  which multiple scheduled reports and dashboards were generated and
  consumed.
- At least one executive-facing report was sent and acted upon before
  correction, requiring a follow-up correction from Finance, described in
  Section 2.
- The January 2026 refactor that introduced the truncate bug was not tested
  against the retry-after-partial-failure scenario, despite that scenario
  being the specific reason the truncate logic exists.
- The dedupe boundary bug represents 14 months of latent risk that no test
  or code review caught, though the postmortem authors note this specific
  bug is genuinely difficult to have caught via code review alone, since it
  required a NULL-handling edge case in a fallback tie-breaker that is not
  obviously wrong when reading the code in isolation.

8A. DETECTION GAPS, IN DETAIL

Beyond the anomaly-detection threshold issue described in Section 5, the
postmortem authors identified two further detection gaps worth recording
separately. First, dedupe-orders-events had no assertion or check comparing
its output row count against its input row count in any expected
relationship (for example, flagging if output is less than some minimum
fraction of input), which would have caught this incident's specific
17,653-row shortfall (Appendix A) directly at the dedup step itself, well
before the load step or any downstream consumption. Second, no dashboard or
report in the affected chain displayed any data-freshness or data-quality
indicator to end users like Deepa Krishnan; she was able to notice the
problem only because she happened to have context on what the trailing
7-day average looked like and found the new figure implausible by
comparison, a form of detection that depends entirely on individual analyst
familiarity rather than a systematic safeguard, and would not have caught a
smaller-magnitude or less-obviously-anomalous corruption.

9. CORRECTIVE ACTIONS

CA-1: Fix the batch_id generation bug so that retried attempts correctly
identify and truncate their failed predecessor's partial output. Owner: Yara
Solberg. Due: 2026-03-15. Status: Complete, deployed 2026-03-12. The fix
reverts to a deterministic batch_id derivation (based on job name and
scheduled run date rather than a per-attempt UUID) while preserving the
clock-skew robustness the January refactor intended, by deriving determinism
from the orchestrator's own run identifier rather than a worker-local
timestamp, which was the actual source of the clock-skew problem the
original refactor was trying to solve.

CA-2: Fix the dedupe boundary bug's NULL-handling tie-breaker so that exact
duplicate rows always retain exactly one copy. Owner: Yara Solberg. Due:
2026-03-15. Status: Complete, deployed 2026-03-12 (same deploy as CA-1). The
fix adds an explicit, unconditional final tie-breaker (row ingestion
timestamp, which is never NULL) after the existing fallback columns, so no
combination of NULL values in the existing fallback columns can result in
zero rows being kept.

CA-3: Add a retry-after-partial-failure test case to orders-events-ingest's
test suite, and audit other ingestion jobs using the same batch_id pattern
for the same class of bug. Owner: Connie Huang. Due: 2026-03-29. Status: In
progress; two other ingestion jobs identified as using a similar pattern,
audit ongoing. Both identified jobs (inventory-events-ingest, already known
to be affected per Section 3, and a third job, clickstream-events-ingest,
not otherwise affected by this incident) will receive the same CA-1 fix
pattern.

CA-4: Replace the fixed absolute-floor row-count anomaly detection with a
relative, trend-aware check comparing each partition's row count against a
trailing rolling average, flagging deviations beyond a configurable
threshold (proposed: 3%). Owner: Owen Baptiste. Due: 2026-04-12. Status: In
progress. The 3% threshold was chosen to be tighter than this incident's
6.2% impact while still leaving margin above normal day-to-day volume
variance, which Owen's preliminary analysis of the trailing 90 days of
partition row counts found to be under 1.5% in the overwhelming majority of
cases.

CA-5: Add a standing automated reconciliation check comparing downstream
warehouse row counts against the raw upstream event log on a daily basis,
rather than relying on manual investigation as performed at 08:41 in this
incident. Owner: Owen Baptiste. Due: 2026-04-26. Status: Not started.

CA-6: Establish a data-quality warning banner mechanism that can be applied
to affected dashboards and reports faster than the manual process used at
09:20 in this incident, ideally triggered automatically by CA-4's anomaly
detection rather than requiring manual action. Owner: Malik Ferreira. Due:
2026-05-10. Status: Not started.

CA-7: Add an input/output row-count assertion to dedupe-orders-events and
dedupe-inventory-events directly, per the first detection gap identified in
Section 8A, so that a shortfall like this incident's is caught at the dedup
step itself rather than only via downstream anomaly detection. Owner: Yara
Solberg. Due: 2026-04-05. Status: In progress.

13. APPENDIX D: GLOSSARY

Staging table: An intermediate table where a pipeline step writes raw or
lightly-processed output before subsequent steps (deduplication, loading)
consume it; orders_events_staging and inventory_events_staging in this
incident.

Batch_id: A column intended to uniquely identify all rows written by a
single job attempt, used both for lineage tracking and, prior to this
incident's root cause, for the truncate-before-retry logic described in
Section 3 and Section 6.

Dedup boundary bug: This postmortem's shorthand for the NULL-handling
tie-breaker defect described in Section 6, so named because it only
manifests for rows at the specific "boundary" created by a retry spanning
two batch_ids.

Anomaly detection floor: The fixed absolute row-count threshold described in
Section 5 and Section 8A, being replaced by a relative check under CA-4.

Reconciliation check: A comparison between two representations of the same
underlying data (for example, raw upstream event log vs. loaded warehouse
table) intended to catch silent data loss; the ad hoc version performed
manually by Owen Baptiste at 08:41 is being formalized as a standing check
under CA-5.

SOLENNE ROBOTICS, INC.
FY2025 ANNUAL REPORT
Fiscal Year Ended December 31, 2025

1. LETTER TO SHAREHOLDERS

Dear Shareholders,

Fiscal 2025 was a year of accelerating momentum for Solenne Robotics. Full-year
revenue grew 15% year-over-year to $494.6 million, up from
$428.7 million in FY2024, driven by continued strength in Industrial
Automation, a return to growth in Consumer Devices after a soft FY2024, and
the fastest growth we have ever posted in Robotics-as-a-Service, which is now
a meaningful contributor to both revenue and, increasingly, margin.

Each quarter built on the last. Q1 revenue was $111.6 million. Q2
revenue grew to $120.5 million. Q3 revenue came in at $122.3 million,
up 8% sequentially, capping our strongest first three quarters as a public
company. Q4 capped the year with $141.7 million, our largest quarter
ever, powered by record Industrial Automation bookings converting to revenue
and a strong seasonal quarter in Consumer Devices.

Profitability improved alongside growth. GAAP net income for the year was
$33.4 million, up 9% from $30.6 million in FY2024, and
non-GAAP net income, which excludes stock-based compensation and
acquisition-related intangible amortization, was $49.2 million.
Operating cash flow was $71.4 million for the year, and we ended FY2025
with $187.3 million in cash and equivalents, up from $166.7 million
at the start of the year, even after continuing our share repurchase
program.

Robotics-as-a-Service deserves particular mention. What began three years ago
as a small pilot program with a handful of logistics customers has grown into
a $65.3 million annual revenue segment, and more importantly, RaaS
customer retention now exceeds 92% on a trailing-twelve-month basis,
validating the subscription-style model we believe will define the next
phase of industrial robotics adoption. We expect RaaS to grow faster than the
company average in FY2026 and to continue improving overall gross margin as
it scales.

We are not without challenges. Consumer Devices had a difficult FY2024 and,
while it returned to growth in FY2025, competitive pricing pressure in that
segment remains real, and we discuss it candidly in the Risk Factors section
of this report. Supply chain costs for certain semiconductor components used
across all three segments remain elevated relative to pre-2023 levels, though
they have stabilized over the past three quarters.

Looking ahead to FY2026, we are targeting continued double-digit revenue
growth, further non-GAAP margin expansion, and continued investment in our
RaaS platform, which we believe is the single largest driver of long-term
shareholder value in our business. Thank you for your continued confidence
in Solenne Robotics.

Sincerely,
Marisol Vega
Chief Executive Officer

2. BUSINESS OVERVIEW

Solenne Robotics designs, manufactures, and increasingly subscribes out
robotic systems across three segments: Industrial Automation, Consumer
Devices, and Robotics-as-a-Service. The company was founded in 2014 and has
been publicly traded since 2021. Solenne's Industrial Automation segment
sells robotic arms, mobile warehouse robots, and associated software to
manufacturing and logistics customers, sold primarily through direct
enterprise sales and a network of systems-integrator partners. The Consumer
Devices segment sells home robotics products, primarily robotic vacuums and
a smaller line of home security robots, through both direct-to-consumer
channels and major retail partners. Robotics-as-a-Service, the company's
newest and fastest-growing segment, offers Industrial Automation hardware
under a subscription model, where customers pay a monthly fee covering
hardware, maintenance, and software updates rather than purchasing equipment
outright, aimed at customers who want robotics deployment without a large
upfront capital expenditure.

Solenne operates two manufacturing facilities, in Ohio and in Malaysia, and
maintains regional sales and support offices in the United States, Germany,
Japan, and Singapore. As of December 31, 2025, the company employed
approximately 3,400 people worldwide, up from approximately 3,050 at the end
of FY2024. The company's fiscal year ends December 31 and it reports results
in four fiscal quarters aligned to the calendar year.

The Ohio facility, located outside Columbus, is Solenne's primary
manufacturing site for Industrial Automation hardware and the site of the
line upgrade referenced in Section 3, completed in Q2 2025. The Malaysia
facility, located near Penang, primarily manufactures Consumer Devices
products and has been in operation since 2019. Both facilities are owned
rather than leased. RaaS hardware is manufactured on the same Industrial
Automation lines at the Ohio facility, since RaaS units are functionally
identical Industrial Automation hardware sold under a different commercial
model, as described in Section 5.

3. SEGMENT OVERVIEW: INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION

Industrial Automation remains Solenne's largest and most established
segment, generating $250.8 million in FY2025 revenue, up 14%
year-over-year from $220.0 million in FY2024. The segment serves
manufacturing, warehousing, and logistics customers, with particular
strength among mid-size manufacturers who have historically been underserved
by the largest industrial robotics vendors. Industrial Automation's growth in
FY2025 was driven primarily by a broadening of the customer base rather than
growth in average deal size; the segment added 47 net new enterprise
customers during the year, compared to 31 in FY2024. Gross margin in
Industrial Automation was approximately 46% for FY2025, roughly flat with
FY2024, as component cost increases were offset by manufacturing efficiencies
at the Ohio facility following a line upgrade completed in Q2 2025.

4. SEGMENT OVERVIEW: CONSUMER DEVICES

Consumer Devices generated $178.5 million in FY2025 revenue, up
6% from $168.4 million in FY2024, a return to growth after
FY2024's decline from FY2023 levels. The segment's recovery was driven by the
Q3 2025 launch of the Solenne Home 3, the company's third-generation robotic
vacuum, which received favorable reviews and outperformed the company's
internal unit sales forecast in its first two quarters on the market.
Consumer Devices gross margin was approximately 31% for FY2025, down from 34%
in FY2024, reflecting continued competitive pricing pressure in the robotic
vacuum category, which management discusses further in the Risk Factors
section below. The segment remains the most seasonal of Solenne's three
segments, with Q4 typically representing 35-40% of the segment's annual
revenue due to holiday retail demand; in FY2025, Q4 Consumer Devices revenue
of $55.6 million represented 31% of the
segment's full-year total, consistent with that pattern.

5. SEGMENT OVERVIEW: ROBOTICS-AS-A-SERVICE

Robotics-as-a-Service generated $65.3 million in FY2025 revenue, up
62% from $40.3 million in FY2024, making it by far
Solenne's fastest-growing segment, albeit from a smaller base. RaaS revenue
is recognized ratably over the subscription term, generally 24 or 36 months,
rather than upfront as with Industrial Automation hardware sales, which
management believes provides more predictable, recurring revenue over time
even though it defers revenue recognition relative to an equivalent outright
hardware sale. Trailing-twelve-month customer retention for RaaS was 92%, up
from 87% a year earlier. RaaS gross margin was approximately 38% for FY2025,
below Industrial Automation's margin due to the segment's continued
investment in field service capacity to support subscription customers, but
management expects RaaS margin to expand over time as the subscriber base
scales against a largely fixed field-service cost base.

11. RISK FACTORS

The following risk factors could materially affect Solenne's business,
financial condition, and results of operations. This list is not exhaustive.

R1. Customer concentration. Our largest customer, a national retail chain we
refer to as "Customer A" under a confidentiality provision in our supply
agreement, represented approximately 11% of FY2025 total revenue, down from
14% in FY2024 as our customer base has broadened. The loss of Customer A, or
a significant reduction in its orders, would have a material adverse effect
on our Consumer Devices segment in particular, since substantially all of
Customer A's purchases are Consumer Devices products sold through its retail
locations and e-commerce site rather than Industrial Automation or RaaS
offerings. Our next four largest customers combined represented an
additional approximately 18% of FY2025 revenue, spread across all three
segments, and no other single customer represented more than 5% of total
revenue.

R2. Supply chain and component costs. We depend on a limited number of
suppliers for certain semiconductor components used across all three
segments. Component costs increased significantly beginning in 2022 and,
while they have stabilized over the past three quarters, remain elevated
relative to pre-2023 levels, and any renewed supply disruption could
increase costs or delay shipments.

R3. Competitive pricing pressure in Consumer Devices. The robotic vacuum
category in which our Consumer Devices segment competes has seen intensified
price competition from both established and new entrants, which contributed
to gross margin compression from 34% to 31% year over year as described in
Section 4, and could continue to pressure margins in this segment.

R4. Robotics-as-a-Service concentration and churn risk. While RaaS retention
improved to 92% in FY2025, the segment remains younger and less proven than
Industrial Automation, and a significant increase in customer churn, or a
slowdown in new subscriber additions, would disproportionately affect our
growth rate given RaaS's outsized contribution to overall growth, as
described in Section 5. Additionally, because RaaS revenue is recognized
ratably over 24- or 36-month subscription terms, a slowdown in new bookings
would not be immediately visible in reported revenue, potentially masking a
deceleration for several quarters before it becomes apparent in the
financial statements, which investors should bear in mind when evaluating
quarter-to-quarter RaaS revenue trends such as those described in Section
10.

R5. International operations and tariffs. We manufacture at facilities in
the United States and Malaysia and sell into international markets including
Germany, Japan, and Singapore. Changes in trade policy, tariffs, or currency
exchange rates could increase our costs or reduce the competitiveness of our
pricing in affected markets.

R6. Cybersecurity. Our RaaS platform and connected devices create a larger
attack surface than a traditional hardware-only business. A significant
security incident affecting customer data or connected robotic systems in
the field could damage customer trust and result in contractual liability.
We maintain a dedicated security engineering team and undergo an annual
third-party penetration test of the RaaS platform, most recently completed
in Q4 2025 with no critical findings, but no security program can guarantee
prevention of all incidents, and our increasing reliance on connected
systems as RaaS scales, described in Section 5, increases our exposure over
time.

R7. Key personnel. Our success depends on our executive team and key
engineering personnel, particularly in robotics software and computer
vision. Competition for this talent is intense, and the loss of key
personnel could disrupt our product roadmap.

R8. Regulatory and safety. Industrial robotics are subject to workplace
safety regulations that vary by jurisdiction, and evolving regulation of
autonomous systems could increase compliance costs or restrict certain use
cases, particularly for future RaaS deployments involving greater autonomy.
The European Union and several U.S. states have proposed or enacted
additional autonomous-systems safety and disclosure requirements over the
past two years, and while none of these have materially affected our current
product lines, we cannot predict whether future regulation in these or other
jurisdictions will require product design changes or additional compliance
costs, particularly as our RaaS platform incorporates greater autonomy over
time as described in Section 5.

R9. Intellectual property litigation. The robotics industry has seen
increased patent litigation activity in recent years. We are currently
party to one immaterial patent dispute related to a Consumer Devices
component design, which we do not believe will have a material impact, but
we may face additional claims in the future, particularly as our RaaS
platform's software footprint grows and as we expand into new international
markets with different intellectual property regimes, described in Section
11A and Appendix E.

R10. Macroeconomic conditions. A slowdown in industrial capital spending or
consumer discretionary spending could reduce demand across our segments,
particularly Industrial Automation, where purchases represent a significant
capital expenditure for our customers.

11A. COMPETITIVE LANDSCAPE

In Industrial Automation, Solenne competes primarily against two larger,
diversified industrial automation vendors as well as a growing number of
venture-backed startups focused on mobile warehouse robotics specifically.
Management believes Solenne's mid-market focus and systems-integrator
partner network, described in Section 3, differentiate it from the larger
incumbents, who have historically focused on the largest enterprise
accounts. In Consumer Devices, the competitive set is broader and includes
both established consumer electronics companies and lower-cost entrants,
particularly from Asia-based manufacturers, which management believes is the
primary driver of the pricing pressure described in Risk Factor R3. In
Robotics-as-a-Service, Solenne's competition today is more often a
customer's own build-versus-subscribe decision than a direct competitor
offering an equivalent subscription model, though management expects
direct RaaS competition to increase as the subscription model proves out
industry-wide.

12. OUTLOOK FOR FY2026

Management expects FY2026 total revenue growth in the low-to-mid teens
percentage range, with Robotics-as-a-Service again expected to grow
significantly faster than the company average, continuing the trend
described in Section 5. Industrial Automation is expected to grow at a
similar rate to FY2025, while Consumer Devices growth is expected to
moderate from FY2025's pace as the initial launch benefit from the Solenne
Home 3 normalizes. Management expects continued non-GAAP gross margin
expansion driven by RaaS scale efficiencies and the Ohio facility upgrade
completed in Q2 2025, and plans to continue the existing share repurchase
program at a similar pace to FY2025.

SECURITY AUDIT REPORT
Q2 2026 — Internal Platform Services

Prepared by: Internal Security Assessment Team
Lead Auditor: Farrah Nazari
Contributing Auditors: Dmitri Volkov, Ada Osei-Mensah
Audit Period: 2026-04-01 through 2026-05-15
Report Date: 2026-05-22
Distribution: Engineering Leadership, Security Steering Committee

1. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

This report presents findings from the Q2 2026 internal security audit of
four platform services: the API Gateway, the Internal Admin Console, the
Notification Service, and the Batch Processing Cluster. The audit identified
12 findings, numbered F-01 through F-12, ranging in severity from Low to
Critical. As of this report's publication date, 9 findings are fully
remediated, 2 are in progress with committed remediation dates, and 1
(F-07) has had its severity formally downgraded from Critical to Medium
following verification of compensating controls, as detailed in Section 6.
Readers relying only on the Findings Summary Table in Section 3 should be
aware that F-07's table entry reflects the severity at time of discovery,
not its current, downgraded severity; Section 6 and Section 7 contain the
current status.

2. SCOPE AND METHODOLOGY

The audit covered four internal platform services selected based on a
risk-ranking exercise conducted with Engineering Leadership in March 2026:
API Gateway (public-facing request routing and authentication), Internal
Admin Console (internal tooling for customer support and operations staff),
Notification Service (email/SMS/push notification delivery), and Batch
Processing Cluster (scheduled and ad hoc batch job execution, including the
data pipeline infrastructure operated by the Data Platform team). The audit
combined automated static analysis (using the company's standard SAST
tooling), manual code review of authentication and authorization logic
specifically, dependency vulnerability scanning, and a limited black-box
penetration test of the API Gateway and Internal Admin Console conducted by
Dmitri Volkov between 2026-04-20 and 2026-04-24. The audit did not cover
infrastructure-level concerns (network segmentation, cloud IAM
configuration), which are covered under a separate, parallel infrastructure
audit not included in this report.

3A. SEVERITY DEFINITIONS

Critical: Exploitable with low effort, resulting in significant data
exposure, full account compromise, or arbitrary code execution with broad
credential access. Requires remediation within 5 business days of
confirmation per the Security Steering Committee's standing SLA.

High: Exploitable with moderate effort or requiring specific conditions,
resulting in meaningful data exposure or privilege escalation within a
bounded scope. Remediation SLA: 15 business days.

Medium: Exploitable but with limited practical impact, requiring unusual
conditions, or providing only information-disclosure value to an attacker
without direct further compromise. Remediation SLA: 30 business days.

Low: Minimal practical exploitability or impact; typically hardening or
defense-in-depth items rather than directly exploitable vulnerabilities.
Remediation SLA: 60 business days.

4. DETAILED FINDINGS

F-01 (API Gateway, High): The gateway's rate-limiting bypass. The API
Gateway's authentication middleware validated JWT signatures correctly but
did not verify the token's audience (aud) claim, allowing a token issued for
one internal service to be replayed against a different service's endpoints
through the gateway. Evidence: Dmitri Volkov demonstrated this during the
penetration test by replaying a token issued for the Notification Service
against an Internal Admin Console endpoint reachable through the shared
gateway, successfully authenticating. CVSS 7.4 (High). Remediation: audience
claim validation added to the gateway's JWT middleware, deployed 2026-04-29.
Remediation owner: Grace Liu (API Gateway team). Retested and confirmed
fixed 2026-05-02.

F-02 (API Gateway, Medium): Verbose error messages on malformed requests
exposed internal service hostnames in stack traces returned to external
clients. CVSS 5.3 (Medium). Remediation: generic error responses for
external clients, detailed errors retained in internal logs only, deployed
2026-05-06. Remediation owner: Grace Liu. Retested and confirmed fixed
2026-05-08.

F-03 (Internal Admin Console, High): Insufficient authorization checks on a
customer-record bulk-export endpoint allowed any authenticated support
agent, regardless of their assigned permission tier, to export records for
customers outside their assigned account segment. CVSS 7.1 (High).
Remediation: permission-tier check added to the export endpoint, deployed
2026-05-01. Remediation owner: Tobias Reinholt (Admin Console team).
Retested and confirmed fixed 2026-05-05.

F-04 (Internal Admin Console, Critical): A debug endpoint,
/internal/_debug/impersonate, left enabled in the production deployment
configuration (intended for staging/development only), allowed any
authenticated Admin Console user to generate a valid session token for any
customer account without additional verification. This is the most severe
finding in this audit. CVSS 9.1 (Critical). Evidence: Dmitri Volkov
confirmed the endpoint was reachable in production during the penetration
test on 2026-04-21 and immediately escalated out-of-band rather than waiting
for the standard report, given the severity. The endpoint had originally
been added in 2023 for a legitimate staging-environment debugging need and
was gated behind an environment-variable check intended to disable it
outside staging; an infrastructure migration in late 2025 changed how that
environment variable was populated in production, and the check silently
evaluated to "staging-like" in production as an unintended side effect,
which is why this had been live in production for approximately five
months before discovery despite not being an intentional production
feature at any point. Remediation: endpoint disabled
in production configuration within 4 hours of escalation (2026-04-21,
same day), and the endpoint's code path was subsequently removed entirely
from the production build target on 2026-04-25 to prevent recurrence via
future configuration drift, rather than relying on the environment-variable
gate a second time. Remediation owner: Tobias Reinholt. Retested
and confirmed fixed 2026-04-25. A review of access logs for the 90 days
prior to discovery found no evidence the endpoint had been used maliciously,
though logging for this specific endpoint was found to be incomplete (see
F-10), limiting confidence in this conclusion; see Section 8 for the risk
acceptance discussion.

F-05 (Notification Service, Medium): SMS notification templates included
user-supplied free-text fields without sufficient sanitization, creating a
theoretical SMS header injection risk with certain carrier gateways. CVSS
5.8 (Medium). Remediation: input sanitization added for all free-text
template fields, deployed 2026-05-03. Remediation owner: Wale Adeyemi
(Notification Service team). Retested and confirmed fixed 2026-05-07.

F-06 (Notification Service, Low): Notification delivery logs retained
full message content, including notification bodies that could contain
sensitive account information, for 180 days, longer than the 30-day
retention the team believed was configured. CVSS 3.1 (Low). Remediation:
retention policy corrected to 30 days, and a one-time purge of logs older
than 30 days was performed. Remediation owner: Wale Adeyemi. Retested and
confirmed fixed 2026-05-09.

F-07 (Batch Processing Cluster, initially rated Critical): A batch job
execution framework used across the Batch Processing Cluster, including
the Data Platform team's pipeline infrastructure, allowed a submitted job's
configuration to specify an arbitrary container image reference rather than
being restricted to an approved image registry and allowlist. In principle,
this would allow a user with job-submission access to run arbitrary code
with the batch cluster's service credentials, which have broad read access
to several production data stores. Initially rated CVSS 9.0 (Critical)
based on this theoretical impact. See Section 6 for the full detail on why
this was downgraded to Medium after further investigation.

F-08 (Batch Processing Cluster, High): Batch job logs, which can contain
job configuration including embedded credentials for jobs that were
configured before secrets-management guidance was introduced in 2025,
are readable by any authenticated user with Batch Processing Cluster access
generally, not scoped to the job's owning team. CVSS 6.9 (High).
Remediation status: In progress; owner Priyanka Deshmukh (Data Platform),
target 2026-06-05. Interim mitigation: a scan of existing job logs for
embedded credential patterns was completed 2026-05-12, identifying 6 legacy
jobs with embedded credentials, all of which have had those credentials
rotated as of 2026-05-14 pending the full log-access-scoping fix.

F-09 (API Gateway, Low): The gateway's health-check endpoint exposed the
gateway's software version string, a minor information-disclosure issue.
CVSS 2.4 (Low). Remediation: version string removed from the public
health-check response, deployed 2026-05-10. Remediation owner: Grace Liu.
Retested and confirmed fixed 2026-05-11.

F-10 (Internal Admin Console, Medium): Access logging for several
sensitive Admin Console endpoints, including the debug endpoint involved in
F-04, was found to be incomplete: requests were logged with a timestamp and
endpoint path but not the requesting user's identity, for endpoints added
before a 2024 logging-standards update that was never retroactively applied
to older endpoints. CVSS 5.0 (Medium). Remediation: identity logging added
to all Admin Console endpoints, deployed 2026-05-13. Remediation owner:
Tobias Reinholt. Retested and confirmed fixed 2026-05-15.

F-11 (Notification Service, Low): The service's dependency scan identified
one outdated third-party library with a published CVE (CVE-2025-41102,
affecting an XML-parsing library used only in an unused legacy code path).
CVSS 3.8 (Low), reduced from the library's own published base score because
the vulnerable code path is not reachable in this service's configuration.
Remediation: library upgraded and the unused legacy code path removed,
deployed 2026-05-04. Remediation owner: Wale Adeyemi. Retested and
confirmed fixed 2026-05-06.

F-12 (Batch Processing Cluster, Medium): The batch cluster's job scheduling
API does not rate-limit job submissions per user, allowing a single user
(malicious or, more likely given the cluster's usage pattern, a
misconfigured recurring job) to exhaust cluster capacity. CVSS 4.9 (Medium).
Remediation status: In progress; owner Priyanka Deshmukh, target 2026-06-12.

4A. SYSTEMIC THEMES ACROSS FINDINGS

Looking across the twelve findings, the audit team identified three
recurring systemic themes that individual finding-level remediation does
not, by itself, address, and which are called out here for the Security
Steering Committee's attention.

Theme 1: Configuration drift between environments. Both F-04 (the debug
endpoint left reachable in production) and, in a milder form, F-07 (where
the auditors' initial severity assessment was based on outdated
documentation of the batch cluster's credential model) stem from the same
underlying pattern: the intended difference between staging and production
configuration, or between documented and actual system behavior, drifted
over time without any process catching the drift. The F-04 remediation
(removing the code path from the production build entirely rather than
re-gating it) is the right instinct generalized: where a capability must
never exist in production, build-level exclusion is more robust than
runtime configuration, because it cannot drift. The audit team recommends
Engineering Leadership sponsor an inventory of other environment-gated
capabilities across the four audited services, since F-04 is unlikely to be
the only such gate whose behavior depends on infrastructure details that
can change underneath it.

Theme 2: Logging completeness as a security control. F-10's incomplete
identity logging directly degraded the incident-response value of the F-04
investigation, turning what should have been a definitive "was this
exploited?" answer into a qualified one that the Steering Committee
ultimately had to accept as an unresolvable historical gap (Section 8).
Logging is often treated as an operational concern rather than a security
control; this audit demonstrates concretely that logging gaps convert
directly into irreducible uncertainty during incident response. The 2024
logging-standards update referenced in F-10 was correct in content but had
no mechanism for retroactive application to older endpoints, which is a
process gap, not a technical one.

Theme 3: Uneven security maturity correlated with surface age. The two
services with the most active recent development (API Gateway and
Notification Service) produced findings that were predominantly
lower-severity hardening items, while the two services carrying more legacy
surface (Internal Admin Console, with endpoints predating the 2024 logging
standards, and Batch Processing Cluster, with jobs predating the 2025
secrets-management guidance) produced the audit's most serious findings.
This is consistent with the broader industry pattern that security posture
correlates more strongly with how recently a surface has been actively
maintained than with how business-critical it is, and argues for future
audit cycles explicitly weighting older, quieter surfaces rather than
allocating attention proportional to development activity.

5. CVSS METHODOLOGY NOTE

All CVSS scores in this report use CVSS v3.1 base scores, computed by the
auditor who identified the finding and cross-checked by at least one other
auditor before inclusion in this report. Where a finding's practical impact
differs materially from what the base score alone would suggest (as with
F-07, described in Section 6), this report notes that explicitly rather
than relying on the base score in isolation to communicate risk.

6. RISK ACCEPTANCE AND SEVERITY REVISION: F-07

F-07 was initially rated Critical based on the theoretical impact of
arbitrary code execution with the batch cluster's broad service credentials.
During remediation planning, Priyanka Deshmukh's team investigated the
batch cluster's actual credential scoping more closely and found that,
contrary to the auditors' initial understanding, the batch cluster's service
credentials had already been scoped down in a separate, unrelated project
completed in February 2026 (prior to this audit), such that any given job
only receives credentials for the specific data stores its job
configuration declares it needs, rather than the broad standing access the
auditors initially assumed based on older documentation. This materially
reduces F-07's actual exploitability: an attacker exploiting the arbitrary-
image vulnerability could still run arbitrary code, but that code would only
inherit the credentials of whatever data stores the specific job declared,
which for the large majority of jobs is narrowly scoped. Farrah Nazari's
team verified this compensating control directly by inspecting the
credential-scoping implementation and confirming, via a controlled test job,
that a job's runtime credentials were indeed limited to its declared scope
rather than the cluster-wide service identity. Based on this verification,
F-07's severity was formally downgraded from Critical to Medium, approved by
Farrah Nazari on 2026-05-18. The underlying arbitrary-image vulnerability is
still being fixed on the same timeline as F-08 and F-12 (target 2026-06-05,
now tracked as F-08's owner Priyanka Deshmukh also owns this fix), since
restricting job images to an approved registry remains good practice
regardless of the reduced severity; the finding's Status in Section 3 is
marked "Remediated" reflecting that the underlying arbitrary-image issue
itself has in fact already been fixed, ahead of F-08 and F-12, as of
2026-05-16, with the Section 6 narrative here documenting the severity
history for completeness rather than describing an open issue.

6A. STEERING COMMITTEE REVIEW OF THE F-07 DOWNGRADE

Because a Critical-to-Medium downgrade is unusual and carries an obvious
incentive risk (teams might prefer downgrades to remediation), the Security
Steering Committee reviewed the F-07 revision at its 2026-05-20 meeting as
a standing-agenda item rather than accepting it on the audit team's
authority alone. The Committee examined three questions. First, was the
compensating control independently verified rather than taken on the owning
team's word? Yes: Farrah Nazari's team ran its own controlled test job and
inspected the credential-scoping implementation directly, as described in
Section 6, rather than relying on the Data Platform team's description.
Second, does the compensating control cover the entire exposed surface?
Substantially but not perfectly: a minority of batch jobs legitimately
declare broad data-store access and would still confer wide credentials to
an attacker exploiting the arbitrary-image path, which is why the finding
was downgraded to Medium rather than Low, and why the underlying image-
allowlist fix proceeded regardless of the downgrade. Third, was the
downgrade decision independent of remediation convenience? Yes, in the
Committee's judgment, and notably the point is moot in practice: the
underlying fix was completed on 2026-05-16, two days before the downgrade
was even formally approved, so the revision changed the finding's
historical classification and its SLA accounting, not whether or when it
was fixed. The Committee ratified the downgrade unanimously and asked that
this reasoning be recorded in the published report, which this section
does.

7. FINDINGS BY SERVICE, WITH CURRENT SEVERITY

Remediated). All API Gateway findings are fully remediated as of this
report.

Internal Admin Console: F-03 (High, Remediated), F-04 (Critical,
Remediated), F-10 (Medium, Remediated). All Internal Admin Console findings
are fully remediated as of this report.

Notification Service: F-05 (Medium, Remediated), F-06 (Low, Remediated),
F-11 (Low, Remediated). All Notification Service findings are fully
remediated as of this report.

Batch Processing Cluster: F-07 (current severity Medium per Section 6,

8. RISK ACCEPTANCE DISCUSSION

Beyond F-07's severity revision, the Security Steering Committee formally
accepted one residual risk without further remediation: the incomplete
historical access logging for the F-04 debug endpoint prior to its removal
(referenced in the F-04 finding text) cannot be retroactively corrected,
since the necessary identity information was simply never recorded for
requests made before the F-10 fix deployed. The Committee accepted this as
an unresolvable historical gap rather than an open finding requiring
further action, on 2026-05-19, on the basis that the endpoint itself has
been removed entirely (F-04) and logging is now complete going forward
(F-10).

8A. COMPLIANCE MAPPING

For the benefit of the compliance team's annual control-attestation work,
the findings in this report map to the company's internal control framework
as follows. F-01, F-03, and F-04 map to control family AC (Access Control),
specifically AC-3 (authorization enforcement) and AC-6 (least privilege);
the F-04 finding constitutes a control failure for the audit period against
AC-3 that will need to be disclosed in the annual attestation, with the
remediation evidence from Section 4 and Appendix A attached. F-06 maps to
control family DR (Data Retention), and its 180-day-versus-30-day retention
discrepancy similarly constitutes a reportable control deviation for the
audit period, though at Low severity. F-08 and the F-04 logging gap (F-10)
map to control family AU (Audit and Accountability), AU-2 and AU-3. F-07,
after its Section 6 downgrade, maps to control family CM (Configuration
Management), CM-7 (least functionality), and is reportable as a deviation
that was remediated within the audit period. The remaining findings (F-02,
F-05, F-09, F-11, F-12) are hardening improvements that do not rise to the
level of control deviations under the framework's definitions and are
mapped for reference only.

8B. WHAT THE AUDIT DID NOT FIND

The audit team considers it worth recording, for calibration of future
audit cycles, several categories where findings were actively looked for
and not found. No SQL injection or other injection-class vulnerabilities
were found in any of the four services' request-handling paths; all four
services consistently used parameterized queries and the standard internal
data-access libraries. No secrets were found committed to any of the four
services' source repositories in a scan of full repository history,
reflecting well on the 2025 secrets-management rollout, F-08's legacy job
configurations notwithstanding (those live in the batch cluster's job
configuration store, not in source control). Session management in both
user-facing surfaces (API Gateway session tokens, Admin Console sessions)
was found to follow current internal standards, including rotation on
privilege change and server-side revocation, with the obvious and glaring
exception of the F-04 impersonation endpoint, which bypassed rather than
subverted the session framework. And the dependency scan across all four
services found only the single actionable CVE recorded as F-11, with the
remaining flagged dependencies either already patched or not reachable in
any configured code path.

14. APPENDIX E: RECOMMENDATIONS FOR THE NEXT AUDIT CYCLE

Based on this cycle's findings and the scope gaps identified during the
work, the audit team recommends the following for the Q4 2026 cycle. First,
extend black-box penetration testing to the Notification Service and Batch
Processing Cluster, which were covered only by code review and scanning
this cycle, per the scope constraint recorded in Appendix B; the density of
serious findings in the Batch Processing Cluster from code review alone
(F-07, F-08, F-12) suggests a penetration test of that surface is likely to
be productive. Second, per Theme 1 in Section 4A, include an explicit
environment-parity review comparing staging and production configuration
for all audited services, using the F-04 root cause as the motivating case
study. Third, per Theme 3, weight the service-selection risk-ranking
exercise toward services with the longest time since last major
refactoring rather than purely by data sensitivity, since this cycle's
worst findings clustered in the least-recently-maintained surfaces. Fourth,
re-verify F-08 and F-12 remediation early in the next cycle if their
committed dates (2026-06-05 and 2026-06-12 respectively) have passed by
then, and confirm the F-07 image-allowlist fix has not regressed, given
that batch job configuration is user-editable surface and allowlist
enforcement is exactly the kind of control that can silently loosen over
time.

15. APPENDIX F: AUDIT EFFORT AND PROCESS NOTES

The audit consumed approximately 31 auditor-days across the three-person
team over the six-and-a-half-week audit period: roughly 12 days from Farrah
Nazari (methodology, manual authorization-logic review of the Admin Console
and Batch Processing Cluster, findings review, and the F-07 compensating-
control verification described in Section 6), 11 days from Dmitri Volkov
(penetration test, API Gateway review, and CVSS cross-checking), and 8 days
from Ada Osei-Mensah (Notification Service review, dependency scanning
across all four services, and retest verification including the F-07
retest recorded in Appendix A). Two process observations are recorded for
future cycles. The out-of-band escalation path used for F-04 worked
exactly as intended: the finding went from discovery to production
mitigation in under 4 hours because Dmitri escalated immediately rather
than batching it into the report, and the team recommends the escalation
threshold (any finding the discovering auditor believes is Critical)
remain unchanged. Conversely, the F-07 experience suggests the team should
verify current-state system behavior with the owning team before assigning
a severity to architecture-dependent findings, rather than relying on
documentation: the initial Critical rating was correct given what the
auditors knew, but a fifteen-minute conversation with the Data Platform
team at discovery time would have surfaced the February 2026 credential-
scoping work immediately and avoided a week of the finding being tracked
at the wrong severity, with the attendant escalation overhead that a
Critical rating triggers under the Section 3A SLA.

16. APPENDIX G: GLOSSARY

Compensating control: An existing mechanism, other than fixing the
vulnerable code itself, that materially reduces a finding's exploitability
or impact; the batch cluster's per-job credential scoping verified in
Section 6 is the canonical example in this report.

Finding: A discrete, tracked security issue identified during the audit,
assigned an F-number, a severity, an owner, and a remediation status.

Out-of-band escalation: Reporting a finding to the affected team
immediately upon discovery rather than holding it for the consolidated
report, used for F-04 per the process described in Appendix F.

Retest: Independent verification, by an auditor other than the remediating
engineer, that a deployed fix actually closes the finding, per the dates
recorded in Appendix A.

SAST: Static application security testing; automated analysis of source
code for vulnerability patterns, one of the four methodology components
listed in Section 2.

Scope gap: A surface or technique deliberately excluded from the current
audit cycle and recorded for future consideration, as distinct from a
finding; the lack of penetration testing for the Notification Service and
Batch Processing Cluster (Appendix B) is this report's primary example.
